Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis: The terms "complementary medicine" or "alternative medicine" refer to a broad range of healthcare practices that are not part of the conventional medical system of a country and are not fully integrated into it. They are often used interchangeably with "traditional medicine" in some countries. Herbal medicine: Herbal medicine encompasses a variety of practices, including herbal remedies, herbal preparations, and processed herbal products, which contain active ingredients derived from plant parts or other plant materials, or combinations thereof. Impact: The WHO's Global Strategy on Traditional Medicine 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).

translations.source: Isu lihlose ukusekela Amazwe Angamalungu ekuthuthukiseni izinqubomgomo ezisebenzayo kanye nokusebenzisa izinhlelo zokusebenza ezizoqinisa indima edlalwa imithi yendabuko ekugcineni imiphakathi iphilile. Ukubhekana nezinselele, ukusabela ezidingweni ezihlonzwe Amazwe Angamalungu kanye nokwakhela phezu komsebenzi owenziwe ngaphansi kwesu lemithi yendabuko yeWHO: 2002-2005, isu elibuyekeziwe lesikhathi sika-2014-2023 linikezela ukunaka okungaphezulu kunokwandulela walo ekubekeni phambili izinsiza zezempilo nezinhlelo. , okuhlanganisa imikhiqizo yemithi yesintu nehambisanayo, izinqubo kanye nodokotela. Izinjongo zamasu yilezi: Ukwakha isisekelo solwazi sokuphatha okusebenzayo kweT&CM ngokusebenzisa izinqubomgomo zikazwelonke ezifanele; Ukuqinisa ukuqinisekiswa kwekhwalithi, ukuphepha, ukusetshenziswa okufanele nokusebenza kahle kweT&CM ngokulawula imikhiqizo, izinqubo kanye nabasebenzi;
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners;
translations.hypothesis: The initiative aims to support Member States in developing effective policies and implementing programs that strengthen the role of traditional medicine in promoting healthy communities. Addressing challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States, and building upon the work done under the WHO's Traditional Medicine Strategy 2002-2005, this revised strategy for 2014-2023 provides a more focused approach to strengthening health systems and programs, including traditional medicine products and practices, regulations, and practitioners. The objectives of the strategy are: to establish a knowledge base for the effective governance of Traditional and Complementary Medicine (T&CM) through appropriate national policies; to strengthen the quality assurance, safety, appropriate use, and effectiveness of T&CM through the regulation of products, practices, and practitioners;

translations.source: Ukukhuthaza ukuhlinzekwa kwezempilo emhlabeni wonke ngokuhlanganisa izinsiza zeT&CM ekulethweni kwezinsizakalo zokunakekelwa kwezempilo kanye nokuzinakekela. Impendulo yeWHO: Umbiko wembulunga yonke weWHO ngemithi yendabuko nehambisanayo 2019 wathuthukiswa ukuze kubhekwane negebe kudatha ethembekile, ethembekile nesemthethweni evela emazweni Angamalungu endaweni yeT&CM. Lo mbiko ubuyekeza inqubekelaphambili yomhlaba kuT&CM kule minyaka engamashumi amabili edlule futhi usekelwe emnikelweni ovela emazweni Angamalungu eWHO ayi-179. Ihlinzeka ngolwazi olubalulekile kubenzi bezinqubomgomo, ochwepheshe bezempilo kanye nomphakathi ukuze usebenzise imali engaba khona yeT&CM empilweni nasenhlalakahleni.
translations.reference: To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting global health by integrating Traditional and Complementary Medicine (T&CM) resources into the delivery of health services and self-care. WHO's response: The WHO's global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible, and official data from member states in the field of T&CM. This report reviews the global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO member states. It provides valuable information for policymakers, health professionals, and the public to effectively utilize available resources for T&CM in health and well-being.

translations.source: Ukufakelwa kabusha Uhlolojikelele: Ukufakelwa kabusha kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu nezitho zomzimba Ukufakelwa kabusha kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu noma izitho kusindisa izimpilo eziningi futhi kubuyisela imisebenzi ebalulekile lapho kungekho ezinye izindlela ezisebenzayo ezikhona. Eminyakeni engu-50, ukufakelwa kabusha sekuyinto ephumelelayo emhlabeni wonke. Kodwa ke, kunomehluko omkhulu phakathi kwamazwe ekufinyeleleni ukufakelwa okulungile kanye nezinga lokuphepha, ikhwalithi, ukusebenza kahle kokunikela kanye nokufakelwa kwamangqamuzana omuntu, izicubu nezitho zomzimba.
translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs.
translations.hypothesis: Organ Transplantation: The transplantation of human organs, tissues, and body parts. Transplantation of organs, tissues, or cells saves many lives and restores important functions when no other effective treatments are available. For 50 years, transplantation has been a successful practice worldwide. However, there is a significant disparity between countries in terms of access to quality transplantation, and in the safety, quality, efficacy of organ donation and transplantation of human organs, tissues, and body parts.

translations.source: Izici zokuziphatha zokufakelwa kabusha zihamba phambili. Ikakhulukazi, izidingo zeziguli ezingahlangatshezwana nazo kanye nokushoda kokufakelwa kabusha kuholela esilingweni sokushushumbiswa kwezingxenye zomzimba womuntu ukuze zifakwe kabusha. Ukufakelwa komuntu Amaseli omuntu kanye nezicubu zokufakelwa kabusha kungasindisa izimpilo noma kubuyisele imisebenzi ebalulekile. Isibonelo, icorneal graft ingabuyisela ukubona ebumpumputheni be-cornea;
translations.reference: The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;
translations.hypothesis: The ethical considerations of transplantation are paramount. Specifically, the conflicting needs of patients awaiting transplants and the scarcity of transplantable organs often lead to difficult decisions regarding the allocation of human body parts. Human cell and tissue transplantation can save lives or restore vital functions. For example, a corneal graft can restore vision in patients with corneal damage.

translations.source: ukufakelwa kabusha kwehaematopoietic stem cell kungalapha izifo zokuzalwa noma ezitholiwe kubandakanya neleukemia Bona ulwazi olwengeziwe ngamaseli weHaematopoeitic Stem Cells ukufakelwa kwe-valve yenhliziyo yomuntu kuvame ukwenza isimo esingcono kakhulu sokubuyisela futhi iziguli ezithola usizo azidingi ukwelashwa kwesikhathi eside okulwa nokuhlangana. I-Xenotransplantation IXenotransplantation, isilwane ukuya kumuntu, okuchazwa njengamaseli aphilayo, izicubu noma izitho zemvelaphi yezilwane kanye noketshezi lomzimba womuntu, amaseli, izicubu noma izitho ezine-ex vivo nalezi zinto eziphilayo, zexenogeneic, zinamandla okwenza enye into yemvelaphi yomuntu kanye nokuvala ukushoda kwezinto ezisetshenziswa abantu zokufakelwa kabusha.
translations.reference: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation.
translations.hypothesis: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can treat congenital or acquired diseases, including leukemia. For more information about hematopoietic stem cells, human heart valve replacement often provides a better outcome for restoration, and patients who receive this treatment may not require long-term medication to manage complications. Xenotransplantation, which involves the use of living cells, tissues, or organs from animals, as well as human body fluids, cells, tissues, or organs that have been modified with these living components, is a xenogeneic process with the potential to create human-like tissues and address the shortage of human-derived materials for transplantation.

translations.source: Umnikelo: Iminikelo evela kubantu abashonile kufanele ithuthukiswe ukuze ibe namandla abo aphezulu okwelapha ukugwema, noma nini lapho kunokwenzeka, izingozi ezingokwemvelo zokunikela abaphilayo, njengoba kushiwo Kumgomo 3 Wokuqondisa weWHO. Kunesidingo esibonakalayo sokuthi imiphakathi, kanye nabasebenzi bezempilo, bafundiswe kangcono ngokunikela nokufakelwa futhi lokho kuwukhiye wempumelelo yezinhlelo zokunikela abashonile. Nokho, naphezu kokusetshenziswa njalo kwezinto ezinikelwa kubanikeli abashonile, iminikelo evela kubanikeli abaphilayo iyadingeka kwezinye izinhlobo zokufakelwa noma ukunxephezela ukunikezwa okulinganiselwe kwezinto ezitholakala kubanikeli abashonile ukuze kuhlangatshezwane nezidingo zesiguli. Ukunikela okuphilayo kwenziwa kanjalo naphezu kweqiniso lokuthi kuhilela izingozi kumnikeli okungenzeka azinakwa. Njengoba ukuthengwa kwezinto ezisetshenziswa abantu zokufakelwa kabusha kusuka kubanikeli abashonile noma abaphilayo kanye nokufakelwa kabusha kwe-allogeneic okulandelayo kungase kufake izingozi zezimiso zokuziphatha nezokuphepha kubo bobabili umamukeli kanye nomnikeli, ukulawulwa okuqinile nokwengamela okusebenzayo kufanele kwenziwe iziphathimandla zezempilo ukuze zibavikele.
translations.reference: Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.
translations.hypothesis: Donation: Donations from deceased individuals should be optimized to maximize their therapeutic potential, and to avoid, whenever possible, the natural risks associated with donating from living individuals, as stated in WHO Directive 3. There is a clear need for better education of the public, and healthcare professionals, about donation and transplantation, and this is key to the success of deceased donor transplantation programs. However, despite the use of donations from deceased donors, donations from living donors are often necessary for certain types of transplantation or to supplement limited resources available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is therefore undertaken despite the fact that it carries potential risks for the donor that cannot be completely eliminated. As the procurement of materials used in transplantation from either deceased or living donors, and subsequent allogeneic transplantation, may involve ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict and enforceable regulations must be implemented by health authorities to protect them.

translations.source: Izimiso Eziqondisayo zigunyaza ukunakekelwa okufanele kubanikeli nabamukeli. Ukwengamela okusobala kweziphathimandla zezempilo mayelana neminikelo kanye nemisebenzi yokufakelwa kabusha kubalulekile futhi ukuze kwandiswe ukwethenjwa komphakathi ohlelweni. Ukwengeza, isinqumo sokuba umnikeli ngokuvamile sisekelwe ekuqondeni ukuthi umnikelo ekutholakaleni kwezinsiza zokufakelwa kabusha kungase ngolunye usuku kuzuze izidingo zezempilo zomndeni walowo onikelayo. Uma sikulungele ukwamukela ukufakelwa kabusha uma sikudinga, kufanele sikulungele ukunikeza Izimiso Zokuziphatha: Izimiso Eziqondisayo zeWHO Kungqamuzana Lomuntu, Izicubu Nezitho Zokufakelwa
translations.reference: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation
translations.hypothesis: The Guiding Principles emphasize the care that should be given to both donors and recipients. Clear communication from healthcare professionals regarding donations and transplantation procedures is crucial to building public trust in the system. Furthermore, the decision to donate is often based on the understanding that the donated resources could one day benefit the donor's own family. If we are willing to accept transplantation when we need it, we should also be willing to give according to the Principles of Conduct: WHO Guiding Principles on Organ, Tissue and Cell Transplantation.

translations.source: Ngo-1987 iWorld Health Assembly yamashumi amane, ephathelene nokuhweba ngenzuzo ezithweni zomuntu, yaqala ukulungiswa kweMigomo Yokuqondisa YeWHO Yokutshala Utshani, eyagunyazwa nguMkhandlu ngo-1991 ngesinqumo esithi WHA44.25. Le Migomo Eqondisayo ibe nomthelela omkhulu kumakhodi nezinqubo zochwepheshe kanye nemithetho emhlabeni wonke cishe phakathi neminyaka engamashumi amabili. Ngemva kwenqubo yokubonisana eyathatha iminyaka eminingana, Umhlangano Wezempilo Womhlaba Wamashumi Ayisithupha Nantathu wamukela isinqumo esithi WHA63.22 mhla zingama-21 kuNhlaba wezi-2010, esigunyaza Izimiso Zokuqondisa zeWHO ezibuyekeziwe kanye nokuhlonza izindawo zenqubekelaphambili ukuze kuthuthukiswe izinqubo zokunikela nokutshala. Isimemezelo seIstanbul Ochwepheshe bomnikelo nokufakelwa kabusha abavela kuzo zonke izifunda, ngokusebenzisa izinhlangano zabo eziningi nezikhungo, bagunyaza Isimemezelo sase-Istanbul mayelana nokushushumbiswa kwezitho zomzimba kanye nezokuvakasha zokufakelwa kabusha ezakhiwe ngaphansi kobuholi beTransplantation Society kanye ne-International Society of Nephrology ngoNhlaba 2008.
translations.reference: In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis: In 1987, the 40th World Health Assembly, focusing on the ethical aspects of human organ transplantation, initiated the development of the WHO Guiding Principles on Organ Transplantation, which were adopted by the Assembly in 1991 through resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles had a significant impact on professional codes and guidelines, as well as legislation worldwide, for nearly two decades. Following a multi-year consultation process, the 63rd World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on May 21, 2010, approving the revised WHO Guiding Principles on Organ Transplantation and identifying areas for further development in transplantation practices. The Istanbul Declaration: Professional Ethics for Transplantation was endorsed by transplant professionals from all regions, through their various organizations and centers, and adopted the Istanbul Declaration on the Ethical Principles for Organ Donation and Transplantation, developed under the auspices of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in November 2008.

translations.source: Ukuhamba nempilo Uhlolojikelele: Ukuvakasha kwamanye amazwe kungabeka engcupheni ehlukahlukene empilweni, kuye ngezici zombili zomhambi kanye nohambo. Abahambi bangase bahlangabezane nezinguquko ezingazelelwe neziphawulekayo endaweni, umswakama, amagciwane, nezinga lokushisa, okungase kubangele impilo ebuthaka. Ukwengeza, izingozi ezingathi sína zezempilo zingase ziphakame ezindaweni lapho indawo yokuhlala isezingeni eliphansi, inhlanzeko kanye nokukhucululwa kwendle kunganele, izinsizakalo zezokwelapha azithuthukisiwe kahle futhi amanzi ahlanzekile engatholakali. Ukuvakasha kwamazwe ngamazwe kuyanda, njengoba izigidi zabantu zihamba ngezinjongo zobungcweti, zezenhlalo, zokungcebeleka kanye nokusiza abantu unyaka ngamunye.
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year.
translations.hypothesis: Travel and Health: Overview: International travel can expose individuals to a variety of health risks, depending on both the traveler's characteristics and the nature of the trip. Travelers may encounter unexpected and significant changes in altitude, climate, exposure to pathogens, and temperature, which can negatively impact their health. Furthermore, various health risks can be heightened in areas where living conditions are substandard, sanitation and hygiene are inadequate, healthcare services are poorly developed, and access to clean water is limited. International travel is increasing, with millions of people traveling for business, leisure, education, and humanitarian purposes each year.

translations.source: Bonke abahambi kufanele balungiselele inhlobonhlobo yezingozi zezempilo abangachayeka kuzo ezindaweni abangazazi ngaphambi, ngesikhathi nangemuva kohambo. Abantu abahlela ukuvakasha kufanele bafune iseluleko mayelana nezingozi ezingase zibe khona ezindaweni abazikhethile futhi baqonde indlela engcono kakhulu yokuvikela impilo yabo kanye nokunciphisa ubungozi bokuthola izifo. Ukuhlela phambili, izinyathelo ezifanele zokuvimbela kanye nezinyathelo zokuqapha ngokucophelela kungavikela impilo yabahambi, futhi kunciphise ubungozi bengozi kanye nokuthola izifo. Nakuba umkhakha wezokwelapha kanye nemboni yezokuvakasha inganikeza usizo olubanzi nezeluleko eziphusile, kusewumthwalo wemfanelo womhambi ukufuna ulwazi, ukuqonda ubungozi obuhilelekile kanye nokuthatha izinyathelo zokuphepha ezidingekayo ukuze avikele impilo yakhe ngenkathi ehamba. Ubungozi: Lapho kunqunywa ubungozi bezempilo bokuvakasha kwamanye amazwe, abahambi kanye nabasebenzi bezempilo kufanele bacabangele lezi zici ezibalulekile ezilandelayo:
translations.reference: All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors:
translations.hypothesis: All travelers should prepare for a variety of health risks they may encounter in unfamiliar locations, both before, during, and after their trip. Individuals planning to travel should seek advice regarding potential health hazards in their chosen destinations and understand the best ways to protect their health and minimize the risk of illness. Careful planning, appropriate preventative measures, and vigilant monitoring can help protect the health of travelers and reduce the risk of accidents and disease. While the medical community and the tourism industry can provide valuable assistance and advice, it is ultimately the responsibility of the traveler to seek information, understand the potential risks, and take the necessary precautions to protect their health while traveling. Risks: When assessing the health risks of international travel, travelers and healthcare professionals should consider the following important factors:

translations.source: indlela yokuthutha indawo (izi) ubude besikhathi kanye nenkathi yokuhamba inhloso yokuhamba amazinga ezindawo zokuhlala, ukuhlanzeka kokudla kanye nokukhucululwa kwendle
translations.reference: mode of transport destination(s) duration and season of travel purpose of travel standards of accommodation, food hygiene and sanitation
translations.hypothesis: The method of transporting goods (products) considers factors such as: the distance and duration of the journey, the purpose of the transportation, the quality of the packaging, the safety of the food, and environmental impact.

translations.source: ukuziphatha komhambi impilo engaphansi yesihambi Abahambi bangase bahlangabezane nalezi zingozi zempilo ezilandelayo kuye ngendawo abaya kuyo: ukulunywa yizilwane nezinambuzane, okuhlanganisa omiyane, imikhaza, amazeze nezinye izimpukane, ezingasabalalisa izifo ezinjengomalaleveva. ukuchayeka egazini noma kolunye uketshezi lomzimba
translations.reference: behaviour of the traveller underlying health of the traveller Travellers may encounter the following health risks depending on their destination: animal and insect bites, including mosquitoes, ticks, fleas, and some flies, which can spread diseases such as malaria exposure to blood or other bodily fluids
translations.hypothesis: Traveler's health: Potential health risks for travelers. Travelers may encounter the following health risks, depending on their destination: animal and insect bites, including mosquitoes, ticks, spiders, and other insects, which can transmit diseases such as malaria. Bloodborne or other bodily fluid exposure.

translations.source: izinguquko zemvelo, njengokuphakama, umswakama, izinga lokushisa izifo ezibangelwa ukudla kanye namanzi izinsiza zezokwelapha ezinganele ukulimala, okuhlanganisa izingozi zomgwaqo noma imisebenzi yokuzijabulisa impilo engokwengqondo, okuhlanganisa ukucindezeleka kanye nokushaqeka kwamasiko
translations.reference: environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock
translations.hypothesis: Environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, and temperature; foodborne and waterborne illnesses; inadequate healthcare; injuries, including those from traffic accidents or recreational activities; and mental health issues, including stress and cultural shock.

translations.source: Abahambi bangase bachayeke ezifweni eziningi ezithathelwanayo, futhi eziningi zazo zingavinjelwa ngokugoma. Kodwa ke, kunezifo ezithathelwanayo, kuhlanganise nezinye eziyingozi kakhulu, ezingenayo imithi yokugoma. Izindawo lapho indawo yokuhlala, inhlanzeko nokukhucululwa kwendle, ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kanye nekhwalithi yamanzi kusezingeni eliphezulu kubangela ubungozi obuncane obumbalwa empilweni yabahambi. Lapho lezi zinsizakalo zinganikezwanga, abahambi kufanele bathathe izinyathelo eziqinile zokugwema ukugula. Izixwayiso zokuhamba ezivela emithonjeni kahulumeni kufanele zithathwe ngokungathi sína.
translations.reference: Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously.
translations.hypothesis: Travelers may encounter various infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented with vaccinations. However, there are infectious diseases, including some of the most serious ones, for which there are no vaccines. Areas where living conditions, sanitation, environmental control, healthcare, and water quality are of a high standard pose a lower risk to travelers' health. When these services are lacking, travelers should take strict precautions to avoid illness. Travel advisories from government sources should be carefully considered.

translations.source: Ukuvimbela: Ngaphambi kohambo: Thintana nesikhungo semithi yokuhamba noma udokotela, okungcono amasonto ama-4-8 ngaphambi kokuhamba ukuze uthole noma yimiphi imigomo edingekayo; funda ngezifo zendawo ezihlobene nendawo oya kuyo; cela ulwazi ngengozi kamalaleveva kanye nokuvimbela ukulunywa omiyane. Lokhu kungase kuhlanganise ukuthenga imithi yokuvimbela efanele kanye nokupakisha inethi kanye nomuthi oxosha izinambuzane; thola ukuhlolwa kodokotela kanye namazinyo;
translations.reference: Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up;
translations.hypothesis: Prevention: Before traveling: Consult with a travel clinic or your doctor, ideally 4-8 weeks before your trip, to obtain any necessary vaccinations; learn about local diseases prevalent in the area you are visiting; inquire about malaria risks and prevention methods. This may include purchasing appropriate malaria medication and packing mosquito nets and insect repellent; get a medical check-up and dental examination.

translations.source: thola imiyalelo yemithi ngokuya ngobude besikhathi sokuhlala futhi uhlanganise isethi yezokwelapha efanele; ukuthenga umshwalense wezokwelapha ngekhava efanele phesheya Phakathi nohambo: dla kuphela ukudla okuphekwe kahle futhi uphuze kuphela iziphuzo ezibandayo ezisemabhodleleni noma ezipakishiwe. Bilisa amanzi okuphuza uma ukuphepha kuthandabuza.
translations.reference: obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful.
translations.hypothesis: Obtain medical advice based on the duration of your stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase travel insurance with adequate coverage for overseas travel. During your trip: only consume properly cooked food and only drink chilled beverages from sealed bottles or containers. Boil drinking water if safety is uncertain.

translations.source: qaphela izingozi noma izinkinga ezihlobene nethrafikhi, izilwane, ukungezwani komzimba nezinto ezithile, ilanga nemidlalo; phatha ikhadi elibonisa iqembu lakho legazi; gqoka isongo lesixwayiso sezokwelapha, uma une-allergies noma isifo esingamahlalakhona Abahambi kufanele bathole ukuhlolwa kodokotela lapho bebuya uma: buya nomkhuhlane ovela ezweni lapho umalaleveva ukhona noma okungenzeka ukuthi ukhona
translations.reference: be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, allergies, sun and sport; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet, if you have allergies or a chronic disease Travellers should obtain a medical examination on their return if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria is or may be present
translations.hypothesis: Please be aware of potential hazards or issues related to traffic, animals, physical exertion, and certain activities; carry a card indicating your blood type; wear a medical alert bracelet if you have allergies or a chronic condition. Travelers should consult a doctor upon their return if: they develop a fever after returning from a country where malaria is present or may be present.

translations.source: uhlushwa isifo esingamahlalakhona bathola ukugula emasontweni alandela ukubuya kwabo ekhaya uthole ukwelashelwa umalaleveva ngesikhathi ehamba kungenzeka ukuthi wayechayeke esifweni esiyingozi esithathelwanayo wachitha izinyanga ezingaphezu kwezingu-3 ezweni elisathuthuka
translations.reference: suffer from a chronic disease experience illness in the weeks following their return home received treatment for malaria while travelling may have been exposed to a serious infectious disease spent more than 3 months in a developing country
translations.hypothesis: She was suffering from a chronic illness, and developed a fever the weeks after returning home. She was also exposed to a potentially dangerous pathogen while traveling. She spent over three months in a developing country.

translations.source: Isifo sofuba Uhlolojikelele: iTB ibangelwa amagciwane (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) futhi ngokuvamile ihlasela amaphaphu. iTB isakazwa ngomoya lapho abantu abaneTB yamaphaphu bekhwehlela, bethimula noma bekhafula. Umuntu udinga ukuhogela amagciwane ambalwa ukuze angenwe yilesi sifo. Njalo ngonyaka, abantu abayizigidi eziyi-10 baphathwa isifo sofuba (TB).
translations.reference: Tuberculosis Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it most often affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with lung TB cough, sneeze or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people fall ill with tuberculosis (TB).
translations.hypothesis: Tuberculosis (TB) Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and primarily affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with pulmonary TB cough, sneeze, or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few bacteria to become infected. Every year, approximately 10 million people are affected by tuberculosis (TB).

translations.source: Nakuba kuyisifo esingagwemeka futhi eselaphekayo, abantu abayizigidi ezingu-1.5 babulawa iTB unyaka ngamunye – okuyenza ibe umbulali ohamba phambili emhlabeni wonke. iTB iyimbangela ehamba phambili yokufa kwabantu abane-HIV futhi iphinde ibe neqhaza elikhulu ekwandeni kwemithi elwa namagciwane Iningi labantu abaguliswa yiTB lihlala emazweni anemali ephansi noma ephakathi, kodwa iTB ikhona emhlabeni wonke. Cishe ingxenye yabo bonke abantu abaneTB bangatholakala emazweni angu-8: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines naseNingizimu Afrika. Cishe ingxenye yesine yabantu emhlabeni wonke kulinganiselwa ukuthi inamagciwane eTB, kodwa abantu abaningi ngeke baqhubeke nokuba nesifo sofuba futhi abanye bazosiqeda lesi sifo.
translations.reference: Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the world’s top infectious killer. TB is the leading cause of death of people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance Most of the people who fall ill with TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present all over the world. About half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines and South Africa. About a quarter of the global population is estimated to have been infected with TB bacteria, but most people will not go on to develop TB disease and some will clear the infection.
translations.hypothesis: Although tuberculosis is a preventable and curable disease, it kills 1.5 million people each year, making it one of the leading causes of death worldwide. TB is a major cause of death among people with HIV, and it also contributes to the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Most people who are infected with TB live in low- or middle-income countries, but TB is present in all parts of the world. Approximately one-third of all people with TB are found in just eight countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, the Philippines, and South Africa. It is estimated that about one-quarter of the world's population is infected with TB, but many people will never develop the disease, and some will eventually clear the infection.

translations.source: Labo abangenwe yilesi sifo kodwa abangakaguli (okwamanje) abanalesi sifo abakwazi ukusidlulisela. Abantu abangenwe amagciwane eTB basengozini engu-5-10% yokuphila konke yokugula iTB. Labo abanamasosha omzimba asengozini, njengabantu abaphila neHIV, abangondleki kahle noma abanesifo sikashukela, noma abantu abasebenzisa ugwayi, basengozini enkulu yokugula. Izimpawu: Izimpawu ezijwayelekile zesifo sofuba zihlanganisa: Ukukhwehlela isikhathi eside, ubuhlungu besifuba, ubuthakathaka noma ukukhathala, ukuncipha komzimba, Imfiva, Ukujuluka ebusuku. Ngokuvamile, lezi zimpawu zizoba mnene izinyanga eziningi, ngaleyo ndlela kuholele ekubambezelekeni kokufuna ukunakekelwa futhi kwandise ingozi yokusabalalisa ukutheleleka kwabanye.
translations.reference: Those who are infected but not (yet) ill with the disease cannot transmit it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5–10% lifetime risk of falling ill with TB. Those with compromised immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of falling ill. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: Prolonged cough, Chest pain, Weakness or fatigue, Weight loss, Fever, Night sweats Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, thus leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading the infection to others.
translations.hypothesis: Those who have been infected with the disease but are not yet showing symptoms (are currently asymptomatic) can still transmit it. People who have been infected with tuberculosis (TB) have a 5-10% lifetime risk of developing active TB disease. Individuals with weakened immune systems, such as those with HIV, malnutrition, diabetes, or those who smoke, are at higher risk of developing the disease. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB include: persistent cough, chest pain, weakness or fatigue, weight loss, fever, and night sweats. Often, these symptoms develop gradually over many months, which can lead to delays in seeking medical care and increase the risk of spreading the infection to others.

translations.source: Uma umhlinzeki wezempilo esola ukuthi isiguli sinesifo sofuba, bazothumela isiguli ukuthi siyohlolwa. Esimeni sokusolwa kwesifo sofuba samaphaphu, iziguli zizocelwa ukuthi zinikeze isampula yesikhwehlela ukuze kuhlolelwe amagciwane eTB. Ngesifo sofuba esingesona esamaphaphu, amasampula oketshezi lomzimba oluthintekile kanye nezicubu angahlolwa. IWHO incoma ukuhlolwa kokuxilonga kwamangqamuzana okusheshayo njengokuhlola kokuqala kwabantu ababonisa izimpawu nezimpawu zeTB. Amanye amathuluzi okuxilonga angabandakanya iSputum smear microscopy nama-X-ray esifuba.
translations.reference: If the healthcare provider suspects a patient to have TB disease, they will send the patient for testing. In the case of suspected lung TB disease, patients will be asked to give a sputum sample for testing for TB bacteria. For non-lung TB disease, samples of affected body fluids and tissue can be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools can include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays.
translations.hypothesis: When a healthcare provider suspects that a patient has tuberculosis, they will refer the patient for testing. In the case of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis, patients will be asked to provide a sputum sample to be examined for TB bacteria. For non-pulmonary tuberculosis, samples of other body fluids or tissues may be tested. The WHO recommends rapid diagnostic tests as the initial screening method for people who show symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools may include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays.

translations.source: Ngokungenwa yiTB, umuntu ungenwa amagciwane eTB alele engasebenzi emzimbeni. Lesi sifo singakhula sibe yisifo sofuba uma amasosha omzimba eba buthaka. Abantu abaneTB ababonisi zimpawu noma izimpawu zeTB. Ukuhlonza ukutheleleka ngeTB, abahlinzeki bezempilo bazohlola iziguli ezisengozini ukuze bakhiphe iTB ephilayo, futhi bangasebenzisa isikhumba noma ukuhlolwa kwegazi ukuhlola ukuthi ayinalo yini iTB. Ukwelashwa: Isifo sofuba siyelapheka.
translations.reference: With TB infection, a person gets infected with TB bacteria that lie inactive in the body. This infection can develop into TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infection do not show any signs or symptoms of TB. To identify TB infection, healthcare providers will screen at-risk patients to rule out active TB, and they may use a skin or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is curable.
translations.hypothesis: In latent TB infection, a person has TB bacteria in their body, but the bacteria are inactive and not causing any symptoms. This condition can develop into active TB disease if the immune system becomes weakened. People with latent TB infection may not have any symptoms or may have mild symptoms of TB. To screen for TB infection, healthcare providers may test patients who are at risk to determine if they have active TB, and they may use skin tests or blood tests to check for TB. Treatment: Tuberculosis is curable.

translations.source: Yelashwa ngenkambo evamile yezinyanga eziyi-6 yama-antibiotic ama-4. Imithi ejwayelekile ihlanganisa irifampicin ne-isoniazid. Kwezinye izimo amagciwane eTB awaphenduli emithini evamile. Kulokhu, isiguli sineTB engazweli emishanguzweni. kuTB engazweli emishanguzweni yinde futhi iyinkimbinkimbi.
translations.reference: It is treated by standard 6 month course of 4 antibiotics. Common drugs include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases the TB bacteria does not respond to the standard drugs. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex.
translations.hypothesis: The standard treatment for tuberculosis typically involves a 6-month course of four different antibiotics. These medications usually include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases, the tuberculosis bacteria are resistant to the standard medications. This means the patient has drug-resistant tuberculosis. Treatment for drug-resistant tuberculosis is longer and more complex.

translations.source: Imithi yeTB inikezwa isiguli ngolwazi, ukugadwa nokusekelwa umsebenzi wezempilo noma ivolontiya eliqeqeshiwe. Ngaphandle kokusekelwa okunjalo, ukubambelela ekwelashweni kungaba nzima. Uma ukwelashwa kungaqedwa kahle, lesi sifo singamelana nemithi futhi singasakazeka. Esimeni sokutheleleka ngeTB (lapho isiguli sitheleleke ngamagciwane eTB kodwa singaguli), ukwelashwa kokuvimbela iTB kungenziwa ukuze kunqandwe ukuqala kwesifo. Lokhu kwelashwa kusebenzisa izidakamizwa ezifanayo isikhathi esifushane.
translations.reference: The course of TB drugs is provided to the patient with information, supervision and support by a health worker or trained volunteer. Without such support, treatment adherence can be difficult. If the treatment is not properly completed, the disease can become drug-resistant and can spread. In the case of TB infection (where the patient is infected with TB bacteria but not ill), TB preventive treatment can be given to stop the onset of disease. This treatment uses the same drugs for a shorter time.
translations.hypothesis: TB medication is provided to the patient with guidance, monitoring, and support from a healthcare professional or trained volunteer. Without this support, adherence to treatment can be challenging. If treatment is completed successfully, the disease can be overcome and will no longer be contagious. In the case of latent TB infection (where the patient is infected with TB bacteria but is not sick), preventative treatment can be administered to prevent the development of the disease. This treatment uses the same medications for a shorter period.

translations.source: Izinketho zokwelashwa zakamuva zifinyeze ubude besikhathi sokwelashwa saba yizinyanga ezi-1 noma ezi-3 kuphela, uma kuqhathaniswa nezinyanga ezingu-6 ezedlule.
translations.reference: Recent treatment options have shortened the duration to treatment to only 1 or 3 months, as compared to 6 months in the past.
translations.hypothesis: The latest treatment options have significantly reduced the duration of treatment, now lasting only 1 or 3 months, compared to the previous 6 months.

translations.source: Imisebe yeUltraviolet Uhlolojikelele: Imisebe ye-Ultraviolet (UV) ivala ububanzi be-wavelength obungu-100–400 nm, okuyifrikhwensi ephezulu kanye nobude obuphansi begagasi kunokukhanya okubonakalayo. Imisebe ye-UV ivela ngokwemvelo ivela elangeni, kodwa ingabuye idalwe ngemithombo yokwenziwa esetshenziswa embonini, ezentengiselwano nezokuzijabulisa. Isifunda se-UV simboza ububanzi be-wavelength 100-400 nm futhi sihlukaniswe ngamabhande amathathu: UVA (315-400 nm) UVB (280-315 nm) UVC (100-280 nm). Njengoba ukukhanya kwelanga kudlula emkhathini, yonke i-UVC kanye nama-90% emisebe ye-UVB amuncwa i-ozone, umhwamuko wamanzi, umoyampilo kanye nesikhutha.
translations.reference: Ultraviolet radiation Overview: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers the wavelength range of 100–400 nm, which is a higher frequency and lower wavelength than visible light. UV radiation comes naturally from the sun, but it can also be created by artificial sources used in industry, commerce and recreation. The UV region covers the wavelength range 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and approximately 90% of UVB radiation is absorbed by ozone, water vapour, oxygen and carbon dioxide.
translations.hypothesis: Ultraviolet Radiation Overview: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers a range of wavelengths from 100–400 nm, which is a higher frequency and shorter wavelength than visible light. UV radiation occurs naturally from the sun, but can also be produced by artificial sources used in industry, medicine, and entertainment. The UV spectrum covers a range of wavelengths from 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm), UVB (280-315 nm), and UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and 90% of UVB radiation are absorbed by the ozone layer, water vapor, and other atmospheric particles.

translations.source: Imisebe ye-UVA ayithinteki kangako emkhathini. Ngakho ke, imisebe ye-UV efinyelela ebusweni bomhlaba ikakhulukazi yakhiwe i-UVA enengxenye encane ye-UVB. Inani lemisebe ye-UV evela elangeni elishaya ebusweni boMhlaba lincike ezintweni ezimbalwa, okuhlanganisa ubude belanga esibhakabhakeni, i-latitude, ukumboza kwamafu, ukuphakama, ukujiya kongqimba lwe-ozone kanye nokubonakala komhlabathi. Ukwehliswa kongqimba lwe-ozone ngenxa yokungcola okudalwe umuntu kwandisa inani le-UVA ne-UVB elifika phezulu. Lokhu kungaba nomthelela empilweni yomuntu, izilwane, izinto eziphilayo zasolwandle kanye nempilo yezitshalo.
translations.reference: UVA radiation is less affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the UV radiation reaching the Earth’s surface is largely composed of UVA with a small UVB component. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that hits the Earth’s surface depends on several factors, including the sun’s height in the sky, latitude, cloud cover, altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer and ground reflection. Reductions in the ozone layer due to human-created pollution increase the amount of UVA and UVB that reaches the surface. This can impact human health, animals, marine organisms and plant life.
translations.hypothesis: UV radiation is not as strongly affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the UV radiation that reaches the Earth's surface is primarily UVA, with a smaller proportion of UVB. The amount of UV radiation reaching the Earth's surface depends on several factors, including the length of the day, the latitude, the amount of cloud cover, the altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer, and the reflectivity of the Earth's surface. The depletion of the ozone layer due to human-caused pollution increases the amount of UVA and UVB that reaches the surface. This can have negative effects on human health, animals, marine life, and plant life.

translations.source: Kubantu, ukwanda kokuchayeka kwe-UV kungabangela umdlavuza wesikhumba, ulwelwesi lwamabala kanye nokulimala kwamasosha omzimba. Ukuvimbela: Ukukhuphuka kwenani leziguli zomdlavuza wesikhumba emashumini eminyaka adlule kuhlobene kakhulu nokuchayeka elangeni ngesikhathi semisebenzi yangaphandle kanye nemithombo yokwenziwa yemisebe ye-UV efana nezibani zelanga nemibhede yokushuka. Ukuchayeka ngokweqile nakho kuyimbangela eyinhloko yemiphumela eyingozi emehlweni namasosha omzimba. Ukwamukela izinyathelo ezimbalwa ezilula zokuphepha kunganciphisa kakhulu ingozi yalezi zimo zempilo: Nciphisa isikhathi elangeni lasemini kusukela ngo-10 ekuseni kuya ku-4 ntambama, ikakhulukazi ngezinsuku lapho inkomba ye-UV iphezulu. Lapho imisebe ye-UV inamandla kakhulu futhi kufanele ube ngaphandle, zama ukuthola umthunzi futhi ugqoke izingubo zokuzivikela, kuhlanganise nesigqoko esinomphetho esinikeza isivikelo elangeni ekhanda lakho, ebusweni nasentanyeni.
translations.reference: In humans, increased UV exposure can cause skin cancers, cataracts and immune system damage. Prevention: The rise in the number of cases of skin cancer over the past decades is strongly related to increased exposure to the sun during outdoor activities and to artificial sources of UV radiation such as sunlamps and tanning beds. Overexposure is also the underlying cause of harmful effects on the eyes and immune system. Adopting a few simple precautions can greatly reduce the risk of these health conditions: Limit time in the midday sun from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., particularly on days when the UV index is high When UV rays are most intense and you must be outside, try to find shade and wear protective clothing, including a brimmed hat that provides sun protection for your head, face and neck.
translations.hypothesis: For humans, increased exposure to UV radiation can lead to skin cancer, cataracts, and damage to the immune system. Prevention: The rise in skin cancer cases over the past few decades is largely linked to sun exposure during outdoor activities and the use of UV-emitting devices such as tanning beds. Excessive exposure is also a major cause of serious eye problems and immune system damage. By adopting a few simple safety measures, the risk of these health problems can be significantly reduced: Limit your time in the sun during peak hours, from 10 am to 4 pm, especially on days when the UV index is high. When you must be outdoors during peak UV hours, seek shade and wear protective clothing, including a hat with a brim to protect your head, face, and neck from the sun.

translations.source: Sebenzisa isikrini sokuvikela ilanga esino-spectrum obanzi okungenani esingu-15 SPF – ngokuhle kakhulu– futhi uphinde ufake isicelo njalo emahoreni ama-2. Gwema amalambu elanga kanye nezindawo zokushuka isikhumba, njengoba zaziwa ngokulimaza isikhumba namehlo. Ukuchayeka kwe-UV kwezingane kufanele kukhawulwe ngokucophelela ngoba zisesimweni sokukhula futhi ngenxa yalokho zisengozini enkulu ezingozini zemvelo kunabantu abadala. Imisebenzi eminingi ebalulekile efana namasosha omzimba ayikakhuli ngokugcwele lapho ezalwa, futhi izindawo ezingaphephile zingaphazamisa ukukhula kwazo okuvamile. Impendulo yeWHO: IWHO isebenza Namazwe Angamalungu kanye nabalingani ukukhulisa ukuqonda komphakathi ngemiphumela yokuchayeka ngokweqile emisebeni ye-ultraviolet.
translations.reference: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen of at least 15 SPF – ideally higher– and reapply every 2 hours. Avoid sunlamps and tanning parlours, as they are known to damage the skin and eyes. Children’s UV exposure should be carefully limited because they are in a dynamic state of growth and therefore more susceptible to environmental threats than adults. Many vital functions such as the immune system are not fully developed at birth, and unsafe environments may interfere with their normal development. WHO Response: WHO works with Member States and partners to increase public understanding of the effects of overexposure to ultraviolet radiation.
translations.hypothesis: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen with a minimum SPF of 15 – ideally higher – and reapply it every two hours. Avoid prolonged sun exposure and tanning beds, as these are known to damage the skin and eyes. Children should be protected from UV exposure with extra care, as they are still developing and are more vulnerable to environmental hazards than adults. Many important bodily functions are not fully developed at birth, and exposure to harmful elements can disrupt their normal development. WHO's response: The WHO works with member states and partners to raise public awareness about the harmful effects of excessive exposure to ultraviolet radiation.

translations.source: Uhlelo lwe-INTERSUN luwukubambisana phakathi kweWHO, i-United Nations Environment Programme, iWorld Meteorological Organization, i-International Agency yeCancer Research kanye neKhomishini Yamazwe Ngamazwe Yokuvikela iNon-ionizing Radiation. Ikhuthaza futhi ihlole ucwaningo ngemiphumela yezempilo yemisebe ye-UV, futhi ithuthukise impendulo efanele ngokusebenzisa imihlahlandlela, izincomo kanye nokusabalalisa ulwazi. Imigomo yalolu hlelo kuhlanganise nokunikeza iseluleko esisebenzayo nesiphusile mayelana nemithelela yezempilo nemvelo yokuchayeka kwe-UV, ukukhuthaza amazwe ukuthi athathe isinyathelo sokunciphisa izingozi zezempilo ezibangelwa yi-UV kanye nokunikeza isiqondiso kuziphathimandla zikazwelonke mayelana nezinhlelo zokuqwashisa ngelanga. Kuze kube manje, lolu hlelo lusebenzisana nochwepheshe nama-ejensi angochwepheshe ukwenza imisebenzi ebalulekile yocwaningo, luhlonze futhi lulinganise ubungozi bezempilo emisebeni ye-UV, luthuthukise izibikezelo ezinokwethenjelwa zemiphumela yezempilo neyemvelo yezinguquko zokuchayeka kwe-UV ngokuncipha kwe-ozone yeStratospheric, futhi lithuthukise izindlela ezingokoqobo ukuqapha ushintsho emiphumeleni yezempilo ebangelwa yi-UV ngokuhamba kwesikhathi. Lolu hlelo luhlinzeka ngamathuluzi neziqondiso ezihlobene nenkomba ye-UV, ukuvikela ilanga ezinganeni, ama-sunbed okwenziwa, ezokuvakasha kanye nempilo yomsebenzi.
translations.reference: The INTERSUN Programme is a collaboration between WHO, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and evaluates research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops an appropriate response through guidelines, recommendations and information dissemination. The goals of the programme including providing practical and sound advice on the health and environmental impacts of UV exposure, to encourage countries to take action to reduce UV-induced health risks and to provide guidance to national authorities about sun awareness programmes. To this end, the programme collaborates with experts and specialist agencies to implement key research activities, identifies and quantifies health risks from UV radiation, develops reliable predictions of health and environmental consequences of changes in UV exposure with stratospheric ozone depletion, and develops practical ways of monitoring change in UV-induced health effects over time. The programme provides tools and guidelines related to the UV index, sun protection for children, artificial sunbeds, tourism and occupational health.
translations.hypothesis: The INTERSUN program is a collaboration between the World Health Organization (WHO), the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency for Research on Cancer, and the International Commission on Non-ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and supports research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops appropriate responses through guidelines, recommendations, and information dissemination. The program's objectives include providing practical and evidence-based advice on the health and environmental risks of UV exposure, encouraging countries to take action to reduce the health risks associated with UV radiation, and providing guidance to national authorities on sun protection programs. To date, the program has worked with experts and organizations to conduct important research, assess and quantify the health risks of UV radiation, develop reliable projections of the health and environmental impacts of changes in UV exposure due to stratospheric ozone depletion, and develop practical methods for monitoring changes in the health effects of UV radiation over time. The program provides tools and guidance on topics such as UV index, sun protection for children, tanning beds, tourism, and occupational health.

translations.source: Ukuhlinzekwa kwezempilo jikelele Uhlolojikelele: I-Universal health coverage (UHC) isho ukuthi bonke abantu banokufinyelela uhla oluphelele lwezinsizakalo zezempilo ezisezingeni eliphezulu abazidingayo, nini futhi kuphi, ngaphandle kobunzima bezezimali. Ihlanganisa ukuqhubeka okuphelele kwezinsizakalo zezempilo ezibalulekile, kusukela ekukhuthazeni impilo kuya ekuvimbeleni, ukwelashwa, ukuhlunyeleliswa kanye nokunakekelwa kwe-palliative. Ukufeza lesi sithembiso, amazwe adinga ukuba nezinhlelo zezempilo eziqinile, ezisebenza kahle nezilinganayo ezigxile emiphakathini ayisebenzelayo. Ukunakekelwa kwezempilo okuyisisekelo (PHC) kuyindlela ephumelela kakhulu neyonga kakhulu yokufika lapho.
translations.reference: Universal health coverage Overview: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to the full range of quality health services they need, when and where they need them, without financial hardship. It covers the full continuum of essential health services, from health promotion to prevention, treatment, rehabilitation and palliative care. To deliver on this promise, countries need to have strong, efficient and equitable health systems that are rooted in the communities they serve. Primary health care (PHC) is the most effective and cost-efficient way to get there.
translations.hypothesis: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to a comprehensive range of quality health services when and where they need them, without facing financial hardship. It includes a full continuum of essential health services, from health promotion and disease prevention to treatment, rehabilitation, and palliative care. To achieve this goal, countries need strong, well-functioning, and equitable health systems that are focused on the needs of the people they serve. Primary health care (PHC) is the most effective and efficient way to achieve this.

translations.source: Izwe ngalinye linendlela ehlukile yokuzuza i-UHC nokunquma ukuthi yini okufanele ihlanganiselwe ngokusekelwe ezidingweni zabantu bakhona kanye nezinsiza ezikhona. Ukutshala imali ku-PHC kuqinisekisa ukuthi zonke lezo zidingo ziyahlonzwa, zibekwe eqhulwini futhi zisingathwe ngendlela edidiyelwe; ukuthi kukhona abasebenzi bezempilo abaqinile nabahlomile; kanye nokuthi yonke imikhakha yomphakathi ibambe iqhaza ekubhekaneni nezici zemvelo nezenhlalo-mnotho ezithinta impilo nokuphila kahle, okuhlanganisa ukulungiselela, ukuphendula kanye nokululama ezimeni eziphuthumayo. Impendulo yeWHO: Njengesisekelo se-UHC, iWHO incoma ukuhlela kabusha izinhlelo zezempilo kusetshenziswa indlela yokunakekelwa kwezempilo okuyisisekelo okyiprimary health care (PHC). Ukuzuza i-UHC yinto eseqhulwini yeWHO, okuhloswe ngayo ukuthi abantu abayizigidi eziyizinkulungwane ezingu-1 bahlomule ekutholeni usizo lwezempilo emhlabeni wonke ngo-2025. Emazweni anezinhlelo zezempilo ezintekenteke, sigxila osizweni lwezobuchwepheshe ukuze kwakhiwe izikhungo zikazwelonke kanye nokulethwa kwezinsiza ukuze sivale izikhala ezibucayi ezimeni eziphuthumayo.
translations.reference: Every country has a different path to achieving UHC and to decide what to cover based on the needs of their populations and the resources at hand. Investing in PHC ensures that all those needs are identified, prioritized and addressed in an integrated way; that there is a robust and equipped health and care workforce; and that all sectors of society contribute to confronting the environmental and socio-economic factors that affect health and well-being, including preparing for, responding to and recovering from emergencies. WHO response: As a foundation for UHC, WHO recommends reorienting health systems using a primary health care (PHC) approach. Achieving UHC is a WHO strategic priority, with the goal of 1 billion more people benefitting from universal health coverage by 2025. In countries with fragile health systems, we focus on technical assistance to build national institutions and service delivery to fill critical gaps in emergencies.
translations.hypothesis: Every country has a unique approach to achieving Universal Health Coverage (UHC) and determines what services should be included based on the needs of its population and the resources available. Investing in primary health care (PHC) ensures that these needs are addressed comprehensively and systematically, that there are sufficient and qualified healthcare workers, and that all sectors of society participate in addressing the environmental and socio-economic factors that affect health and well-being, including preparedness, response, and recovery from emergencies. WHO's Response: As a foundation for UHC, the WHO advocates for restructuring health systems around the principles of primary health care (PHC). Achieving UHC is a top priority for the WHO, with the goal of ensuring that 1 billion more people have access to essential health services worldwide by 2025. In countries with fragile health systems, the focus is on providing technical assistance to build national capacity and deliver essential services to address critical gaps in emergency situations.

translations.source: Ezilungiselelweni eziqinile zesistimu yezempilo, siqhuba umthelela wezempilo womphakathi ekuhlinzekeni impilo yabo bonke ngezingxoxo zenqubomgomo yezinhlelo zesikhathi esizayo kanye nokwesekwa kwamasu okwenza ngcono ukusebenza. Lo msebenzi usekelwa isiqondiso esivamile nezivumelwano; idatha, ucwaningo kanye nokusungula izinto ezintsha; kanye nobuholi emikhakheni yezokuxhumana, ukumela, ukulingana ngokobulili, ukulingana kwezempilo kanye namalungelo abantu, isenzo semikhakha eminingi, kanye nezezimali. I-Ajenda ka-2030 yama-SDGs Umsebenzi weWHO uhambisana nezinjongo zeSustainable Development Goals (SDG) ezihlosiwe 3.8, ezigxile ekuzuzeni usizo lwezempilo emhlabeni wonke, okuhlanganisa nokuvikelwa kwengozi yezezimali, ukufinyelela ezinsizeni zokunakekelwa kwezempilo ezibalulekile kanye nokufinyelela ekuphepheni, ngempumelelo, kwekhwalithi nokuthengekayo. imithi ebalulekile kanye nemigomo yawo wonke umuntu. Emhlabeni jikelele, inqubekelaphambili emayelana nokuhlinzekwa kwezempilo kuyinselele. Inqubekelaphambili ekuhlinzekeni ngezinsizakalo imile ngenkathi ingxenye yabantu ababhekene nenhlekelele yokusetshenziswa kwemali kwezempilo ephuma ephaketheni ikhula ngokuqhubekayo.
translations.reference: In more robust health system settings, we drive public health impact towards health coverage for all through policy dialogue for the systems of the future and strategic support to improve performance. This work is supported by normative guidance and agreements; data, research and innovation; and leadership in the realms of diplomacy, advocacy, gender equality, health equity and human rights, multisectoral action, and finance. 2030 Agenda for SDGs WHO’s work is aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) target 3.8, which focuses on achieving universal health coverage, including financial risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines and vaccines for all. Globally, progress towards universal health coverage is a challenge. Progress in service coverage has stalled while the proportion of the population facing catastrophic out-of-pocket health spending increases continuously.
translations.hypothesis: Within a robust health system framework, we are driving progress in public health by ensuring access to healthcare for all through policy dialogues, future-oriented planning, and the strengthening of systems to improve efficiency. This work is supported by common principles and agreements; data, research, and innovation; and leadership in areas such as communication, advocacy, gender equality, health equity, human rights, multi-sectoral action, and financing. The WHO's work aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), specifically Goal 3.8, which aims to achieve universal health coverage, including financial risk protection, access to essential health services, and access to safe, effective, quality, and affordable essential medicines and vaccines for all. Globally, progress in health coverage remains a challenge. Advances in service delivery are being hampered while a growing proportion of the population faces the burden of out-of-pocket health spending.

translations.source: IWHO iqapha inqubekelaphambili futhi ishicilele imibiko yomhlaba wonke ukuze iveze ubufakazi bakamuva njalo eminyakeni emibili. Bheka imibiko lapha futhi uvakashele ikhasi lamaqiniso e-UHC ukuze uthole ulwazi olwengeziwe.
translations.reference: WHO monitors progress and publishes global reports to reveal the latest evidence every two years. See the reports here and visit the UHC fact sheet for more information.
translations.hypothesis: WHO monitors progress and publishes global reports to present the latest evidence every two years. You can find the reports here, and you can visit the UHC facts page for more information.

translations.source: Impilo yasemadolobheni Uhlolojikelele: Ukufudukela emadolobheni kungenye yezindlela ezihamba phambili emhlabeni wonke zekhulu lama-21 ezinomthelela omkhulu kwezempilo. Bangaphezu kwama-55% abantu bomhlaba abahlala ezindaweni zasemadolobheni - ingxenye okulindeleke ukuthi yenyuke ifinyelele ku-68% ngo-2050. Njengoba ukwanda kwamadolobha amaningi esikhathini esizayo kuzokwenzeka emazweni asathuthuka, umhlaba namuhla unethuba eliyingqayizivele lokuqondisa ukufudukela kwabantu emadolobheni kanye nezinye izindlela ezinkulu zokuthuthukiswa kwamadolobha ngendlela evikela futhi ikhuthaze impilo. Lokhu kubalulekile, hhayi okungenani ngoba impilo nokuphila kahle kwezakhamizi mhlawumbe kuyimpahla ebaluleke kakhulu yedolobha.
translations.reference: Urban health Overview: Urbanization is one of the leading global trends of the 21st century that has a significant impact on health. Over 55% of the world’s population live in urban areas – a proportion that is expected to increase to 68% by 2050. As most future urban growth will take place in developing countries, the world today has a unique opportunity to guide urbanization and other major urban development trends in a way that protects and promotes health. This is important, not least because the health and well-being of citizens is perhaps a city’s most important asset.
translations.hypothesis: Urban Health: An Overview: Urbanization is one of the most significant global trends of the 21st century, with a profound impact on health. More than 55% of the world's population now lives in urban areas – a proportion that is expected to rise to 68% by 2050. As urbanization continues, particularly in developing countries, the world has a unique opportunity to shape this process and guide urban development in ways that protect and promote health. This is crucial, not least because the health and well-being of citizens is arguably the most important asset of a city.

translations.source: Nokho, iningi labantu abayizigidi eziyizinkulungwane ezingu-4,2 abahlala emadolobheni alinazo izindlu nezinto zokuhamba ezintula, ukukhucululwa kwendle nokungaphathwa kahle kwemfucuza, kanye nezinga lomoya elizephula iziqondiso zeWHO. Ezinye izinhlobo zokungcola, ezinjengomsindo, ukungcoliswa kwamanzi nomhlabathi, lokho okubizwa ngokuthi iziqhingi zokushisa zasemadolobheni, kanye nokuntuleka kwendawo yokuhamba, ukugibela amabhayisikili nokuphila okumatasa kuhlanganisa ukwenza amadolobha abe yisizinda sesifo esiwumqedazwe wezifo ezingathathelwana kanye nezisusa zokushintsha kwesimo sezulu. IWHO Repository on Urban Health Umthelela: Cishe u-40% wokukhula kwedolobha usemijondolo engenawo amanzi aphephile nezindlu zangasese, kanti abantu abangu-91% basemadolobheni baphefumula umoya ongcolile. Uma kukhulunywa ngezidlo ezinempilo, ukufudukela kwabantu emadolobheni kukhulisa ibanga ukusuka epulazini kuye kwelinye, kushayela isidingo sokudla okungenampilo, okugayiwe.
translations.reference: However, most of the 4.2 billion people living in cities suffer inadequate housing and transport, poor sanitation and waste management, and air quality that fails WHO guidelines. Other forms of pollution, such as noise, water and soil contamination, so-called urban heat islands, and a lack of space for walking, cycling and active living further combine to make cities epicentres of a noncommunicable disease epidemic and drivers of climate change. WHO Repository on Urban Health Impact: Around 40% of urban growth is in slums that lack safe water and sanitation, and 91% of people in urban areas breathe polluted air. When it comes to healthy diets, urbanization increases the distance from farm to fork, driving demand for unhealthy, processed foods.
translations.hypothesis: However, the vast majority of the world's 4.2 billion urban residents live in cities that lack adequate housing, sanitation, and transportation, and where air quality exceeds World Health Organization guidelines. Other forms of pollution, such as noise, water contamination, and soil degradation, as well as a lack of green spaces, cycling infrastructure, and pedestrian-friendly environments, contribute to making cities breeding grounds for disease and drivers of climate change. The WHO's Urban Health Repository highlights the impact: nearly 40% of urban growth occurs in slums lacking access to clean water and private sanitation, and 91% of urban dwellers are exposed to polluted air. Furthermore, urbanization increases the distance between food production and consumption, leading to a greater reliance on processed and unhealthy foods.

translations.source: Abantu abahlala emadolobheni basengozini enkulu yokushintsha kwesimo sezulu ngenxa yokuthembela kwabo ezintweni ezimbiwa phansi zokuthutha, ukupheka kanye nokushisisa. Amadolobhakazi athatha ngaphezu kwezingxenye ezimbili kwezintathu zamandla omhlaba futhi akhipha ama-60% amagesi abamba ukushisa, futhi lawo amaphakathi nezwe angase abe nezinga lokushisa elingu-3–5º C ngaphezu kwezindawo zasemaphandleni ezizungezile ngenxa yobukhulu bazo obukhonkolo kanye nezindawo eziluhlaza ezivulekile ezilinganiselwe. Konke lokhu kubeka impilo yabantu abahlala emadolobheni engcupheni. Iningi lezimbangela eziyishumi eziphezulu zokufa zihlobene eduze nokukhula ngokushesha nokungahleliwe kwedolobha, kanye nokuklanywa nokungahlelwa kahle kwamadolobha. Amadolobha abhekene nomthwalo wezempilo ophindwe kathathu wezifo ezithathelwanayo njengeHIV/AIDS, isifo sofuba, inyumoniya, udenga kanye nesifo sohudo;
translations.reference: Urban dwellers are also highly vulnerable to the effects of climate change because of their dependence on fossil fuels for transport, cooking and heating. Cities account for over two thirds of the world’s energy and emit 60% of greenhouse gases, and those inland may experience temperatures 3–5º C higher than surrounding rural areas because of their large expanses of concrete and limited open green spaces. All of this puts urban dwellers’ health at risk. Most the top 10 causes of death are closely related to rapid and unplanned urbanization, and poor urban design and planning. Cities face the triple health burden of infectious diseases like HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, pneumonia, dengue and diarrhoea;
translations.hypothesis: People living in urban areas are particularly vulnerable to the effects of climate change due to their reliance on fossil fuels for transportation, cooking, and heating. Cities consume over two-thirds of the world's energy and are responsible for 60% of greenhouse gas emissions. Furthermore, urban areas can be 3-5°C warmer than surrounding rural areas due to the urban heat island effect, caused by their dense construction and limited green spaces. All of this puts the health of urban residents at risk. Many of the top ten causes of death are linked to rapid and unplanned urban growth, as well as poor urban planning. Cities also face a disproportionate burden of infectious diseases such as HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, pneumonia, influenza, and diarrheal diseases.

translations.source: izifo ezingathathelaniyo ezifana nesifo senhliziyo, unhlangothi, isifuba somoya, umdlavuza, isifo sikashukela kanye nokudangala; futhi udlame nokulimala, okuhlanganisa nokulimala emgwaqeni. Impendulo yeWHO: Naphezu kwezinselele zawo, amadolobha angakha amathuba empilo engcono, indawo ehlanzekile kanye nokusebenza kwesimo sezulu. Izinqubomgomo eziqinile zasemadolobheni kufanele zibeke ezempilo kuqala, njengoba ibalulekile ekukhuthazeni impilo enhle yasemadolobheni, ukwakha abasebenzi abakhiqizayo, ukudala imiphakathi eqinile nenempilo, evumela ukuhamba, ukukhuthaza ukuxhumana nomphakathi kanye nokuvikela abantu abasengozini. Indlela yeWHO yezempilo yasemadolobheni igxile eqophelweni eliphezulu lomoya, amanzi kanye nokukhucululwa kwendle, kanye nezinye izici zemvelo; ukuhlela kahle kwedolobha; izindawo ezinempilo nezingabhemi; ukuhamba okuphephile nokunempilo; ukuvimbela udlame nokulimala; ukukhuthaza izinhlelo zokudla okunempilo nokudla okunempilo; ukuphathwa kwemvelo kwezifo ezithwalwa yizilwane; kanye nokulungela amadolobha ezimeni eziphuthumayo.
translations.reference: noncommunicable diseases like heart disease, stroke, asthma, cancer, diabetes and depression; and violence and injuries, including road traffic injuries. WHO response: Despite their challenges, cities can create opportunities for better health, cleaner environments and climate action. Strong urban policies must prioritize health, as it is essential for fostering good urban livelihoods, building a productive workforce, creating resilient and vibrant communities, enabling mobility, promoting social interaction and protecting vulnerable populations. WHO’s approach to urban health focuses on better air quality, water and sanitation, and other environmental determinants; healthy urban planning; healthier and smoke-free environments; safe and healthy mobility; preventing violence and injuries; promoting healthy food systems and diets; environmental management of vector-borne diseases; and urban preparedness for emergencies.
translations.hypothesis: Non-communicable diseases such as heart disease, stroke, respiratory illnesses, cancer, diabetes, and mental disorders; and violence and injuries, including road traffic accidents. WHO's response: Despite these challenges, cities can create opportunities for better health, a cleaner environment, and a more sustainable future. Strong urban policies should prioritize health, as it is essential for promoting healthy urban living, building a productive workforce, creating strong and healthy communities, enabling mobility, fostering social connections, and protecting vulnerable populations. The WHO's approach to urban health focuses on air quality, water and sanitation, and green spaces; urban planning; healthy and safe environments; safe and healthy mobility; preventing violence and injuries; promoting healthy diets and physical activity; managing zoonotic diseases; and preparing cities for emergencies.

translations.source: Ukusebenza kuyo yonke imikhakha kanye nokuqinisekisa ukuhambisana kwezinqubomgomo kuzo zonke izindawo ezihlukene kubalulekile ekudaleni izindawo ezisekelayo nezivumelayo zezempilo, okuqinisekisa ukuthi ukucatshangelwa kwezempilo nokulingana kuyahlanganiswa phakathi nenqubo yokuhlela, ukutshalwa kwezimali kanye nezinqumo zenqubomgomo ezingeni lendawo. Ukusiza Amazwe Angamalungu ukubhekana nalezi zinto ezibalulekile ezingenhla, iWHO isebenza ukuqinisa isisekelo sobufakazi ukuze isize abenzi bezinqubomgomo benze izinqumo ezinolwazi lapho bebhekana nezingozi zezempilo. Ihlinzeka ngamathuluzi nesiqondiso kulokho okusebenzayo, futhi isekela ukubhekwa kwezinkomba ezibalulekile ezihlobene nempilo. IWHO ihola futhi izibandakanye ekukhuthazeni ukuhwebelana kwedolobha nedolobha futhi isiza ukuthuthukisa izinhlaka zesikhungo nenqubomgomo yokuphatha okuhle kwedolobha kwezempilo nokuphila kahle emadolobheni.
translations.reference: Working across sectors and ensuring the coherence of policies across different areas is key to creating supportive and enabling environments for health, which ensures that health and equity considerations are integrated throughout the planning process, investments and policy decisions at the local level. To help Member States address the above priorities, WHO works to strengthen the evidence base to help policy-makers make informed decisions when addressing health risks. It provides tools and guidance on what works, and supports monitoring of key health-related indicators. WHO leads and engages in fostering city-to-city exchanges and helps develop institutional and policy frameworks for good urban governance for health and well-being in cities.
translations.hypothesis: Working across all sectors and ensuring policy coherence across different areas is crucial for creating supportive and enabling environments for health. This ensures that health considerations and equity are integrated throughout the planning, investment, and policy-making processes at the local level. To help member states address these important issues, the WHO is working to strengthen the evidence base to support policymakers in making informed decisions when addressing health risks. It provides tools and guidance on effective interventions, and supports the monitoring of key health-related indicators. The WHO also leads and participates in efforts to promote city-to-city collaboration and helps to develop frameworks for good governance of health and well-being in cities.

translations.source: Udlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni Uhlolojikelele: Udlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni luhlanganisa zonke izinhlobo zodlame olubhekiswe kubantu abangaphansi kweminyaka engu-18 ubudala. Ezinsaneni nasezinganeni ezincane, udlame luhilela ikakhulukazi ukuphathwa kabi kwezingane (okungukuthi, ukuhlukunyezwa ngokomzimba, ngokocansi nangokomzwelo kanye nokunganakwa) ezandleni zabazali nabanye abaphethe. Abafana namantombazane basengozini elinganayo yokuhlukunyezwa ngokomzimba nangokomzwelo nokunganakwa, futhi amantombazane asengozini enkulu yokuhlukunyezwa ngokocansi.
