Njengoba izingane zifinyelela eminyakeni yobusha, udlame lontanga nodlame olusondelene nozakwethu, ngaphezu kokuphathwa kabi kwezingane, kuyanda.
translations.reference: Violence against children Overview: Violence against children includes all forms of violence against people under 18 years old. For infants and younger children, violence mainly involves child maltreatment (i.e. physical, sexual and emotional abuse and neglect) at the hands of parents and other authority figures. Boys and girls are at equal risk of physical and emotional abuse and neglect, and girls are at greater risk of sexual abuse. As children reach adolescence, peer violence and intimate partner violence, in addition to child maltreatment, become highly prevalent.
translations.hypothesis: Child Maltreatment: Child maltreatment encompasses all forms of abuse against individuals under the age of 18. In children and adolescents, maltreatment primarily involves the mistreatment of children, including physical, sexual, and emotional abuse, as well as neglect, perpetrated by parents or other caregivers. Boys and girls are equally at risk of physical, emotional, and neglectful abuse, while girls are at a higher risk of sexual abuse. As children enter adolescence, peer violence and dating violence, in addition to child maltreatment, become more prevalent.

translations.source: Udlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni lungagwemeka. Ukuvimbela nokusabela odlameni olubhekiswe ezinganeni kudinga ukuthi imizamo ibhekane nengozi kanye nezici zokuzivikela kuwo wonke amazinga amane ahlobene obungozi (umuntu ngamunye, ubudlelwano, umphakathi, umphakathi). Isinqumo soMhlangano Womhlaba Wezempilo ka-Nhlaba 2016 sagunyaza uhlelo lokuqala lweWHO Lokusebenza Lomhlaba wonke lokuqinisa indima yohlelo lwezempilo ngaphakathi kwempendulo yemikhakha ehlukahlukene kazwelonke yokubhekana nodlame phakathi kwabantu, ikakhulukazi kwabesifazane namantombazane, kanye nezingane. A Ngokwalolu hlelo, iWHO ngokubambisana Namazwe Angamalungu kanye nabanye ababambisene nabo, izibophezele: Ukuqapha ubukhulu bomhlaba wonke kanye nezici zodlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni kanye nokusekela imizamo yezwe yokubhala kanye nokukala ubudlova obunjalo. Ukugcina isistimu yolwazi lwe-elekthronikhi efingqa idatha yesayensi yomthwalo, izici engcuphe kanye nemiphumela yodlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni, kanye nobufakazi bokuvikeleka kwalo.
translations.reference: Violence against children can be prevented. Preventing and responding to violence against children requires that efforts systematically address risk and protective factors at all four interrelated levels of risk (individual, relationship, community, society). May 2016 World Health Assembly resolution endorsed the first ever WHO Global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health system within a national multisectoral response to address interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls, and against children. A According to this plan, WHO in collaboration with Member States and other partners, is committed to: Monitoring the global magnitude and characteristics of violence against children and supporting country efforts to document and measure such violence. Maintaining an electronic information system that summarizes the scientific data on the burden, risk factors and consequences of violence against children, and the evidence for its preventability.
translations.hypothesis: Violence against children is a pervasive problem. Preventing and responding to violence against children requires efforts that address the risk factors and protective factors at all four levels related to violence (individual, relationship, community, and society). The resolution of the World Health Assembly in May 2016 endorsed the first global WHO framework for action to strengthen the role of health systems within a multi-sectoral national response to address interpersonal violence, particularly against women and girls, and against children. According to this framework, the WHO, in collaboration with member states and other partners, is committed to: Monitoring the global prevalence and characteristics of violence against children and supporting national efforts to document and measure such violence. Maintaining a robust electronic system to collect scientific data on the incidence, risk factors, and consequences of violence against children, as well as evidence of its prevention.

translations.source: Ukuthuthukisa nokusabalalisa imibhalo yeziqondiso zezobuchwepheshe ezisekelwe ebufakazini, izinkambiso nezindinganiso zokuvimbela kanye nokusabela kubudlova obubhekiswe ezinganeni. Ukushicilela njalo imibiko yesimo somhlaba ngemizamo yezwe yokubhekana nodlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni ngezinqubomgomo zikazwelonke nezinhlelo zokwenza, imithetho, izinhlelo zokuvimbela kanye nezinsizakalo zokuphendula. Amazwe asekelayo nabalingani ekusebenziseni amasu okuvimbela nokuphendula asekelwe ebufakazini, njengalawo afakwe ku-INSPIRE: Amasu ayisikhombisa okuqeda udlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni. Ukusebenzisana nama-ejensi nezinhlangano zamazwe ngamazwe ukunciphisa nokuqeda udlame olubhekiswe ezinganeni emhlabeni wonke, ngezinhlelo ezifana ne-Global Partnership Yokuqeda Udlame Olubhekiswe Kubantwana, IGlobal Partnership Ukuqeda Udlame Olubhekiswe Kubantwana,
translations.reference: Developing and disseminating evidence-based technical guidance documents, norms and standards for preventing and responding to violence against children. Regularly publishing global status reports on country efforts to address violence against children through national policies and action plans, laws, prevention programmes and response services. Supporting countries and partners in implementing evidence-based prevention and response strategies, such as those included in INSPIRE: Seven strategies for ending violence against children. Collaborating with international agencies and organizations to reduce and eliminate violence against children globally, through initiatives such as the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children, the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children,
translations.hypothesis: Developing and disseminating evidence-based technical guidelines, standards, and indicators for preventing and responding to violence against children. Regularly publishing reports on the global situation regarding national efforts to address violence against children, including policies, programs, legislation, prevention strategies, and response services. Supporting countries and partners in the use of evidence-based prevention and response strategies, such as those included in INSPIRE: A Framework for Protecting Children. Collaborating with UN agencies and international organizations to reduce and eliminate violence against children worldwide, through initiatives such as the Global Partnership to End Violence Against Children.

translations.source: Sihlangene Samantombazane kanye neViolence Prevention Alliance. Ukuphathwa kabi kwezingane Ukuhlukunyezwa kwezingane wukuhlukunyezwa nokunganakwa okwenzeka ezinganeni ezingaphansi kweminyaka eyi-18. Kuhlanganisa zonke izinhlobo zokuphathwa kabi ngokomzimba kanye/noma ngokomzwelo, ukuhlukunyezwa ngokocansi, ukunganakwa, ubudedengu nokuxhashazwa kwezentengiselwano noma okunye, okuholela ekulimaleni kwangempela noma okungaba khona empilweni yengane, ekusindeni kwayo, ekukhuleni kwayo noma ekuhloniphekeni kwayo esimweni sobudlelwano umthwalo wemfanelo, ukwethenjwa noma amandla. Ukuchayeka kudlame lozakwethu kwesinye isikhathi kufakwa njengendlela yokuphathwa kabi kwezingane.
translations.reference: Together for Girls and the Violence Prevention Alliance. Child maltreatment Child maltreatment is the abuse and neglect that occurs to children under 18 years of age. It includes all types of physical and/or emotional ill-treatment, sexual abuse, neglect, negligence and commercial or other exploitation, which results in actual or potential harm to the child’s health, survival, development or dignity in the context of a relationship of responsibility, trust or power. Exposure to intimate partner violence is also sometimes included as a form of child maltreatment.
translations.hypothesis: We are partnering with the Girl Scouts and the Violence Prevention Alliance. Child abuse is the mistreatment or neglect of a child under the age of 18. It includes all forms of physical, emotional, sexual, or psychological abuse, neglect, exploitation, and any other behavior that harms a child's well-being, development, or sense of self-worth. Domestic violence is sometimes considered a form of child abuse.

translations.source: Ukuphathwa kabi kwezingane kunemiphumela emikhulu esheshayo neyesikhathi eside. Ngaphandle kokufa, ukulimala ngokomzimba nokukhubazeka, udlame lungaholela ekucindezelekeni okuphazamisa ukukhula kobuchopho futhi kulimaze amasosha omzimba kanye nemizwa. Lokhu kubuye kuhlotshaniswe nokubambezeleka kokuthuthukiswa kwengqondo, ukungaphumeleli kahle kwesikole kanye nokuyeka isikole, izinkinga zempilo yengqondo, ukuzama ukuzibulala, ukwanda kokuziphatha okuyingozi empilweni, ukuvuselelwa kanye nokubhebhezelwa kodlame. Izindaba ezinhle ukuthi ukuhlukunyezwa kwezingane kungavinjelwa ngokungenelela okusekela abazali nabanakekeli, ukukhuthaza imikhuba nezindinganiso ezingenalo ubudlova, ukuhlinzeka ngemfundo nokuqeqeshwa kwamakhono okuphila, ukuqinisa iholo lemindeni kanye nokuvikeleka kwezomnotho, ukunikeza impendulo yezinga eliphezulu kanye nezinsizakalo zokusekela, ukudala. kanye nokusimamisa izindawo eziphephile zezingane, nokusebenzisa nokuphoqelela imithetho emelene nokuphathwa kabi kwezingane. IWHO, ngokubambisana nenani lozakwethu:
translations.reference: Child maltreatment has enormous immediate and long-term repercussions. Beyond death, physical injury and disability, violence can lead to stress that impairs brain development and damages the nervous and immune systems. This in turn is associated with delayed cognitive development, poor school performance and dropout, mental health problems, suicide attempts, increased health-risk behaviours, revictimization and the perpetration of violence. The good news is that child maltreatment can be prevented through interventions that support parents and caregivers, promote non-violent norms and values, provide education and life skills training, strengthen families’ income and economic security, offer high quality response and support services, create and sustain safe environments for children, and implement and enforce laws against child maltreatment. WHO, in collaboration with a number of partners:
translations.hypothesis: Child maltreatment has significant and far-reaching consequences, both in the short and long term. Beyond physical harm and disability, abuse can lead to psychological distress that impairs cognitive development and damages the body's immune system. It is also linked to delays in intellectual development, poor academic performance and school dropout, mental health problems, suicidal ideation, increased risk of risky behaviors, substance abuse, and a perpetuation of violence. The good news is that child maltreatment can be prevented through supportive interventions for parents and caregivers, promoting non-violent attitudes and behaviors, providing education and life skills training, strengthening family support systems and economic security, offering high-quality responses and support services, creating and maintaining safe environments for children, and enacting and enforcing laws against child maltreatment. The WHO, in partnership with its partners:

translations.source: inikeza isiqondiso sobuchwepheshe nesivamile sokuvimbela ukuhlukunyezwa kwezingane okusekelwe ebufakazini; ikhuthaza ukwesekwa kwamazwe ngamazwe kanye nokutshalwa kwezimali ekuvimbeleni ukuhlukunyezwa kwezingane okusekelwe ebufakazini; inikeza ukwesekwa kwezobuchwepheshe kwezinhlelo zokuvimbela ukuphathwa kabi kwezingane ezisekelwe ebufakazini emazweni amaningana anemali engenayo ephansi naphakathi. Udlame lwentsha Udlame lwentsha luyinkinga yezempilo yomphakathi emhlabeni jikelele.
translations.reference: provides technical and normative guidance for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; advocates for increased international support for and investment in evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; provides technical support for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention programmes in several low- and middle-income countries. Youth violence Youth violence is a global public health problem.
translations.hypothesis:
It provides professional and practical guidance on preventing child abuse based on evidence; it promotes international cooperation and investment in evidence-based child protection; and it offers technical support to child protection systems in low- and middle-income countries.

Youth violence is a global public health problem.

translations.source: Kubandakanya uhla lwezenzo kusukela ebuxhwanguxhwangwini kanye nokulwa ngokomzimba, ukuya ekuhlaselweni kanzima ngokocansi nangokomzimba kuya ekubulaleni. Udlame lwentsha lunyusa kakhulu izindleko zezempilo, inhlalakahle kanye nobulungiswa bobugebengu; kunciphisa ukukhiqiza; futhi ngokuvamile ibukela phansi isisekelo somphakathi. Ngaphandle kokufa, ukulimala kanye nokulimala kwengqondo, udlame lwentsha lungaholela ekwandeni kokuziphatha okuyingozi kwezempilo njengokubhema, ukusebenzisa kabi izidakamizwa, ucansi olungaphephile, kanye nobudlova obengeziwe. Lokhu-ke kuhlotshaniswa nezifo ezingelapheki zokuphefumula, umdlavuza, izifo zenhliziyo, isifo sikashukela, ukukhulelwa besebancane kanye nezifo ezithathelwana ngocansi. Amasu asebenzayo okuvimbela kanye nokusabela ahlanganisa lawo akhuthaza ukuba umzali kanye nokuthuthukiswa kwengane isencane;
translations.reference: It includes a range of acts from bullying and physical fighting, to more severe sexual and physical assault to homicide. Youth violence dramatically increases health, welfare and criminal justice costs; reduces productivity; and generally undermines the fabric of society. Beyond deaths, injuries and psychological harm, youth violence can lead to increased health risk behaviours such as smoking, substance abuse, unsafe sex, and further violence. These in turn are associated with chronic respiratory diseases, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, early pregnancy and sexually transmitted diseases. Effective prevention and response strategies include those that promote parenting and early childhood development;
translations.hypothesis: It encompasses a range of behaviors, from petty theft and physical fights to serious sexual and physical assault, and even murder. Youth violence significantly increases healthcare costs, social welfare burdens, and crime rates; it reduces productivity; and it often undermines the social fabric. Beyond death, injury, and mental trauma, youth violence can lead to an increase in risky health behaviors such as smoking, substance abuse, unsafe sex, and increased rates of obesity. This, in turn, is associated with preventable chronic diseases like respiratory illnesses, cancer, heart disease, diabetes, teenage pregnancy, and sexually transmitted infections. Effective prevention and response strategies include those that promote positive parenting and early childhood development.

translations.source: izinhlelo zokuvimbela ubuxhwanguxhwangu ezikoleni, ezifundweni, kanye nezokuthuthukisa amakhono empilo nezenhlalo; izindlela zokwelapha ezinabantu abasengozini enkulu, kanye nezindlela ezisezingeni lomphakathi kanye nomphakathi ezifana nokunciphisa ukufinyelela kanye nokusetshenziswa budedengu kotshwala nezibhamu, izindawo ezishisayo kanye namaphoyisa agxile ezinkingeni, ukuthuthukisa amadolobha, kanye nokuqeda ubumpofu. IWHO kanye nozakwethu banciphisa udlame lwentsha ngezinyathelo ezisiza ukuhlonza, ukulinganisa nokuphendula inkinga, lokhu kufaka phakathi: ukwakha iphakethe lezinhlelo zokuvimbela udlame ezikoleni; idonsela ukunaka ubukhulu bobudlova bentsha kanye nesidingo sokuvimbela;
translations.reference: school-based bullying prevention, academic, and life and social skills development programmes; therapeutic approaches with high-risk individuals, and community- and society-level approaches like reducing access to and misuse of alcohol and firearms, hotspots and problem-oriented policing, urban upgrading, and poverty de-concentration. WHO and partners decrease youth violence through initiatives that help to identify, quantify and respond to the problem, these include: developing a package for schools-based violence prevention programmes; drawing attention to the magnitude of youth violence and the need for prevention;
translations.hypothesis: School-based, community-based, and health and social development programs; interventions targeting high-risk individuals, as well as community-level approaches such as reducing access to and misuse of alcohol and firearms, targeted policing of crime hotspots, urban development, and poverty reduction. WHO and its partners are working to reduce youth violence through measures that promote prevention, address the root causes, and respond effectively, including: developing a package of violence prevention programs for schools; raising awareness of the extent of youth violence and the need for prevention;

translations.source: ukwakha ubufakazi kububanzi nezinhlobo zodlame ezindaweni ezahlukene; ukuthuthukisa isiqondiso saMazwe Angamalungu kanye nayo yonke imikhakha efanele ukuvimbela udlame lwentsha nokuqinisa izimpendulo kulo; ukusekela imizamo kazwelonke yokuvimbela udlame lwentsha; futhi ukusebenzisana nezinhlangano zamazwe ngamazwe kanye nezinhlangano ukuvimbela udlame lwentsha emhlabeni jikelele.
translations.reference: building evidence on the scope and types of violence in different settings; developing guidance for Member States and all relevant sectors to prevent youth violence and strengthen responses to it; supporting national efforts to prevent youth violence; and collaborating with international agencies and organizations to prevent youth violence globally.
translations.hypothesis: Building evidence on the scope and nature of violence in different contexts; strengthening national policies and all relevant sectors to prevent youth violence and improve responses to it; supporting national efforts to prevent youth violence; and collaborating with international organizations and partners to prevent youth violence globally.

translations.source: Udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane Uhlolojikelele: Udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane - ikakhulukazi udlame olusondelene nabalingani kanye nodlame locansi - yinkinga enkulu yezempilo yomphakathi neyezempilo kanye nokwephulwa kwamalungelo abantu besifazane. Isekelwe futhi iqhubekisela phambili ukungalingani kobulili. Emhlabeni wonke owesifazane oyedwa kwabathathu uba nodlame olungokomzimba kanye/noma locansi ekuphileni kwabo, kakhulukazi okwenziwa umlingani osondelene naye. Lesi yisikhumbuzo esiqinile sezinga lokungalingani ngokobulili kanye nokucwaswa kwabantu besifazane. I-United Nations ichaza udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane “njenganoma yisiphi isenzo sodlame olusekelwe ebulilini esiholela, noma esingase sibangele, ukulimala ngokomzimba, ngokocansi, noma kwengqondo noma ukuhlupheka kwabesifazane, okuhlanganisa izinsongo zezenzo ezinjalo, ukuphoqelelwa noma ukuncishwa okuthile ngokungafanele. inkululeko, kungakhathaliseki ukuthi yenzeka emphakathini noma empilweni yangasese.
translations.reference: Violence against women Overview: Violence against women – particularly intimate partner violence and sexual violence – is a major public and clinical health problem and a violation of women's human rights. It is rooted in and perpetuates gender inequalities.Globally 1 in 3 women experience physical and/or sexual violence in their lifetime, mostly by an intimate partner. This is a stark reminder of the scale of gender inequality and discrimination against women. The United Nations defines violence against women as "any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in, physical, sexual, or mental harm or suffering to women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private life.
translations.hypothesis: Violence against women: Overview: Violence against women – particularly intimate partner violence and sexual violence – is a major public health and human rights issue. It is rooted in and perpetuates gender inequality. Globally, one in three women experience physical and/or sexual violence in their lifetime, most often at the hands of an intimate partner. This is a stark reminder of the extent of gender inequality and the vulnerability of women. The United Nations defines violence against women as "any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in, physical, sexual, or psychological harm or suffering to women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private."

translations.source: Udlame lozakwethu abasondelene lusho ukuziphatha komlingani osondelene naye noma owake wathandana naye okubangela ukulimala ngokomzimba, ngokocansi noma ngokwengqondo, okuhlanganisa ulaka olungokomzimba, ukucindezelwa ngokocansi, ukuhlukumeza ngokomqondo kanye nokuziphatha okulawulayo. Udlame locansi "yinoma yisiphi isenzo socansi, umzamo wokuthola isenzo socansi, noma esinye isenzo esiqondiswe ngokumelene nobulili bomuntu kusetshenziswa ukuphoqwa, yinoma yimuphi umuntu ngokunganaki ubudlelwano bakhe nomuntu ohlukunyeziwe, kunoma yisiphi isimo. Kuhlanganisa ukudlwengulwa, okuchazwa njengokungena ngenkani ngokomzimba noma ngenye indlela kwengquza noma ididi ngepipi, enye ingxenye yomzimba noma into." Umthelela: Udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane luyinkinga yomhlaba wonke yokwanda kobhubhane. Kudala umonakalo, ukulimala ezimpilweni zabesifazane kanye nezabantwana babo.
translations.reference: Intimate partner violence refers to behaviour by an intimate partner or ex-partner that causes physical, sexual or psychological harm, including physical aggression, sexual coercion, psychological abuse and controlling behaviours. Sexual violence is "any sexual act, attempt to obtain a sexual act, or other act directed against a person’s sexuality using coercion, by any person regardless of their relationship to the victim, in any setting. It includes rape, defined as the physically forced or otherwise coerced penetration of the vulva or anus with a penis, other body part or object." Impact: Violence against women is a global problem of pandemic proportions. It causes devastating, harm to women’s lives and that of their children.
translations.hypothesis: Intimate partner violence refers to abusive behavior exhibited by someone in a close relationship or former relationship that causes physical, sexual, or emotional harm. This can include physical assault, sexual coercion, emotional abuse, and controlling behavior. Sexual violence is defined as "any sexual act that occurs without the explicit consent of the other person, including unwanted touching, rape, and sexual coercion." Impact: Violence against women is a global public health problem. It causes significant harm, injury, and health problems for women and their children.

translations.source: Kulimaza futhi impilo yezomnotho nenhlalo yemindeni yabo, imiphakathi, kanye namazwe. Nakuba abanye besifazane besengozini kakhulu kunabanye, udlame lungenzeka kunoma yimuphi umuntu wesifazane, kunoma yiliphi izwe - kungakhathaliseki isiko, inkolo noma isimo somnotho. Ukungalingani ngokobulili okuhlanganisa nezinkambiso zobulili eziyingozi yizona ezihamba phambili zodlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane. Udlame lungaphazamisa impilo yabesifazane ngokomzimba, ngokwengqondo, ocansini, kanye nokuzala. Kuhambisana nokwanda kwengozi yokulimala, ukudangala, ukuphazamiseka kokukhathazeka, ukukhulelwa okungahleliwe, izifo ezithathelwana ngocansi, iHIV nezinye izinkinga eziningi zempilo.
translations.reference: It also hurts the economic and social health of their families, communities, and countries. While some women are more at risk than others, violence can happen to any woman, in any country – regardless of culture, religion or economic status. Gender inequality including harmful gender norms are key drivers of violence against women. Violence can negatively affect women’s physical, mental, sexual, and reproductive health. It is associated with increased risk of injuries, depression, anxiety disorders, unplanned pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, HIV and many other health problems.
translations.hypothesis: It damages the health, economic well-being, and social lives of women, their families, communities, and nations. While some women are more vulnerable than others, violence can affect any woman, in any country – regardless of culture, religion, or socioeconomic status. Gender inequality, including harmful gender norms, is a major driver of violence against women. Violence can have a devastating impact on women's physical, mental, emotional, and reproductive health. It is associated with an increased risk of injury, disability, anxiety, unwanted pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, HIV, and many other health problems.

translations.source: Izingane ezikhulela emikhayeni enobudlova zingase zibe nokuphazamiseka okuhlukahlukene kokuziphatha nemizwelo. Lokhu kungaholela ezinkingeni zengqondo nokukhula futhi kuhlotshaniswe nokwenza noma ukubhekana nodlame kamuva ekuphileni. Impendulo yeWHO: iWHO idlala indima ebalulekile ekuletheni ukunaka nokuphendula udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane njengendaba yezempilo yomphakathi kanye nokulingana kobulili ngokusebenzisa: Ucwaningo nokwakhiwa kobufakazi ukuze kugqanyiswe ubukhulu bodlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane, izici zalo eziyingozi kanye nemiphumela, kanye nokuhlonza ukungenelela okuphumelelayo kokuvimbela nokusabela. Ukuthuthukisa imihlahlandlela namathuluzi, ukubeka izimiso nezindinganiso zokuphendula ngempumelelo kwezempilo kubudlova obubhekiswe kwabesifazane.
translations.reference: Children who grow up in families where there is violence may suffer a range of behavioural and emotional disturbances. These can lead to mental and development problems and also be associated with perpetrating or experiencing violence later in life. WHO response: WHO plays a key role in bringing attention to and responding to violenceagainst women as a public health and gender equality issue through: Research and evidence-building to highlight the magnitude of violence against women, its risk factors and consequences, and to identify effective interventions for prevention and response. Developing guidelines and tools, setting norms and standards for an effective health response to violence against women.
translations.hypothesis: Children who grow up in abusive households may experience a variety of behavioral and emotional problems. This can lead to mental health issues and developmental difficulties, and can also be associated with perpetrating or experiencing violence later in life. The WHO's response: The WHO plays a crucial role in raising awareness and addressing violence against women as a public health and gender equality issue, through: Research and evidence-building to understand the extent, nature, risk factors, and consequences of violence against women, and to identify effective prevention and response strategies. Developing guidelines and tools, and setting standards for effective health sector responses to violence against women.

translations.source: Ukuqinisa amandla ezwe wezinhlelo zezempilo nabahlinzeki ukuze kuvinjelwe futhi kuphendule udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane. Ukukhuthaza ubuholi ezinhlelweni zezempilo kanye nokwakha intando yezepolitiki yokubhekana nodlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane ngokukhulumela nokusebenzisana. iWHO 13th General Programme of Work (GPW) ihlanganisa udlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane njengengxenye yomnikelo walo kuSustainable Development Goal (SDG) 5 mayelana nokulingana kobulili kanye nokufukulwa kwabesifazane (ikakhulukazi, SDG 5.2 mayelana nokuqedwa kodlame olubhekiswe kwabesifazane namantombazane). Emhlanganweni Wezempilo Womhlaba ngoNhlaba 2016, Amazwe Angamalungu agunyaza uhlelo lokusebenza lomhlaba wonke lokuqinisa indima yezinhlelo zezempilo ekubhekaneni nodlame phakathi kwabantu, ikakhulukazi abesifazane namantombazane kanye nezingane.
translations.reference: Strengthening country capacity of health systems and providers to prevent and respond to violence against women. Encouraging leadership in health systems and building political will to address violence against women through advocacy and partnerships. The WHO 13th General Programme of Work (GPW) includes violence against women as part of its contribution to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 5 on gender equality and women’s empowerment (specifically, SDG 5.2 on the elimination of violence against women and girls). At the World Health Assembly in May 2016, Member States endorsed a global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health systems in addressing interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls and against children.
translations.hypothesis: Strengthening the capacity of health systems and healthcare providers to prevent and respond to violence against women. Promoting leadership within health systems and building political will to address violence against women through advocacy and collaboration. The WHO's 13th General Programme of Work (GPW) includes violence against women as part of its contribution to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 5 on gender equality and women's empowerment (specifically, SDG 5.2 on ending all forms of violence against women and girls). At the World Health Assembly in May 2016, Member States endorsed a global plan of action to strengthen the role of health systems in addressing violence against people, particularly women and girls and children.

translations.source: Imililo yequbula Uhlolojikelele: Imililo yequbula iyanda emhlabeni wonke ngobuningi, ubukhali kanye nobude besikhathi, ikhulisa isidingo sokuqonda imiphumela yezempilo yokuchayeka emlilweni wequbula. Ingozi yomlilo wequbula ikhula ezimeni ezome kakhulu, njengesomiso, amagagasi okushisa nangesikhathi somoya onamandla.Intuthu yomlilo wequbula iyingxube yezinto ezingcolisa umoya eziyingozi, ezifana nePM2.5, NO2, i-ozone, amahydrocarbon anephunga elimnandi, noma umthofu. Ngaphezu kokungcolisa umoya ngezinto ezingcolisayo ezinobuthi, imililo yequbula iphinde ithinte isimo sezulu ngasikhathi sinye ngokukhiphela inqwaba yesikhutha namanye amagesi abamba ukushisa emkhathini. Ngokushintsha kwesimo sezulu okuholela emazingeni okushisa afudumele nezimo ezomile kanye nokwanda kokukhula kwezindawo zasemadolobheni, inkathi yomlilo iqala ngaphambi kwesikhathi futhi iphele kamuva.
translations.reference: Wildfires Overview: Wildfires are increasing around the globe in frequency, severity and duration, heightening the need to understand the health effects of wildfire exposure. The risk of wildfires grows in extremely dry conditions, such as drought, heat waves and during high winds.Wildfire smoke is a mixture of hazardous air pollutants, such PM2.5, NO2, ozone, aromatic hydrocarbons, or lead. In addition to contaminating the air with toxic pollutants, wildfires also simultaneously impact the climate by releasing large quantities of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. With climate change leading to warmer temperatures and drier conditions and the increasing urbanization of rural areas, the fire season is starting earlier and ending later.
translations.hypothesis: Wildfires: Wildfires are increasing globally in terms of frequency, intensity, and duration, highlighting the need to understand the health impacts of wildfire exposure. The risk of wildfires is growing, particularly in hotter, drier conditions and during periods of strong winds. Wildfire smoke is a complex mixture of hazardous air pollutants, including PM2.5, NO2, ozone, volatile organic compounds, and carbon monoxide. In addition to air pollution from these harmful pollutants, wildfires also impact the climate by releasing large amounts of greenhouse gases and other heat-trapping gases into the atmosphere. Due to climate change, which is leading to higher temperatures, drier conditions, and increased urbanization, the wildfire season is starting earlier and lasting longer.

translations.source: Imicimbi yomlilo wequbula iba ngokwedlulele ngokwedlulele mayelana nokushiswa kwamahektare, ubude besikhathi namandla, futhi ingaphazamisa ezokuthutha, ukuxhumana, ukuhlinzekwa kwamanzi, namandla kanye nezinsizakalo zegesi. Umthelela: Imililo yequbula evutha eduze kwezindawo ezinabantu abaningi ingaba nomthelela omkhulu endaweni ezungezile, impahla, imfuyo kanye nokufa kwabantu kanye nezifo kuye ngobukhulu, isivinini kanye nokuba seduze komlilo, kanye nokuthi ingabe abantu banesexwayiso esithuthukisiwe sokuthi baphume. Intuthu yomlilo wequbula iyingxube yezinto ezingcolisa umoya okuyiparticulate matter (PM) ewusongo olukhulu lwezempilo yomphakathi. iPM2.5 evela kuntuthu womlilo wequbula ihlotshaniswa nokufa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kubantu abaningi, futhi ingabangela futhi yandise izifo zamaphaphu, inhliziyo, ubuchopho/isimiso sezinzwa, isikhumba, amathumbu, izinso, amehlo, ikhala nesibindi. Kubuye kwaboniswa ukuthi kuholela ekukhubazekeni kwengqondo nokulahlekelwa inkumbulo.
translations.reference: Wildfire events are getting more extreme in terms of acres burned, duration and intensity, and they can disrupt transportation, communications, water supply, and power and gas services. Impact: Wildfires that burn near populated areas can have significant impact on the environment, property, livestock and human mortality and morbidity depending on the size, speed and proximity to the fire, and whether the population has advanced warning to evacuate. Wildfire smoke is a mixture of air pollutants of which particulate matter (PM) is the principal public health threat. PM2.5 from wildfire smoke is associated with premature deaths in the general population, and can cause and exacerbate diseases of the lungs, heart, brain/nervous system, skin, gut, kidney, eyes, nose and liver. It has also been shown to lead to cognitive impairment and memory loss.
translations.hypothesis: Wildfires are characterized by their rapid spread, extensive area coverage, and intensity, and they can disrupt transportation, communication, water and power supplies, and gas services. Impact: Wildfires that occur near populated areas can have a significant impact on the surrounding environment, property, and livestock, and can also result in human fatalities and illnesses. The severity, speed, and proximity of the fire, as well as the level of warning and evacuation preparedness, all contribute to the extent of the impact. Smoke from wildfires is a complex mixture of air pollutants, with particulate matter (PM) being a major concern for public health. PM2.5, a component of wildfire smoke, has been linked to premature death in many people, and can also exacerbate respiratory, cardiovascular, neurological, skin, gastrointestinal, kidney, eye, and liver diseases. It has also been shown to contribute to mental health problems and memory loss.

translations.source: Izicishamlilo kanye nabasebenzi bezimo eziphuthumayo nabo bathintwa kakhulu ukulimala, ukusha nokuhogela intuthu, ikakhulukazi ezindaweni ezigxile kakhulu. Ucwaningo olwengeziwe lwezinhlaka ezahlukene lufanelekile ukuze kuqondwe imiphumela yezempilo ecashile neyesikhathi eside yokuchayeka komlilo wequbula kubantu abasengozini (izingane, abantu abadala, abantu abakhulelwe, abantu abagula ngokungalapheki), ikakhulukazi ezindaweni ezibekezelela ukuchayeka kaningi kanye nokujikeleza kule micimbi yomlilo wasendle. Impendulo yeWHO: Ubukhulu bezindleko ezingokwenyama nezomuntu ezivela emlilweni wequbula zingancishiswa uma ukuvimbela okwanele kwezimo eziphuthumayo, ukulungela, ukusabela kanye nezinyathelo zokuthola kabusha zenziwa ngendlela eqhubekayo nangesikhathi esifanele. Ukusebenzisana Namazwe Angamalungu, iWHO isiza ekwakheni izinhlelo zezempilo eziqinile nezisebenzayo ezingase zilindele izidingo nezinselele ngesikhathi sezimo eziphuthumayo, ukuze sikwazi ukunciphisa izingozi futhi siphendule ngokuphumelelayo lapho kudingeka. IWHO isebenza nabalingani ekulungiseleleni, ekuvimbeleni, ekutholeni, ekuphenduleni nasekululameni ezimeni eziphuthumayo nezinhlekelele, okuhlanganisa ukuchayeka kwemvelo, njengokwenza kube lula izinhlelo zokuxwayisa kusenesikhathi kanye nokukhipha izeluleko zezempilo nezomoya;
translations.reference: Firefighters and emergency response workers are also greatly impacted by injuries, burns and smoke inhalation, particularly at high concentrations. More interdisciplinary research is warranted to understand the latent and long-term health effects of wildfire exposure on vulnerable populations (children, older people, pregnant people, chronically ill people), particularly for geographic areas enduring repeated and cyclical exposure to these wildﬁre events. WHO Response: The magnitude of the physical and human costs from wildfires can be reduced if adequate emergency prevention, preparedness, response and recovery measures are implemented in a sustainable and timely manner. Working with Member States, WHO helps to build resilient and proactive health systems that can anticipate the needs and challenges during emergencies, so that we can be more likely to reduce risks and respond effectively when needed. WHO works with partners in preparing, preventing, detecting, responding and recovering from emergencies and disasters, including environmental exposures, such asimplementing early warning systems and issuing health and air quality advisories;
translations.hypothesis: Firefighters and emergency responders are particularly vulnerable to injuries, burns, and exposure to toxic fumes, especially in areas with intense fires. Further research into different demographics is warranted to understand the long-term and short-term health impacts of wildfire exposure on vulnerable populations (children, the elderly, pregnant women, and individuals with pre-existing conditions), particularly in areas prone to frequent wildfires and natural disasters. WHO's response: The significant physical and psychological costs associated with wildfires can be reduced if effective disaster prevention, preparedness, response, and recovery measures are implemented consistently and in a timely manner. In collaboration with member states, the WHO helps to build robust and effective health systems that can anticipate and address the needs and challenges during emergencies, enabling us to reduce risks and respond effectively when necessary. The WHO works with partners in preparing for, preventing, responding to, and recovering from emergencies and disasters, including natural hazards, by facilitating early warning systems and disseminating public health and safety information.

translations.source: equkethe ukukhululwa kwezinto eziyingozi; ukuhlola izidingo zezempilo zomphakathi kanye nokulimala kwengqalasizinda; ukubuyisela izinsiza zokunakekelwa okuyisisekelo, njengokugoma, impilo yezingane kanye nomama, kanye nempilo yengqondo; ukuqinisa izinsiza zabantu ekulawuleni izinhlekelele; ukuqoqa, ukuhlaziya nokusabalalisa ulwazi oluhlobene nezimo eziphuthumayo nezinhlekelele okungenzeka zenzeke esifundeni; ukusungula nokuphatha isitokwe sezimpahla zosizo kanye nemishini;
translations.reference: containing the release of hazardous materials; assessing health needs of the community and infrastructure damage; restoring primary care services, like immunization, child and maternal health, and mental health; strengthening human resources for disaster management; collecting, analysing and disseminating information related to emergencies and disasters that are likely to occur in a region; establishing and managing stocks of relief supplies and equipment;
translations.hypothesis: It includes the release of hazardous materials; assessing public health needs and infrastructure damage; restoring essential services such as sanitation, maternal and child health, and mental health; strengthening community resources for disaster management; collecting, analyzing, and disseminating information related to potential emergencies and disasters in the region; establishing and managing a stockpile of relief supplies and equipment.

translations.source: ukubiza uxhaso oluphuthumayo ukuze kusekelwe isenzo sezempilo; futhi ukuthuthukisa izinqubomgomo zikazwelonke, izincomo kanye nezinhlelo zikazwelonke zokubhekana nezimo eziphuthumayo.
translations.reference: calling for emergency funding to support health action; and developing national policies, recommendations and national emergency response plans.
translations.hypothesis: to mobilize immediate support for health interventions; and to develop national policies, guidelines, and strategies for addressing health emergencies.

translations.source: Impilo yabesifazane Uhlolojikelele: Ukuba owesilisa noma owesifazane kunomthelela omkhulu empilweni, ngenxa yakho kokubili umehluko webhayoloji nohlobene nobulili. Impilo yabesifazane namantombazane ikhathaza kakhulu ngoba, emiphakathini eminingi, bancishwe amathuba ngenxa yokucwaswa okubangelwa izici zezenhlalo namasiko. Isibonelo, abesifazane namantombazane babhekene nokwanda kobungozi beHIV/AIDS. Ezinye zezici zezenhlalo namasiko ezivimbela abesifazane namantombazane ukuthi bazuze ezinsizeni zezempilo ezisezingeni eliphezulu futhi bathole izinga lezempilo elingcono kakhulu zifaka phakathi:
translations.reference: Women's health Overview: Being a man or a woman has a significant impact on health, as a result of both biological and gender-related differences. The health of women and girls is of particular concern because, in many societies, they are disadvantaged by discrimination rooted in sociocultural factors. For example, women and girls face increased vulnerability to HIV/AIDS. Some of the sociocultural factors that prevent women and girls to benefit from quality health services and attaining the best possible level of health include:
translations.hypothesis: Women's Health: Overview: Gender plays a significant role in health, due to both biological differences and societal factors. Women's and girls' health is particularly concerning because, in many societies, they face reduced opportunities due to discrimination based on gender. For example, women and girls are disproportionately affected by the HIV/AIDS epidemic. Some of the social and cultural factors that prevent women and girls from accessing quality healthcare and achieving optimal health include:

translations.source: ukungalingani kwamandla ubudlelwano phakathi kwabesilisa nabesifazane; izinkambiso zomphakathi ezehlisa imfundo kanye namathuba omsebenzi akhokhelwayo; ukugxila okukhethekile ezindimeni zokuzala zabesifazane; futhi okungenzeka noma okuhlangenwe nakho kwangempela kodlame olungokomzimba, locansi kanye nolwemizwa. Nakuba ubumpofu buyisithiyo esibalulekile emiphumeleni emihle yezempilo kubo bobabili abesilisa nabesifazane, ubumpofu buvame ukuletha umthwalo omkhulu empilweni yabesifazane namantombazane ngenxa, isibonelo, imikhuba yokudla (ukungondleki) kanye nokusetshenziswa kwezibaseli zokupheka ezingaphephile (COPD).
translations.reference: unequal power relationships between men and women; social norms that decrease education and paid employment opportunities; an exclusive focus on women’s reproductive roles; and potential or actual experience of physical, sexual and emotional violence. While poverty is an important barrier to positive health outcomes for both men and women, poverty tends to yield a higher burden on women and girls’ health due to, for example, feeding practices (malnutrition) and use of unsafe cooking fuels (COPD).
translations.hypothesis: Inequality in power dynamics between men and women; social norms that limit women's education and access to paid employment; a focus on women's reproductive roles; and the potential or actual experience of physical, sexual, and emotional violence. While poverty is a significant factor in negative health outcomes for both men and women, poverty often places a disproportionate burden on the lives of women and girls, for example, due to practices like malnutrition and the use of unsafe cooking fuels (leading to COPD).

translations.source: Izikhala (Endemic treponematoses) Uhlolojikelele: Izadla yisifo sesikhumba esingalapheki esibonakala ngamapapillomas (izigaxa ezingenawo umdlavuza) kanye nezilonda. Kubangelwa i-Bacterium Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue, eyingxenye yeqembu elifanayo lamabhaktheriya abangela i-venereal syphilis. Izikhala zithinta kakhulu izingane ezineminyaka engaphansi kwe-15 ezihlala emiphakathini empofu ezindaweni ezifudumele, ezinomswakama kanye namahlathi ashisayo e-Afrika, e-Asia, eLatin America naseziqhingini zasePacific. Iningi labantu abathintekile lihlala ezindaweni zasemakhaya, kude nezinsiza zezempilo.
translations.reference: Yaws (Endemic treponematoses) Overview: Yaws is a chronic skin infection characterized by papillomas (noncancerous lumps) and ulcers. It is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue, which belongs to the same group of bacteria that causes venereal syphilis. Yaws primarily affects children aged under 15 years who live in poor communities in warm, humid and tropical forested areas of Africa, Asia, Latin America and the Pacific islands. The majority of affected populations live rural areas, far from health services.
translations.hypothesis: Endemic Treponematoses (Yaws): Overview: Yaws is a non-venereal skin disease characterized by papillomas (wart-like growths) and ulcers. It is caused by the bacterium *Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue*, which is related to the bacteria that cause venereal syphilis. Yaws primarily affects children under the age of 15 who live in impoverished communities in tropical, humid, and forested areas of Africa, Asia, Latin America, and the Pacific Islands. Many of those affected live in rural areas with limited access to healthcare.

translations.source: Ubumpofu, izimo zenhlalomnotho eziphansi kanye nenhlanzeko yomuntu siqu kusiza ukusabalala kwamaza. Yize kunezigameko ezingaphezu kuka-80 000 zokuzamula unyaka nonyaka, ongoti bakholelwa ukuthi lesi sifo singalawuleka futhi sigcine siqedwe ngenxa yezizathu eziningi. Okokuqala, kwenzeka kuphela kubantu, hhayi izilwane, okwenza ukulawula kube lula kakhulu. Kulula futhi ukuyelapha ngemithi etholakala kalula futhi isivele yaqedwa kwamanye amazwe, kuhlanganise nelaseNdiya. Amaphakethe asele okutheleleka nge-yaws, nakuba ngokuvamile ezindaweni ezikude, kusho ukuthi ukusakazeka okwengeziwe mancane amathuba okuba nezinyathelo ezifanele zokuqapha nokulawula.
translations.reference: Poverty, low socio-economic conditions and poor personal hygiene facilitate the spread of yaws. Although there are over 80 000 cases of yaws each year, experts believe the disease can be controlled and ultimately eradicated for several reasons. First, it only occurs in humans, not animals, making control much easier. It is also easy to treat with readily available drugs and has already been eliminated in some countries, including India. The remaining pockets of yaws infection, although usually in remote places, means further spread is less likely with proper surveillance and control measures.
translations.hypothesis: Poverty, poor socioeconomic conditions, and inadequate personal hygiene contribute to the spread of yaws. While there are over 80,000 cases of yaws reported annually, experts believe that this disease can be controlled and eventually eradicated due to several factors. Firstly, it only affects humans, not animals, which makes control efforts easier. Secondly, it is treatable with readily available medications that have already been successful in eliminating the disease in other countries, including India. The relatively small number of remaining cases, even in remote areas, suggests that further spread is unlikely if appropriate surveillance and control measures are implemented.

translations.source: Izimpawu: Kunezigaba ezimbili zokutheleleka nge-yaws. Isigaba sokuqala yilapho isiguli sithelela khona futhi singasabalalisa lesi sifo kwabanye. Kulesi sigaba, i-papule (iqhubu elingenawo umdlavuza, elikhula ngaphandle) liyakhula endaweni yegciwane. Le papule igcwele izinto eziphilayo futhi ingase iqhubeke izinyanga ezingu-3-6 kulandelwa ukuphulukiswa kwemvelo. Ngaphandle kokwelashwa, lokhu kulandelwa izilonda zesikhumba ezisatshalaliswa emzimbeni.
translations.reference: Symptoms: There are two stages of yaws infection. The early stage is when the patient is infectious and can spread the disease to others. In this stage, a papule (a noncancerous, outward-growing lump) develops at the site of infection. This papule is full of the organisms and may persist for 3–6 months followed by natural healing. Without treatment, this is followed by disseminated skin lesions over the body.
translations.hypothesis: Symptoms: There are two main stages of yaws infection. The first stage is when the patient is infected and can spread the disease to others. In this stage, a papule (a non-cancerous, growing lesion) develops at the site of the infection. This papule is filled with living tissue and can persist for 3-6 months before resolving on its own. Without treatment, this is followed by skin lesions that spread throughout the body.

translations.source: Ubuhlungu bethambo kanye nezilonda zamathambo kungase kwenzeke. Isigaba sesibili asitheleleki futhi ngokuvamile sivela eminyakeni emihlanu ngemva kokutheleleka. Ibonakala ngokukhubaza imiphumela yekhala, amathambo kanye nesundu/plantar hyperkeratosis (ukuqina). Ngemva kokuxilongwa kwensimu okusekelwe kulezi zimpawu, lesi sifo singaqinisekiswa ngokuhlola isampula ku-legion yesikhumba ngaphansi kohlobo olukhethekile lwe-microscope (ukuhlolwa kwedarkfield). Ukuhlolwa kwegazi akuvamile ukusetshenziswa ngoba izamula zihlobene eduze nebhaktheriya elibangela ugcunsula futhi lezi zifo ezimbili zizobonisa umphumela ofanayo.
translations.reference: Bone pain and bone lesions may also occur. The second stage is noninfectious and typically appears five years after infection. It is characterized by disabling consequences of the nose, bones and palmar/plantar hyperkeratosis (thickening). After a field diagnosis based on these symptoms, the disease can be confirmed by examining a sample from a skin legion under a special type of microscope (darkfield examination). Blood tests are not typically used because yaws is closely related to the bacterium that causes syphilis and the two diseases will show the same result.
translations.hypothesis: Bone pain and skin lesions can occur. The second stage is less common and usually appears five years after the initial infection. It is characterized by neurological symptoms, bone lesions, and skin nodules/plantar hyperkeratosis (thickening). After a diagnosis based on these symptoms, the disease can be confirmed by examining a skin sample under a specialized type of microscope (darkfield microscopy). Blood tests are not typically used because the samples are close to the bacteria causing the infection, and these two conditions would show similar results.

translations.source: Ukwelashwa: Okwamanje awukho umuthi wokugomela izamula. Imfundo yezempilo kanye nenhlanzeko yomuntu siqu ethuthukisiwe kungamaqhinga abaluleke kakhulu okuvimbela. Ama-antibiotics amabili angasetshenziswa ukwelapha amayaws. Umthamo owodwa womlomo we-azithromycin (30 mg/kg, ubukhulu obungu-2 g) uwukwelashwa okunconyiwe. IBenzathine penicillin (umthamo owodwa we-intramuscular) emayunithi ayizigidi ezingu-0.6 (izingane ezineminyaka engaphansi kweyi-10) kanye namayunithi ayizigidi ezingu-1.2 (abantu abaneminyaka engaphezu kweyi-10) angasetshenziswa ezigulini ezisolwa ngokwehluleka ukwelashwa ngemva kwe-azithromycin, noma iziguli ezingakwazi ukwelashwa nge-azithromycin. .
translations.reference: Treatment: There is currently no vaccine for yaws. Health education and improved personal hygiene are the most important tactics for prevention. Two antibiotics can be used to treat yaws. A single oral dose of azithromycin (30 mg/kg, maximum 2 g) is the recommended treatment. Benzathine penicillin (single intramuscular dose) at 0.6 million units (children aged under 10 years) and 1.2 million units (people aged over 10 years) can be used for patients with suspected clinical treatment failure after azithromycin, or patients who cannot be treated with azithromycin.
translations.hypothesis: Treatment: Currently, there is no medication to eradicate established infections. Improved hygiene and public health education are the most important strategies for prevention. Two antibiotics can be used to treat chlamydia. A single oral dose of azithromycin (30 mg/kg, up to a maximum of 2 g) is the recommended treatment. Benzathine penicillin (a single intramuscular injection of 0.6 million units for children under 10 years of age, and 1.2 million units for individuals over 10 years of age) can be used in patients who have failed treatment with azithromycin, or in patients who are unable to take azithromycin.

translations.source: Lokhu kwelashwa kuzophumela ekwelapheni okuphelele komtholampilo kuma-95% ezimeni kodwa iziguli kufanele zihlolwe kabusha emasontweni ama-4 ngemuva kokuthola ama-antibiotics. Ukubuyekezwa kwemibhalo yomlando kusukela ngeminyaka yawo-1950 kukhombisa ukuthi amazwe nezindawo ezingaphezu kuka-90 zaziyinsakavukela umchilo wesidwaba. Amazwe nezindawo eziyi-14 kuphela kwangu-90 anedatha yakamuva mayelana ne-yaws esekelwe ohlelweni lokuqapha olujwayelekile; nokho, lezi zibalo zingase zibe inkomba nje yokuba khona kwalesi sifo hhayi izinga laso eligcwele. IGhana, iPapua New Guinea kanye neSolomon Islands babika amacala angaphezu kuka-10 000 ngonyaka. i-Ecuador kanye ne-India kubonakala sengathi kuphazamise ukuhanjiswa.
translations.reference: This treatment will result in complete clinical healing in 95% of cases but patients should be re-examined 4 weeks after receiving the antibiotics. A review of the historic documents from 1950s shows that over 90 countries and territories were endemic for yaws. Only 14 out of the 90 countries and territories have recent data on yaws based on the routine surveillance system; however, these figures may just be an indication of the presence of the disease and not its full extent. Ghana, Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands report over 10 000 cases per year. Ecuador and India appear to have interrupted transmission.
translations.hypothesis: This treatment typically results in a complete cure in 95% of cases, but patients should be re-examined four weeks after completing the antibiotic course. A review of historical data from the 1950s shows that over 90% of countries and regions were affected by yaws. Only 14 of the 90 countries and regions have recent data on yaws, based on routine surveillance systems; however, these figures may only reflect the presence of the disease and not its actual prevalence. Ghana, Papua New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands report over 10,000 cases annually. Ecuador and India appear to be experiencing disruptions in reporting.

translations.source: Umkhuhlane ophuzi Uhlolojikelele: iYellow fever yisifo esiyigciwane esidluliselwa kubantu ngokulunywa omiyane abanalesi sifo. Ijwayele ukungenwa izifo eziwumshayabhuqe futhi ingavinjelwa ngomgomo. Lezi zimiyane eziluma emini zizalela ezindlini (zasekhaya), emahlathini noma emahlathini (endle), noma kuzo zombili izindawo zokuhlala (ezisekhaya). iYellow fever yisifo esiyingozi, esiyingozi kakhulu, esisengozini yokusakazeka emhlabeni wonke, emele usongo olungase lube khona ekuvikelekeni kwezempilo emhlabeni wonke.
translations.reference: Yellow fever Overview: Yellow fever is a viral disease that is transmitted to humans by the bites of infected mosquitoes. It is prone to epidemics and is preventable with a vaccine. These day-biting mosquitoes breed around houses (domestic), in forests or jungles (wild), or in both habitats (semi-domestic). Yellow fever is a high-impact, high-threat disease, with a risk of international spread, representing a potential threat to global health security.
translations.hypothesis: Yellow Fever: Yellow fever is a viral disease transmitted to humans through the bite of infected mosquitoes. It is often a severe and potentially fatal illness, and there is no specific treatment. These mosquitoes typically live and breed in tropical and subtropical regions, both in urban and rural areas. Yellow fever is a serious public health concern, posing a significant risk of global spread and representing a potential threat to global health security.

translations.source: Kunezinhlobo ezi-3 zemijikelezo yokudlulisela: iSylvatic (noma ihlathi) iyellow fever isakazeka lapho izinkawu zilunywa omiyane basendle abadlulisela leli gciwane kwezinye izinkawu nakubantu. iYellow fever ephakathi isakazeka lapho omiyane besemi-domestic bethelela kokubili izinkawu nabantu. Lolu wuhlobo oluvame kakhulu lokuqubuka kwezifo e-Afrika. i-Urban yellow fever ingabangela ubhubhane olukhulu lapho abantu abanaleli gciwane bengenisa igciwane ezindaweni ezinabantu abaningi abanomiyane abaningi nalapho abantu bengenawo amandla okuzivikela. Kwesinye isikhathi, abahambi abanaleli gciwane baye bathumela amacala emazweni angenayo iyellow fever.
translations.reference: There are 3 types of transmission cycles: Sylvatic (or jungle) yellow fever spreads when monkeys are bitten by wild mosquitoes that pass the virus on to other monkeys and humans. Intermediate yellow fever spreads when semi-domestic mosquitoes infect both monkeys and people. This is the most common type of outbreak in Africa. Urban yellow fever can cause large epidemics when infected people introduce the virus into heavily populated areas with high mosquito density and where people have little immunity. Occasionally, infected travellers have exported cases to countries that are free of yellow fever.
translations.hypothesis:
There are three main transmission cycles for yellow fever:
*   **Sylvatic (or jungle) yellow fever:** This occurs when mosquitoes infect monkeys, who then transmit the virus to other monkeys and, occasionally, to humans.
*   **Intermediate yellow fever:** This occurs when domestic mosquitoes infect both monkeys and humans. This is the most common type of yellow fever outbreak in Africa.
*   **Urban yellow fever:** This can cause large epidemics when unvaccinated people are introduced to areas with high mosquito populations and no prior immunity. Sometimes, travelers who are not vaccinated can introduce the virus to areas where yellow fever is not present.

translations.source: Kodwa ke, lesi sifo singasakazeka kalula ezweni elisha kuphela uma kunezinhlobo zomiyane ezikwazi ukusidlulisela, izimo ezithile zezulu, kanye nedamu lezilwane elidingekayo ukuze lisilondoloze. Izimpawu: iYellow fever ingaba nezinhlobonhlobo zezimpawu kanye nobukhulu. Uma selingenile, igciwane leYellow fever lifukamela emzimbeni izinsuku ezingu-3-6. Abantu abaningi ababi nazo izimpawu, kodwa uma lokhu kwenzeka, okuvame kakhulu imfiva, ubuhlungu bemisipha obunomgogodla ogqamile, ikhanda elibuhlungu, ukungakuthandi ukudla, isicanucanu noma ukuhlanza. Ezimweni eziningi, izimpawu ziyanyamalala ngemva kwezinsuku ezingu-3-4.
translations.reference: However, the disease can only spread easily to a new country if there are mosquito species able to transmit it, specific climatic conditions, and the animal reservoir needed to maintain it. Symptoms: Yellow fever can present with a wide range of symptoms and severity. Once contracted, the yellow fever virus incubates in the body for 3–6 days. Many people do not experience symptoms, but when these do occur, the most common are fever, muscle pain with prominent backache, headache, loss of appetite, and nausea or vomiting. In most cases, symptoms disappear after 3–4 days.
translations.hypothesis: However, this disease can only easily spread in a new area if there are suitable mosquito vectors, specific weather conditions, and a sufficient animal population to maintain the virus. Symptoms: Yellow fever can have a variety of symptoms and severity. Once inside the body, the yellow fever virus incubates for 3-6 days. Many people do not experience any symptoms, but when they do occur, they commonly include fever, severe muscle pain, headache, loss of appetite, and nausea or vomiting. In most cases, the symptoms subside after 3-4 days.

translations.source: Iphesenti elincane leziguli lingena esigabeni sesibili, esinobuthi obuthe xaxa phakathi namahora angama-24 ukululama ezimpawini zokuqala. Ukubuya komkhuhlane ophezulu kanye nezinhlelo zomzimba eziningana ziyathinteka, ngokuvamile isibindi nezinso. Kulesi sigaba, abantu kungenzeka babe ne-jaundice (isikhumba namehlo sibe phuzi, yingakho kuqanjwe iYellow fever), umchamo omnyama, nobuhlungu besisu obuhambisana nokuhlanza. Ukopha kungase kwenzeke emlonyeni, ekhaleni, emehlweni, noma esiswini. Ingxenye yalezi ziguli ifa phakathi kwezinsuku ezingu-7-10. iYellow fever kunzima ukuyixilonga, ikakhulukazi phakathi nezigaba zokuqala.
translations.reference: A small percentage of patients enter a second, more toxic phase within 24 hours of recovering from initial symptoms. High fever returns and several body systems are affected, usually the liver and kidneys. In this phase, people are likely to develop jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes, hence the name yellow fever), dark urine, and abdominal pain with vomiting. Bleeding can occur from the mouth, nose, eyes, or stomach. Half of these patients die within 7–10 days.Yellow fever is difficult to diagnose, especially during the early stages.
translations.hypothesis: A small percentage of patients progress to the second stage, characterized by a rapid worsening of symptoms within 24 hours. This stage involves the onset of high fever, multiple organ system involvement, most commonly the liver and kidneys. During this phase, patients may experience jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), dark urine, and abdominal pain associated with liver damage. Bleeding can occur from the nose, gums, eyes, or skin. This stage typically lasts for 7-10 days. Yellow fever is difficult to diagnose, especially during the early stages.

translations.source: Izimo ezinzima kakhulu zingadideka nomalaleveva onzima, ileptospirosis, iviral hepatitis (ikakhulukazi amafomu agcwele), eminye imfiva yokopha, ukungenwa amanye amagciwane eflavivirus (afana nedengue haemorrhagic fever) kanye nobuthi. Ukuhlolwa kwePolymerase chain reaction (PCR) egazini nasemchamweni ngezinye izikhathi kungathola igciwane ezigabeni zokuqala zesifo. Ezigabeni zakamuva, ukuhlolwa ukuhlonza amasosha omzimba kuyadingeka (IgM). Ukwelashwa: Izifo zeYellow fever zingabangela ukugula okubi kakhulu futhi zingabulala ezimweni ezimbi kakhulu. Ukwelashwa okusekela kusenesikhathi ezibhedlela kuthuthukisa amazinga okusinda.
translations.reference: More severe cases can be confused with severe malaria, leptospirosis, viral hepatitis (especially fulminant forms), other haemorrhagic fevers, infection with other flaviviruses (such as dengue haemorrhagic fever) and poisoning. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing in blood and urine can sometimes detect the virus in early stages of the disease. In later stages, testing to identify antibodies is needed (IgM). Treatment: Yellow fever infections can cause serious illness and can be fatal in severe cases. Early supportive treatment in hospitals improves survival rates.
translations.hypothesis: Severe cases can be complicated by severe malaria, leptospirosis, viral hepatitis (particularly fulminant forms), other infectious diseases, infections with certain flavivirus pathogens (such as dengue hemorrhagic fever), and drug reactions. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing of blood and sometimes cerebrospinal fluid can detect the pathogen in the early stages of the disease. In later stages, testing to detect IgM antibodies is necessary. Treatment: Yellow fever can cause serious illness and can be fatal in severe cases. Supportive care in a hospital setting improves survival rates.

translations.source: Okwamanje awukho umuthi othize we-antiviral we-yellow fever, kodwa ukunakekelwa okukhethekile ukwelapha ukuphelelwa amanzi emzimbeni, imfiva, nokwehluleka kwesibindi nezinso kuthuthukisa imiphumela. Izifo ezihambisana namagciwane zingelashwa ngama-antibiotics. Iziguli kudingeka zihlale ngaphansi kwamanethi omiyane phakathi nosuku ukuze kukhawulelwe ingozi yokusakazeka kwabanye ngokulunywa omiyane. Ukugoma kuyisinyathelo esisodwa esibaluleke kakhulu sokuvimbela iyellow fever. Ukuvimbela ukuqubuka kungafezwa kuphela uma iningi labantu ligonywa.
translations.reference: There is currently no specific anti-viral drug for yellow fever, but specific care to treat dehydration, fever, and liver and kidney failure improves outcomes. Associated bacterial infections can be treated with antibiotics. Patients need to stay under mosquito nets during the day to limit the risk of spreading to others through mosquito bites. Vaccination is the single most important measure for preventing yellow fever. The prevention of outbreaks can only be achieved if the majority of the population is immunized.
translations.hypothesis: Currently, there is no specific antiviral medication for yellow fever, but supportive care to treat dehydration, fever, and liver and kidney failure can improve outcomes. Bacterial infections that may occur alongside yellow fever can be treated with antibiotics. Patients need to be isolated in a hospital setting to minimize the risk of spreading the disease through mosquito bites. Vaccination is the most important step in preventing yellow fever. Preventing outbreaks can only be achieved if a large proportion of the population is vaccinated.

translations.source: Umuthi wokugomela iyellow fever uphephile futhi uyathengeka, futhi umthamo owodwa unikeza ukugonywa impilo yonke ngokumelene nesifo. Abantu abelulama ekuthelelekeni ngeyellow fever nabo bavikelekile impilo yabo yonke. Funda kabanzi ngomgomo wokugomela iyellow fever. Ukulawula omiyane kungasiza futhi ekuvimbeleni iyellow fever futhi kubalulekile ezimeni lapho ukugoma kuncane noma umgomo ungatholakali ngokushesha. Ukulawula omiyane kuhlanganisa ukuqeda izindawo lapho omiyane bengazala khona futhi babulale omiyane abadala nezibungu ngokusebenzisa izibulala zinambuzane ezindaweni ezinomiyane abaningi.
translations.reference: The yellow fever vaccine is safe and affordable, and a single dose provides life-long immunity against the disease. People who recover from yellow fever infection are also protected for life. Read more about the yellow fever vaccine. Mosquito control can also help to prevent yellow fever and is vital in situations where vaccination coverage is low or the vaccine is not immediately available. Mosquito control includes eliminating sites where mosquitoes can breed and killing adult mosquitoes and larvae by using insecticides in areas with high mosquito density.
translations.hypothesis: The yellow fever vaccine is safe and effective, and a single dose provides lifelong protection against the disease. People who are vaccinated against yellow fever are protected for the rest of their lives. Learn more about the yellow fever vaccine. Mosquito control can also help prevent yellow fever, and it is particularly important in situations where vaccination is limited or unavailable. Mosquito control involves eliminating breeding sites and killing adult and larval mosquitoes using insecticides in areas with high mosquito populations.

translations.source: Ukuzibandakanya komphakathi ngemisebenzi, njengokuhlanza imisele yasendlini kanye nokuvala izitsha zamanzi lapho omiyane bengazalana khona kuyindlela ebalulekile nesebenzayo yokulawula omiyane.
translations.reference: Community involvement through activities, such as cleaning household drains and covering water containers where mosquitoes can breed is an important and effective way to control mosquitoes.
translations.hypothesis: Community involvement in activities such as cleaning water storage containers and covering water sources where mosquitoes breed is a crucial and effective way to control mosquito populations.

translations.source: Abantu abanezidingo zokwelashwa "bashiywa ngemuva ezinhlungwini" kuveza umbiko omusha Inhlangano yeZempilo yoMhlaba (WHO) isishicilele umbiko omusha wokungena kumofini yokusetshenziselwa ukwelapha, echaza ukuthi isetshenziswa kanjani imofini, njengomuthi obalulekile wezinhlungu, awulingani futhi awusigcwalisi isidingo sokwelapha. Umbiko osihloko "Bashiywe ngemuva ezinhlungwini' uveze izinkinga zokungena kulokhu kulashwa okubalulekile futhi unikeza izenzo zokwenza ngcono ukuphepha kokungena ngenqubomgomo eyisilinganiso. Ngaphandle kokuthi imofini iyasebenza futhi ibiza kancane ukuthi ingadambisa izinhlungu kakhulu, isohlweni kusuka ngo-1977 kuhlobo lokuqala lweWHO Model list of Essential Medicines, inkinga yokungena kumazwe onke inzima. Kukhona ukwahlukana kwefolidi okungu-5 kuya ku-63 esilinganisweni semedeni yokugcina imofini phakathi kwamazwe anemali engenayo ephezulu kanye namazwe anemali engenayo encane.
translations.reference: People with medical needs are “left behind in pain” reveals new report The World Health Organization (WHO) has published a new report on access to morphine for medical use, which describes how the global distribution of morphine, as a vital pain medicine, is unequal and does not fulfil the medical need. The report, titled “Left behind in pain”, highlights the problems in access to this essential medicine and offers actions to improve safe access through balanced policy. Despite morphine being an effective and relatively low-cost medicine for relieving strong pain, listed since 1977 in the first edition of the WHO Model list of Essential Medicines, the disparity in access across countries is stark. There is a 5- to 63-fold difference in the estimated median consumption of morphine between high-income countries and lower income countries.
translations.hypothesis: People with medical needs are being "left behind" in the face of rising healthcare costs, according to a new report. The World Health Organization (WHO) has published a new report examining the affordability of essential medicines, highlighting how a crucial painkiller, morphine, is often inaccessible and fails to meet the needs of patients. The report, titled "Left Behind in the Shadows," identifies challenges in accessing this vital medication and proposes actions to improve affordability through a standardized pricing policy. While morphine is effective and relatively inexpensive for managing severe pain, its availability remains a significant issue. There is a stark difference in the cost of morphine between high-income and low-income countries, with a price variation of 5 to 63 times.

translations.source: Ukugcina iphethini kuyahlukana kakhulu emazweni onke ngokufana komcebo; Akuhambisani nesidingo sokwelapha,njengoba kuveziwe ngenamba ehlawumbelesiwe yabantu abasezinhlungwini noma abakhathazeke ngokuphelelwa ngumoya okukhulu okunokufa okubulalayo. Le datha ivumelana naleyo ye-2018 Lancet Commission eyayichaza ngokuthola umuthi wokudambisa izinhlungu njengomunye oyinkinga kakhulu,ukungalingani okufihliwe kwezempilo yomhlaba ngokuceba kwa-10% wamazwe ano-90% wokuhambisa imofini elingana neophiyodi. "Ukushiya abantu besezinhlungwini uma ikhona imithi esebenzayo ukuthi umele izinhlungu ikakhulukazi kungqikkithi yokuphela kokunakekelwa kwempilo, kumele kube yisizathu sokukhathazeka okukhulu kubenzi benqubomgomo," kusho uDkt Yukiko Nakatani, Umsizi woMqondisi Jikelele weWHO weZokwelapha neMikhiqizo yeZempilo. "Kumele sibamele ngokushesha ukuze baphephe nokungena ngesikhathi kumofini kulabo abanezidingo zeokulashwa ngenqubomgomo enesilinganiso, kuyo yonke indawo."
translations.reference: Consumption pattern varies significantly across countries of similar wealth; it does not correspond to medical need, as indicated by the estimated number of days people are in pain or suffer with severe shortness of breath for people with a terminal illness. This data echoes that of the 2018 Lancet Commission which described the lack of access to pain relief medication as ‘one of the most heinous, hidden inequities in global health’ with the richest 10% of countries possessing 90% of distributed morphine-equivalent opioids. “Leaving people in pain when effective medicines are available for pain management, especially in the context of end-of-life care, should be a cause of serious concern for policy-makers,” says Dr Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General for Medicines and Health Products. “We must urgently advocate for safe and timely access to morphine for those in medical need through balanced policy, everywhere.”
translations.hypothesis: Access to pain medication varies significantly across countries in terms of wealth; it does not align with the need, as evidenced by the large number of people suffering from chronic pain or experiencing breathlessness that could be alleviated with effective medication. This data is consistent with the 2018 Lancet Commission, which identified the lack of access to pain relief as one of the most significant, yet often overlooked, global health inequities, affecting 10% of the world's population and costing the equivalent of the GDP of Greece. "Leaving people in pain when effective medication exists to relieve that pain is a failure of healthcare, and it should be a major concern for policymakers," said Dr. Yukiko Nakatani, Assistant Director-General of the WHO for Access to Medicines and Health Products. "We must address this urgently to ensure that everyone has access to timely and appropriate pain management, regardless of where they live."

translations.source: Izizathu zokungalingani. Ukuthola imofini ukuyisebenzisa ukwelapha kwenziwa izinto eziningi ezixhumanayo okufaka ukukwazi okuhlobene nokuphatha kahle,ukwethembeka nokuthenga okufanele nezinhlelo zesaplayi, ukubakhona kwezinsizakusebenza nemisebenzi yokukwwazi yamabhilidi ehambisana nezivimbo ezihlobene nemithetho yokuvimba eyeqile nenqubomgomo, umsebenzi ongagculisi nokuzizwa kolwazi okungeyilo nendlela yokucabanga. Ngezingqikithi zamazwe ahlukene, abenzi,izivimbi kanye nokuhamba phambili ukwenza kuyahlukana,ababambisani bocwaningo olwenziwe lwathulwa kulombiko, nabaphendulile ku-105 WHO Members States, kuqinisekisa lokhu kwehlukana. Isibonelo, ukungasaplayi ngendlela kwemfini kanye namanye ama-ophiyodi aqinile ezindaweni zezempilo ngenxa yomkhawulo wezimali kuqapheleke ngokufana njengomngcele kumazwe anemali engenayo encane nephakathi nendawo uma uqhathanisa namazwe anemali engenayo enkulu. Ngaphezu kwamakota amathathu kwabaphendulile kuzona zonke izifunda nokuqaphela izivimbo okunyuswa ezothetho nezinto zokulawula ngesikhathi sibona ukubaluleka komhlahlandlela womthetho nemigomo ekutholeni ukungena okuphephile.
translations.reference: Reasons for the disparity Access to morphine for medical use is influenced by many interacting factors including enablers related to good governance, reliable/efficient procurement and supply processes, resource availability and capacity-building activities, along with barriers related to overly restrictive legislation and policies, inadequate service provision, and misinformed attitudes and perception. Given different country contexts, enablers, barriers and priorities for action vary; a stakeholder survey presented in this report, with respondents from 105 WHO Member States, confirms this variation. For example, irregular supply of morphine and other strong opioids at health facilities due to limited financing were commonly noted as a barrier in low- and middle-income countries compared to high-income countries. More than a third of respondents across all regions also noted barriers arising from legislative and regulatory factors while recognizing the importance of a legislative and regulatory framework in achieving safe access.
translations.hypothesis: Reasons for disparities: Access to and utilization of essential medicines is influenced by a complex interplay of factors, including knowledge and skills related to proper management, affordability and appropriate procurement processes, the availability of essential medicines and health services, the infrastructure of health facilities, and factors related to regulations, policies, and the overall healthcare system. Furthermore, cultural norms, beliefs, and ways of thinking can also play a significant role. A review of research conducted in this area, drawing on responses from 105 WHO Member States, highlights these disparities. For example, the lack of access to essential medicines and other health products in healthcare facilities due to financial constraints is a common challenge, particularly in low- and middle-income countries compared to high-income countries. In addition, three-quarters of the respondents across all regions emphasized the importance of clear legal frameworks and regulations in ensuring safe and effective access to essential medicines.

translations.source: Ukungabi nesiqiniseko kwezidingo zezivimbo kungavimba ukungena kweziguli ngoba kungavimba ukuhamba kwesaplayi noma ukwenza imvume nokuhambisa kanzima kwabasebenzi bokunakekela ezempilo. Kubalulekile ukuqaphela ukukhathazeka ngemithelela enamandla engalimaza i-opiyodi okuyiyo, njengamanlda okuholela ukugula kokusebenzisa ophiyodi. Ngalesi sizathu, inani elithile lokuqaphela ngokulimala okungenzeka ngokusebenzisa ama-ophiyodi (isibonelo ukusebenzisa kumahlalakhona ezinhlungu zomdlavuza) kubalulekile kwezempilo yomphakathi kangangokusebenzisa wazi kahle nokuqondene nengcuphe. Lokhu kukhathazeka akumele kuthathe kancane izinzuzo zokusebenzisa i-opiyodi uma kuveziwe ngokwezempilo futhi uma kusetshenziswe ngokuphepha yizisebenzi eziqeqeshiwe. Amathuba okwenza umbiko wethula isethi ehambisanayo ezindaweni zokwenza ezihlose ukwenza kangcono ukuphepha kokungena kumofini.
translations.reference: Unduly restrictive requirements could hamper patient access because they impede the flow of supply or make prescribing and dispensing difficult for health-care professionals. It is important to note that concerns about the potential harmful effects of opioids are valid, such as their potential to lead to opioid use disorder. For this reason, a certain amount of caution about the potential harms of opioid use (e.g. use in chronic non-cancer pain) is important for public health insofar as its use is well-informed and proportionate to risks. Such concerns should not undermine the benefits of opioid use when clinically indicated and when used safely by trained professionals. Opportunities for actionThe report presents a complementary set of areas for action that aim to improve safe access to morphine.
translations.hypothesis: Uncertainty regarding patient needs can hinder access to care, as it can impede the movement of stretchers or create difficulties for healthcare workers in providing assistance. It is crucial to be aware of the potential risks associated with using stretchers, such as the possibility of pressure sores or other injuries resulting from prolonged use. Therefore, a certain level of caution regarding potential injuries when using stretchers (for example, in cancer treatment centers) is essential in public health, and this awareness should be well-understood and communicated.
