translations.source: Izigidi ezingu-152 izingane ezizalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kushuminyaka elidlule Ezinganeni ezingu-10 ezizalwayo, eyodwa izalwa phambi kwesikhathi futhi emizuzwini engu-40 eyodwa kulezo ngane iyafa Amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kushuminyaka eledlule kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni Imithelela yengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu,ingcuphe yokunyuka kweCOVID-19 kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo Isu liyadingeka ngokushesha ukwenza ngcono ukuvikela ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okuhambisana nokunakekela okungcono ezinganeni ezikhahlamezekile kanye nemindeni yazo
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families
translations.hypothesis: Last year, 1.52 million babies were born prematurely. Of every 10 babies born, one is born prematurely, and one of those premature babies dies. Rates of premature births have not changed in the past year in any region of the world. Conflicts, climate change, and the ongoing threat of COVID-19 continue to endanger women and children worldwide. Urgent action is needed to improve the prevention of premature births, along with better care for premature babies and their families.

translations.source: Isilinganiso sezigindi ezingu-13.4 izingane ezizalwe ngaphambi kwesikhathi nokungafika esigidini esisodwa semithelela yokufa, ngokombiko omusha okhishiwe yi-United Nations ama-ejensi kanye nababambisani namhlanje. Lo mzuliswano wokulingana wengane eyodwa kweziyishumi ezisheshe zizalwe (ngaphambi kwamasonto angu-37 okukhulelwa) emhlabeni wonke. Ukusheshe uzalwe: ishuminyaka lokwenza ekuzalweni ngaohambi kwesikhathi, kukhiqizwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni (WHO) ne United Nations Children Fund (UNICEF) ndawonye nePMNCH -- ubudlelwane obukhulu emhlabeni babantu besifazane, izingane, nentsha, kuveza ukuuthusa bokuthula kokuphuthumayo kwezokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okunesikhathi eside kunganakiwe esikalini sakho sobucayi okuveza ukuqhubeka ekwenzeningcono ezempilo zezingane nokuphila. Umbiko ukubuyekezwa kulinganisa okuphuma kuWHO ne-UNICEF, izilungiselele neLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, ekusabalaleni kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Kukhona konke kutholakala ukuthi amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni kuleshuminyaka eledlule nezigidi ezingu-152 zezingane ezihluphekile ezisheshe zazalwa kusuka ngo-2010 kuya ku-2020.
translations.reference: An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.
translations.hypothesis: A new report released today by United Nations agencies and partners reveals that approximately 13.4 million babies are born prematurely each year, and tragically, one million of them do not survive. This represents a significant proportion of all births, with one in ten babies being born prematurely (before 37 weeks of gestation) worldwide. The report, compiled by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) in collaboration with PMNCH – a global partnership for women, children, and adolescents – highlights the devastating impact of preterm birth, a leading cause of death and long-term disability among children. The report provides an updated estimate of the global burden of preterm birth, drawing on data from WHO and UNICEF, as well as the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. The findings show that rates of preterm birth have not improved in any region of the world over the past decade, and that an estimated 152 million preterm babies were born between 2010 and 2020.

translations.source: Ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi manje sekuyimbangela eholayo ekufeni kwezingane,ukubala okungaphezu kuka 1 wokungu 5 wakho konke ukufa okwenzeka ngaphambi kosuku lokuzalwa lweminyaka emihlanu. Abaphilayo kwabazalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi babhekana nemiphumela yezempilo impilo yabo yonke, ngokukhula kokujwayeleka kokukhubazeka kanye nokubambezeleka kokukhula. Izikhala zokuphila yisifunda, imali engenayo, ibala Ukwakhela emmbikweni wesikhonkwane esihlokweni sango-2012,lokhu kusheshe uzalwe, ishuminyaka, umbiko unikeza ukubhekwa okukhulu kokusabalala kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nomthelela oqinile kwabesifazane, imiphakathi nezomnotho. Kujwayelekile lapho izingane zizalelwe khona kuzonquma ukuthi zizophila yini.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive.
translations.hypothesis: Premature birth is now a leading cause of infant mortality, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths that occur before the age of five. Infants born prematurely face a range of health challenges throughout their lives, with an increased risk of disability and developmental delays. A study published in the journal *Lancet* in 2012, for example, found that premature birth is associated with a significant increase in the risk of chronic diseases, such as heart disease, stroke, and diabetes, in adulthood. The study also found that premature birth has a significant impact on women, families, and the economy. Where a child is born can often determine their chances of survival.

translations.source: Umbiko uqaphela ukuthi uyedwa kuphela kwabangu-10 abantwana abasheshe bazalwa (<amasonto angu-28) abaphilayo emzaweni anemali encane engenayo,uma uqhathanisa kwangaphezu kwangu-9 ku-10 emazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Izikhala zokungalingani ezihlobene nebala, nobuzwe nemali engenayo nokthola ukunakekelwa okufanele kukhomba ukuphila kokusheshe uzalwe ukufa kanye nokukhubazeka ngisho nasemazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Southern Asia nesub-Saharan Afrika banamazinga aphezulu okusheshe kuzalwe kulezizizfunda futhi abhekene nengcuphe yokushona kwezingane ephezulu. Ndawonye, lezi zifunda ezimbili zibala ngaphezu kuka-65% wezingane ezisheshe zazalwa emhlabeni. Umbiko uphinde uveze umthelela wengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu nokulimala kwendawo, COVID-19 nokunyuka kokubiza kwempilo okunyusa ingcuphe kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo.
translations.reference: The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.
translations.hypothesis: The report notes that only one in ten preterm babies (born before 28 weeks) survive in low-income settings, compared to more than nine in ten in high-income countries. These disparities, related to race, country, and income, and access to proper care, highlight the fact that preterm birth remains a major cause of death and disability, even in wealthy nations. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and these regions face a high risk of infant mortality. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births worldwide. The report also highlights the impact of conflict, climate change and environmental degradation, COVID-19, and rising healthcare costs, which are increasing the risks for women and children everywhere.

translations.source: Isibonelo, ukungcola komoya kulinganiselwa ukuthi kunomthelela wezigidi ezingu-6 zokusheshe kuzalwe unyaka ngamunye. Kungafika ku 1 kwabangu 10 izingane ezisheshe zazalwa ezizalwe emazweni abuthaka angu-10 akhahlamezwe izinkinga zabantu ngokokuhlaziya okusha embikweni. Ingcuphe yomama abakhulelwe, njengokukhulelwa kwamatshitshi kusondelene nokusheshe uzale. Lokhu kugqamisa isidingo sokuqinisekisa ukuthola ezempilo zokuzala nezocansi, okufaka ukusebenza kokuhlela umndeni, ukunakekelwa okusezingeni eliphezulu kwabakhulelwe nangesikhathi sokuzala. Ajenda yokwenza: utshalomali oluningi ezweni nokushoshova okuholwa ngabazali
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism
translations.hypothesis: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to approximately 6 million preterm births each year. Up to 1 in 10 preterm births occur in the 10 countries most affected by humanitarian crises, according to a recent analysis in a report. Maternal health risks, such as teenage pregnancy, are closely linked to preterm birth. This highlights the need to ensure access to quality reproductive and sexual health services, including family planning, antenatal care, and safe childbirth. Agenda for action: increased investment in countries and parental support.

translations.source: Ishuminyaka eledlulesibone ukukhula kwezishoshovu emphakathini ekuvinjweni kokusheshe kuzalwe kwezingane, okuholwa uhlelokuxhumana lwabazali, izifundiswa zempilo, izifundiswa umphakathi nabanye. Ezweni lonke amaqoqo emindeni ekhahlamezekile ngokusheshe kuzalwe kwengane alokhu ephambili ekumeleni ukuthola ukunakekelwa okungcono nokushinthwa kwenqubomgomo nokweseka imindeni. Okuholela kuInternational Maternal Newborn Helath Conference, eKapa eNingizimu Afrika kusuka ngo-8-11 kuNhlaba, iWHO, i-UNICEF, i-UNFPA nePMNCH bamemezela ukulandela izenzo ukwenza ngcono ukunakekela kwabesifazane nabazelwe ubukhulu bezingcuphe okuvela kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Ukunyuswa kotshalomali:ukuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza zamazwe angaphandle nezendawo ukusiza ezempilo zomama nezingane ezizelwe, ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okuphezulu kuyanikezwa uma noma nalapho kudingeka khona. Ukunyuswa kokwenza: Ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe ngamazwe kokuqhubeka ngokwenza okusungulwe yinqubomgomo yezwe kokunakekelwa komama nezingane ezizelwe.
translations.reference: The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.
translations.hypothesis: Over the past few years, there has been significant progress in addressing the issue of high rates of child mortality within communities, driven by collaborative efforts involving parents, healthcare providers, community workers, and other stakeholders. Globally, initiatives focused on reducing child mortality are increasingly recognized as crucial for improving access to quality care and for shaping policies and support systems for families. In this context, the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, held in Cape Town, South Africa from November 8-11, saw the WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, and PMNCH announce a series of actions aimed at improving the care of women and newborns, particularly those at risk of premature birth. Key priorities include: Increased investment: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to support maternal and newborn health services, ensuring that high-quality care is available whenever and wherever it is needed. Accelerated action: Fostering national commitment and coordinated action to implement evidence-based policies for maternal and newborn health.

translations.source: Ukuhlanganisa kuyona yonke imikhakha: Ukugqugquzela ezemfundo ngempilo, ukweseka utshalomali lwezomnotho oluhlakaniphile, nokubambisana kwezimali kuyona yonke imikhakha, ukuqinisa ukuhambisana kwesimo sezulu nokuphendula kuyona yonke impilo, ukwenza ngcono ukuxhumana nokubekezela ezinhlelweni eziphuthumayo. Okwenziwa abendawo okusha: ukutshala endaweni okuholwa okusha nocwaningo lokweseka intuthuko yokunakekelwa okuhle nokuthola ngokulingana. Okucashuniwe: uSolwazi Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Omunye wabaholayoUsheshe wazalwa-ishuminyaka lwesenzo ekushesheni uzalwe (2023) noKusheshe uzalwe (2012) "Lombiko omusha ukhombisa ukuthi ukubiza kokungenzi kulelishuminyaka eledlule kwakuyizigidi ezingu-152 ezisheshe zazalwa.
translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon.
translations.hypothesis: Integration is crucial across all sectors: promoting education and health, supporting sustainable economic investment, and fostering cross-sectoral collaboration. It also involves strengthening climate resilience and responding to health challenges, as well as improving communication and coordination in emergency response systems. New initiatives are emerging: innovative local solutions and research to support improved healthcare and equitable access. Highlight: Professor Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. Key findings from the report: The cost of inaction in the past year was estimated at $152 billion in lost births.

translations.source: Ngesikhathi kwezinye izifunda bekhahlamezeke kakhulu ukusheshe uzale kusabisa ukuqhubeka kwezempilo kuwona wonke amazwe. Utahlomali olukhulu ekunakekelweni kwabazelwe ababuthaka kungasindisa izigidi zemindeni ekuphukeni kwenhliziyo. Umsebenzi omuningi uyadingeka ukugwema ukusheshe kuzalwe, okuzophinde kwenze ngcono ukuqhubeka kokwehlisa ukufa komama nezingane ezisheshe zazalwa. Ndawonye lokhu okubili kokuvikela ukusheshe kuzalwe nokunakekelwa kuzokhiqiza abantu abanempilo nemiphakathi ukuletha kwezomnotho kanye nokuthuthukiswa kwenhlalakahle. Isizukulwane sethu esizayo sincike kuthina kukhona konke esikwenzayo manje-utshalomali ngeke lube luncane kodwa izimbuyiselo kulolutshalomali luzoba lukhulu kakhulu wonke amazwe"
translations.reference: While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"
translations.hypothesis: In many regions, the high rate of teenage pregnancies poses a significant challenge to public health worldwide. The lack of adequate care for pregnant teenagers can lead to devastating consequences for both the mother and the child, potentially breaking the hearts of countless families. More effort is needed to prevent teenage pregnancies, which will also contribute to improving maternal and child health outcomes. Together, preventing teenage pregnancies and providing proper care will lead to healthier individuals and stronger communities, fostering economic growth and social development. Our future depends on the actions we take today. The investment will be significant, but the returns for all nations will be even greater.

translations.source: uDkt Nahya Salim,waseTanzania: Ukusheshe uzalwe omunye wababhali nezwe eliholwa ngeNewborn Essentials Solutions and Technology 360 (NEST360) "Asisekho manje isizathu sokuthula. Manje sinamadivayisi ezandleni zethu kanye nolwazi lukushintsha imiphumela yezingane zethu ezihluphekile ezizelwe. Ngiyaziqhenya ukubona uhulumeni wethu manje etshala futhi ezibophezela ukwenza yonke indawo ngisho nasemakhaya. Ishuminyaka elizayo lingaba futhi kumele lihluke kulabo ababhekene zokusheshe bazale lana nakuyona yonke indawo."
translations.reference: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon co-author and national lead of Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) “There is now no excuse for silence. We now have in our hands the devices and the knowledge to change outcomes for our most vulnerable newborns. I am proud to see my government now investing and committed to implementation across even rural areas. The next decade can and must be different for those facing preterm birth here and everywhere.”
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Nahya Salim, from Tanzania: A significant breakthrough has been achieved thanks to the Newborn Essentials Solutions and Technology 360 (NEST360) initiative. "There is now no reason for complacency. We have the tools and the knowledge to change the outcomes for our vulnerable newborns. I am proud to see our government investing and committing to implementing these solutions, even in remote areas. The next year should be different for those facing premature births, and for the entire country."

translations.source: UDkt Anshu Banerjee, uMqondisi wabakhulelwe, abazelwe, izingane nentsha kwezempilo kanye nokuguga eWHO "Ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okufanele kwabancane, abantekenteke nemindeni yabo kufanele impela nokwenzangcono ezempilo nokuphila komntwana. Inqubekela phambili iyadingeka futhi ukusiza ukuvikela ukusheshe bazale-lokhu kusho ukuthi wonke umuntu wesifazane kumele akwazi ukuthola ezempilo ezifanele ngaphambi nangesikhathi sokukhulelwa ukuze kuhlonzwe futhi kuphathwe izingcuphe." USteven Lauwerier, uMqondisi weZempilo (a.i.), e-UNICEF "Ngemva kokufa kokusheshe uzalwe njalo ukulandelana kokulahlekelwa nokuphuka kwenhliziyo.
translations.reference: Dr Anshu Banerjee, Director for Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at WHO “Ensuring quality care for these tiniest, most vulnerable babies and their families is absolutely imperative for improving child health and survival. Progress is also needed to help prevent preterm births -- this means every woman must be able to access quality health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks.” Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.), UNICEF “After every preterm death is a trail of loss and heartbreak.
translations.hypothesis:
Dr. Anshu Banerjee, Director of Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at the WHO, stated: "Ensuring proper care for newborns, infants, children, and their families is crucial for improving their health and well-being. Progress is needed to help prevent early pregnancies, which means that every woman should have access to appropriate health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks."

Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.) at UNICEF, added: "Following the loss of a newborn, there is often a cascade of grief and trauma."

translations.source: Ngaphandle kobungcono okuningi emhlabeni esikwenzile eshumininyaka eledlule, asikenzi nqubekela phambili ekwehliseni inamba yezingane ezincane ezisheshe zizalwe noma sivimbe ingcuphe ekuzalweni kwabo. Loluhambo luyakhubaza. Yisikhathi sokwenza ngcono ukuthola ukunakekelwa komama abakhulelwe kanye nezingane ezizalwe kungashayi isikhathi nokuqinisekisa ukuthi yonke ingane ithola ukuqala kwezempilo nokuphokophela empilweni." UHelga Fogstad, uMqondisi Omkhulu, wePMNCH Ukusheshe uzalwe kusho incazelo yokuthi kumele sinyuse utshalomali nesibophezelo kwabasheshe bazalwa - imbangela enkulu emhlabeni yokufa kwezingane ezingaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu.
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five.
translations.hypothesis: Despite the significant progress made globally in recent years, we are not making enough progress in reducing the number of stillbirths or preventing them. This is a deeply concerning trend. It is time to improve the care provided to pregnant women and newborns, ensuring that every child has a healthy start to life." - Helga Fogstad, Director of PMNCH. "Stillbirths indicate that we must increase investment and commitment to preventing them – a major cause of child mortality under the age of five worldwide.

translations.source: Ukuqhubeka kokulinganisa kumama nezempilo zomntwana ozelwe, kanye nokuvikelwa kokuzalwa kwabantwana kungakashayi isikhathi, manje sekubekwe emuva ngokuhlanganiswa okukhathazayo kweCOVID-19, ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukwandisa izingxabano nokukhulisa izindleko zokuphila. Ngokusebenza ndawonye ngokubambisana - uhulumeni, abaxhasi, umkhakha ozimele, umphakathi, abazali, izifundiswa zempilo - singaveza inkinga ngokuthula kokuphuthuma nokuletha ukuvikelwa kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nemizamo yokunakelewa phambili kwezempilo yezwe nemizamo yokuthuthukisa, ukwakha amandla omuntu ngokweseka imindeni, imiphakathi nezomnotho yonke indawo. "UGabriela noJerome Foster, e-Las Vegas, e-United States: abazali baJalen Foster, abasunguli beJalen Gift Foundation "Indawo yethu yasungulwa ngemva kokushona komfana wethu, uJalen Foster, owasheshe wazalwa enamasonto angu-27 ngenxa yokusheshe ngisikwe ngegciwane elingaziwa njengeqoqo B streptococcus. Ukulahlekelwa yindodana yethu kwasifakela ingcindezi futhi kwakungekho ukwesekwa emphakathini nokuthi ngesikhathi sinquma ukunikezela ngendawo ukududuza nokunika ithemba emindenini elahlekelwe ngumntwana.
translations.reference: Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.
translations.hypothesis:
The ongoing efforts to improve maternal and child health, and to ensure safe childbirth, are now facing setbacks due to the alarming combination of COVID-19, climate change, increasing conflicts, and rising living costs. By working together in a coordinated manner – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and healthcare professionals – we can address these challenges and accelerate progress towards ensuring safe childbirth and improving the overall health and well-being of families and communities worldwide.

"Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: Parents of Jalen Foster, founders of the Jalen Gift Foundation."
"Our foundation was established after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was born prematurely at 27 weeks due to an infection caused by Group B Streptococcus. The loss of our son was devastating, and we realized there was a lack of support for families who had experienced the loss of a child. That's why we decided to create a space to provide comfort and hope to grieving families."

translations.source: Besingafuni eminye imindeni izezwe njengba sazizwa - uwedwa. Ngakho, siqale iJalen's Gift Foundation ukusiza imindeni elahlekelwe, ukubanikeza usizo lwamahhala oluningi, njengezithombe, ukwesekwa kokuzila nokubasiza ezinhlelweni zesikhumbuzo." ***Landa usheshe wazalwa kuzinsizakusebenza zezindaba
translations.reference: We didn’t want other families to feel like we did – alone. So, we started Jalen’s Gift Foundation to help other families going through a loss, to provide multiple free services, such as photograph, grief support and help towards memorial services.” ***Download the Born too soon media resources
translations.hypothesis: We didn't want other families to feel the way we did – alone. So, we started the Jalen's Gift Foundation to help families who have lost a loved one, providing them with free services such as memorial videos, grief support, and assistance with memorial planning. ***(Downloaded quickly from a news service)***

translations.source: I-UEA neWHO ihambise iziphakamisi moya zamasaplayi ezokwelapha ezibucayi eSudan United Arab Emirates (UAE) neNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) bahambise amathoni angu-30 amasaplayi ezokwelapha okuphuthumayo eSudan namhlanje. Ibhanoyi eliphethe amasaplayi yokwelapha abalimele, ukuhlinza okuphuthumayokanye nezidakamizwa eziphuthumayo lifike isikhumulweni samabhanoyi iPort Sudan ekuseni kakhulu. Ukuhanjswa kwenani elingu US$ 444 000, eyokuqala iWHO ekwazile ukuyihambisa ngezomoya iya eSudan kusukela kube nokubheduka kwengxabano. I-WHO ihambise amasaplayi ezindaweni zempilo ngaphambi kokunyuka kwengxabano.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict.
translations.hypothesis: The UAE and WHO have delivered emergency medical supplies to Sudan. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have delivered 30 tons of emergency medical supplies to Sudan today. The shipment, containing essential medicines, surgical supplies, and other medical equipment, arrived at the Port Sudan airport earlier this morning. The shipment, valued at $444,000, is the first that the WHO has been able to deliver to Sudan by air since the outbreak of the conflict. The WHO has been working to deliver medical supplies to health facilities in Sudan before the escalation of the conflict.

translations.source: Babekhathele ngemva kwezinsuku ezimbalwa uma ubheka inamba yabalimele. Ukukhishwa kwendizamshini kuza njengengxenye ye-UAE imizamo yokuqhubeka nokukhulula ekwesekeni abantu baseSudanese nokuveza ubuhlobo obunzulu neSudan, ikakhulukazi ngokwesimo samanje esibhekene nezwe. Ukukhululwa kuphinde kube ukululwa kobuntu be-UAE umbono nesikalo sobudlelwano namanye amazwe ancike kubantu nouzibophezela kokusiza abanye ngezikhathi zezinkinga nezimo eziphuthumayo. UDkt Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Unqongqoshe wezwe leInternational Cooperation, uthe i-UAE izoqhubeka nokusebenzisana neNhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni ukuqinisekisa ukubekwa okuyimpumelelo kokusebenza kwelojistikhi ebucayi nokuthumela ezokwelapha ngokuphuthuma nosizo lokudla ukusiza ukubhekana nenkinga yamanje eSudan. Nokuhambisana nesimemezelo kuzona zonke izinngxenye zengxabano yamanje ukuyeka manje unya nokuqhuba isimo esidala ukuhlupheka okungashiwo kubantu baseSudan, i-UAE isheshile ekuzibophezelei kwayo ukunikeza usizo nokusiza mazwe ngesikhathi beludinga.
translations.reference: These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need.
translations.hypothesis: After only a few days, the number of casualties is already significant. The deployment of the field hospital is part of the UAE's ongoing efforts to provide support to the people of Sudan and to demonstrate a strong commitment to the country, particularly in light of the current crisis. This deployment also reflects the UAE's humanitarian values and its dedication to international cooperation, working with other nations to assist those in need during times of crisis. Dr. Reem bint Ibrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of International Cooperation, stated that the UAE will continue to work with the World Health Organization to ensure the successful implementation of logistical support and the rapid delivery of medical supplies and food aid to help address the current situation in Sudan. In line with the call for a ceasefire in all areas of the ongoing conflict to allow for a humanitarian pause and to alleviate the suffering of the Sudanese people, the UAE has swiftly responded to its commitment to provide assistance and support to nations in need.

translations.source: I-UAE igxile kakhulu ekunikezeni amaqoqo abuthaka kakhulu akhahlamezekile kulesisimo saseSudan, ikakhulukazi abagulayo, izingane,nabadala nabesifazane abasengcupheni yokuqhubeka kwengxabano - futhi lamabhanoyi akhululayo azobhekana ngqo nezikhala eziphuthumayo kwezokulapha kanye nokuhlinzekwa ngokudla. I-UAE igcine ngokujula ubuntu kusho ukuthi isiqinisekisile iyaqhubeka nokuxhumana nokumhlaba okukhulu nokuzinikela kokuqinisa ukuthula, ukuphepha nozinzo, ayi esizindeni kuphela kodwa emhlabeni wonke ngesikhathi nababambisani nomphakathi wamazwe angaphandle, izwe liyaqhubeka nokusiza iSudanese ezikhathini ezinzima. Amasaplayi ezempiloafaka ukuthuka okwanele, amasaplayi okuhlinza okuphuthumayo nemithi ebalulekile ukuthi ifike manje kubantu abangu-165 000 abadinga kakhulu usizo lobuntu. Izindawo zezempilo ezweni lonke zibika ukuthi seziphelelwe masaplayi ayisisekelo sezempilo nokuthi izitolo zokwelapha zezwe akusafinyeleki kuzona ngenxa yezokuphepha ezweni. Kokunye ukwesekwa, iWHO izobeka amalojistikhi amabili okuphuthumayo ukuhamba ngebhanoyi ukuqinisekisa lamasaplayi asehanjiswa ezindaweni zempilo ezinkulu ezingu-13 ukweseka abasebenzi bezempilo nokwengeza ukunakekela kulabo abakudingayo.
translations.reference: The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need.
translations.hypothesis: The UAE is focusing on providing essential supplies to those most affected by the situation in Sudan, particularly the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are at risk due to the ongoing conflict. These aid shipments will directly address critical gaps in healthcare and food supplies. The UAE's commitment to humanitarian principles is demonstrated by its continued engagement and dedication to strengthening peace, security, and stability, not only in Sudan but also globally. Working with partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people during these difficult times. The medical supplies include sufficient quantities of essential medicines, emergency surgical supplies, and other vital medical equipment, which will now reach approximately 165,000 people who are in dire need of humanitarian assistance. Health facilities across the country are reporting shortages of essential medical supplies, and pharmacies are inaccessible due to the security situation. In addition to the supplies, the WHO will deploy two mobile logistics units to accompany the shipments and ensure that the supplies reach 13 major health facilities, supporting healthcare workers and providing care to those in need.

translations.source: WHO inamathoni amakhulu angu-30 amasaplayi amalalwveva nezifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo njengehayihayi noshukela, izimo ezingabulala uma zishiywa zingalashiwe. Lokhu nezikhwama zegazi ezingu-23 000 sezilungisiwe nelojistikhi yomhlaba yeWHO uhabhu kuInternational Humanitarian City neWHO njengamanje ibheka zonke izindlela zokuletha lamasaplayi eSudan ngokushesha ekubambisaneni noNgqongqoshe weZempilo eSudan. UDkt Michael Ryan, uMqondisi omkhulu wohlelo lweziphuthumayo zempilo weWHO, uthe Iwho Global Logistic Hub e-UAE isibe okubalulekile kokuphindeka kwempendulo yesaplayi esigamekweni ezibucayi emhlabeni. Ukweseka amazwe angaphezu kuka-140 kuzona zonke izindawo zezifunda zweWHO, ihabhu inikezela ngempilo yonke yeMember States kusipiliyoni sokuphuthumayo kwezempilo okuvelayo ekubhedukeni kwezifo, izinhlekelele njengezikhukhula nokuzamazama komhlaba, nomthelela wengxabano ezinhlelweni zempilo. Ukwesekwa okunikeziwe yi-UAE ivumele iWHO ukuthi ibe phambili ekuphenduleni kweziphuthumayo zempilo-ukuhambisa usizo longoti bezokwelapha kulabo abadingayo lapho kudingeka khona.
translations.reference: WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO has dispatched 30 tons of essential medical supplies and equipment to combat diseases such as cholera and measles, conditions that can be fatal if left untreated. These supplies, along with 23,000 blood bags, have been secured and are being prepared for shipment. The WHO's logistics hub, located in the International Humanitarian City, is currently coordinating all efforts to deliver these supplies to Sudan in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr. Michael Ryan, Executive Director of the WHO's Health Emergencies Programme, stated that the WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has been crucial in scaling up the response to critical health emergencies around the world. Supporting over 140 countries across all regions, the hub provides comprehensive health support to Member States in responding to health emergencies, including disease outbreaks, natural disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE has enabled the WHO to be at the forefront of responding to health emergencies, delivering vital medical assistance to those in need, wherever they are.

translations.source: Njenge ngxenye yokuqinisa ezokwakha umhlaba kwezokulungiselela eziphuthumayo zempilo, ukuphendula nokubekezela Iuae, iWHO nokubambisana bayasebenzisana ukuhambisa amasaplayi ukufinyelela kwababuthaka kakhulu emhlabeni esikhathini sabo abadinga usizo ngayo. Ngengxenye yakhe, uDkt Ahmed Al-Mandhari, uMqondisi wesifunda seWHO wase-Eastern Mediterranean, uthe iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni izoqhubeka nokuxhumanisa nabasemagunyeni bezempilo eSudan ukuletha amasaplayi kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu emhlabeni esikhathi sokudinga usizo kakhulu. Ngenxa yokuxabana, izibhedlela, amakhemisi nezindawo zokunakekela ezempilo yokuqala seziphelelwe okubalulekile, okuyimithi yokusindisa izimpilo. Ukufika kwamathani amakhulu angeziwe awu-30 okukhathazeka namasaplayi ezokuhlinza okuphuthumayo kanye nemithi ebalulekile ukukwazi iWHO izokweseka izindawo zempilo ezinkulu ezingu-13 nokwenza kabusha kwezempilo umsebenzi wokunakekela kulabo abadingayo. Ilojistikhi yomhhlaba wethu kuhabhu yaseDubai idlala indawo ebaluleke kakhulu ukuqinisekisa ukungaphazamiseki kwesaplayi kwezempilo futhi siyajabula ukwesekwa esikunikiwe yi-United Arab Emirates ukusiza ishatha yokuqala bebhanoyi lalamasaplayi adingeka ngokushesha.
translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies.
translations.hypothesis: As part of the ongoing efforts to strengthen health systems and provide emergency health assistance, the UAE, WHO, and partners are working together to deliver essential supplies to those most in need around the world, at the time they need it most. Dr. Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Regional Director of the WHO Eastern Mediterranean, stated that the World Health Organization will continue to work with health authorities in Sudan to deliver supplies to those most in need, especially during this critical time. Tragically, hospitals, pharmacies, and primary health care facilities are running out of essential supplies, including life-saving medications. The arrival of an additional 30 tons of emergency medical supplies, surgical equipment, and essential medicines will enable the WHO to support 13 major health facilities and resume essential health services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a crucial role in ensuring the uninterrupted supply of medical supplies, and we are grateful for the support we have received from the United Arab Emirates in facilitating the rapid delivery of these urgently needed supplies.

translations.source: Kungukweseka okunjalo njengoba lokhu kukhombisa ukwenza kombono wethu eSifundeni wezempilo kuwona wonke umuntu yiwona wonke umuntu. Usizo lwezokwelapha olunikezwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni nge-UAE kubalelwe kwafinyelela ku-55% isamba samasaplayi okwelapha anikezwe iSudan asuka phesheya ngo-2022, ukuveza isikhundla seUAE njengehabhu enkulu yosizo loluntu emazweni angaphandle.
translations.reference: It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis: This demonstrates how our vision for a comprehensive healthcare system in the region benefits everyone. The medical assistance provided by the UAE-based organization to the global health sector reached 55% of the total medical supplies delivered to Sudan from abroad in 2022, highlighting the UAE's role as a major hub for humanitarian aid internationally.

translations.source: Ukubambisana endaweni kubalulekile ekuqhubekeni emhlabeni okuya kokuhlobene nezempilo zamaSDG Umbiko wokuqhubeka omusha ukhishwe ngokuhlanganyela namhlanje kuma ejensi asayinile angu-13 eGlobal Action Plan for Helath Lives and Well being for All (SDG3 GAP). Umbiko usihloko esthi "What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?" uma kubhekwa iminyaka emine kusetshenzwa ndawonye ngama-ejensi ezindlela eziningi ezinkulu kuvezwa ukuthi yikuphi okusebenzile nokungasebenzanga ekuqinisweni ukubambisana nokwesekwa kwenqubeko yezwe okuuya nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSustainable Development Goals (amaSDG). Lo nyaka umaka iphuzu lahhafu eliya kumaSDG kodwa umhlaba usele ngemuva ukufika kuzinhloso zomhlaba. Kusukela ngokwethulwa kwayo ngo-2019, iSDG3 GAP yenze ukubambisana okusha kwezinhlaka phakathi kokusayina kwama ejensi ezindaweni ezibalulekile njengokugcineka kwezimali nokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala kwabanye abantu.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others.
translations.hypothesis: Collaboration at the local level is crucial for progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). A new progress report, released today jointly by 13 agencies that have signed the Global Action Plan for Health Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP), examines what has worked, what hasn't, and what's next. Over the past four years, the report, titled "What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?", highlights the successes and challenges of various approaches to strengthening local partnerships and supporting national progress towards the health-related SDGs. While this year marks the halfway point to achieving the SDGs, the world is still lagging behind in reaching these global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP has fostered new partnerships among agencies in key areas, such as financing for health and primary healthcare for vulnerable populations.

translations.source: Okungenani amazwe angu-67 akhulumile kanye noma ngaphezulu ngeSDG3 GAP ukuqhuba izingqikithi. Senze inqubeko ebalulekile, kodwa kusanendlela ende okumele siyihambe ukwenza ngcono indlela izinhlangano eziningi ezisebenzisana ngayo ukweseka amazwe. Kumele silalele esikutshelwa ngamazwe futhi silandele imiyalelo,kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO noSihlalo weSDG3 GAP kuzinhloko zeqoqo. "Ngibonga ababambisani ngokubambisana kwazo nokuzihlola ngokwethembeka okuqukethwe kulombiko. "Umbiko unikeza iziphakamiso eziyisithupha ezibalulekile ukuqinisekisa ukuthi amazwe ayazuza ekwesekweni okukhishiwe nokubona ukuzibophezela okwenziwe kuSDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.
translations.hypothesis: At least 67 countries have spoken out about the SDG3 GAP and are driving progress. We have made significant progress, but there is still a long way to go to improve the way many organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries are telling us and follow their guidance," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO and Chair of the SDG3 GAP, in his opening remarks. "I thank the partners for their collaboration and their commitment to the findings contained in this report." The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from the support provided and to see the commitments made to the SDG3 GAP realized.

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka: Ukuqhubeka kokwenza ngcono kweSDG3 GAP kwezempilo ngokufuna imibono yeMember State ukuthi sibambisana kanjani ezingeni lezwe nokuphendula okuhlobene neziphakamiso; Ukugcina iGAP njengenkundla yokubambisana; ukulondoloza ukubambisana okuqinile ezingeni lezwe ekunakekelweni kwezempilo kokuqala nokubheka izindlela ezintsha njengesimo sezulu sokubekezela kuzinhlelo zempilo; Ngokuhlanganyela sebenzisa izindlela ezintsha ezingeni lezwe njengokuhambisa indlela yomthelela;
translations.reference: These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach;
translations.hypothesis: This includes: Continuing the improvement of the SDG3 GAP in health by seeking input from Member States on how we can collaborate at the national level and responding to related proposals; Maintaining the GAP as a platform for collaboration; Preserving strong national-level collaboration in primary healthcare and exploring new approaches, such as climate resilience in health systems; Collaboratively implementing innovative approaches at the national level, such as impact pathways.

translations.source: ukukhuluma kakhulu nomphakathi; kanye nokusebenza neMember States ukuqinisa imiklomelo yokubambisana ngobuholi bezepolitiki,indlela yokubusa noxhasomali lokweseka ukubambisana. Imiphumela ebalulekile yombiko sizoyifaka ezingxoxweni kuUnited Nations General Assembly SDG Summit elandelayo nomhlangano wezinga eliphezulu kuUniversal Helath Coverage ngoMandulo 2023. Izinyanga eziholela kulezigameko ezibalulekile zinikeza ithuba ukuphusha ndawonye yiMember States kamaejensi ezindlela eziningi ukuhlonza izindlela ukuthi kuzokwenziwa kanjani iziphakamiso ukulungiselella indawo yesibambiso esiphelele ukuqina kokubambisana nokuqhuba inqubeko eya kokuhlobene nezempilo SDG kuhhafu wesibili weSDG wesikhathi esibekiwe. Inothi eliya kumhleli:
translations.reference: engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:
translations.hypothesis: Engaging extensively with civil society, and working with Member States to strengthen incentives for political leadership, governance, and support for cooperation. The key findings of the report will be presented at the upcoming United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the high-level meeting on Universal Health Coverage in May 2023. The months leading up to these important events provide an opportunity for Member States, working together through various channels, to explore how to best implement the recommendations to create a supportive environment for comprehensive cooperation and to drive progress towards health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:

translations.source: I-SDG3 GAPichaza ukuthi ukusayina kwama ejensi angu-13 kuzithatha kanjani izindlela ezintsha zokusebenza, ukwakha phezu kokubambisana okukhona okuphumelele, ukubambisana okuhambisana nokweseka kwabo ezinhlelweni zezwe namasu okungawezwe futhi aholwa yilo. Ukusayidwa kuSDG3 GAP kuyiGavi ubudlelwane bomgomo; Indawo yezezimali zomhlaba yabesifazane izingane nenstha (GFF); uxhasomali lomhlaba lokulwa kengculaza,isifo sofuba nomalaleveva(Global Fund); I-International Labour Organisation (ILO);
translations.reference: The SDG3 GAP describes how the 13 signatory agencies will adopt new ways of working, building on existing successful collaborations, and jointly align their support around national plans and strategies that are country-owned and -led. The signatories to the SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); International Labour Organization (ILO);
translations.hypothesis: The SDG3 GAP initiative explains how the participation of 13 agencies will introduce new approaches to collaboration, building upon existing successful partnerships, and aligning with their support for national and regional programs that they have developed. The partners in the SDG3 GAP include: Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; the Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria; the International Labour Organization (ILO);

translations.source: Uhlelo lokuhlanganyela lweUnited Nations lwe HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); I-United Nation Development Fund (UNDP); I-United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); I-UnitedNations Children Fund(UNICEF); Unitaid; I-United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);
translations.reference: Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); United Nations Development Programme (UNDP); United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF); Unitaid; United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);
translations.hypothesis: The United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP); The United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); The United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF); Unitaid; The United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);

translations.source: Iqoqo lebhange lomhlaba; Uhlelo lwezokudla emhlabeni (WFP); kanye NeNhlangano Yezempilo Emhlabeni (WHO). Bona umbiko ogcwele, Yikuphi okusebenzile? Yikuphi okulandelayo?
translations.reference: World Bank Group; World Food Programme (WFP); and World Health Organization (WHO). See full report, What Worked? What’s next?
translations.hypothesis: The World Bank; the World Food Programme (WFP); and the World Health Organization (WHO). See the full report: What worked? What's next?

translations.source: Imiphumela yocwaningo olusha ikhombisa izinhlelo zempilo ziqala ukuba ngcono esikhathini sobhubhane Ngemva kweminyaka emithathu yobhubhane lweCOVID-19 izinhlelo zempilo emazweni seziqale ukukhombisa izinkomba ezinkulu zokuvuseleleka kwezinhlelo zempilo, ngokweWHO umbiko wesikhashana emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lephalisi yomhlaba ekuqhubekeni kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19: Lwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ngokuqala kuka-2023 amazwe abike ukwenzeka kokwehla kokuphazamiseka ekulethweni kwendlela yemisebenzi yezempilo,kodwa kwavezwa isidingo sokutshala kokungalungiswa nokubekezela kakhulu kwikusasa. Kulamazwe angu-139 aphendule kumzuliswane wesine wokuzwakala kocwaningo lweWHO, amazwe abike ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka emisebenzini yekota esilinganisweni. Emazweni angu-84 lapho ukuhlaziya okudumile kungenzeka,iphesenti lemisebenzi ephazamisekile yehlile ngesilinganiso esisuka ku-56% ngoNtulikazi kuya kuMandulo 2020 ngo-23 % ngoLwezi 2022-Masingana 2023.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023.
translations.hypothesis: Recent research findings indicate that healthcare systems are beginning to recover from the pandemic. After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, healthcare systems in many countries are showing signs of significant improvement, according to a recent WHO report on the progress of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: 2022-March 2023. At the beginning of 2023, countries reported a decrease in disruptions to essential health services, but emphasized the need for continued investment and resilience for the future. In the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of the WHO survey, countries reported a continuation of disruptions to routine immunization services. In the 84 countries where data was available, the percentage of disrupted services decreased from 56% in July 2020 to 23% in March 2022-March 2023.

translations.source: Ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka kungenxa yakho kokubili ukufuneka nokusaplaya izinkinga eziseceleni, okufaka amazinga aphansi okunakekelwa kwezempilo emphakathini okudingayo kanye nomkhawulo wokubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo nezinsizakusebenza ezinye zokunakekelwa kwezempilo njengokuvulwa kwemitholampilo noma ukubakhona kwesitoko semithi nemikhiqizo. "Izindaba ezamukelwayo ukuthi izinhlelozempilo emzaweni amaningi ziqala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ezigidini zabantu abawugejile ngesikhathi sobhubhane," kusho uDkt Rudi Eggers, Umqondisi weWHO weMisebenzi yeZempilo yoKuhlanganisiwe. "Kodwa sidinga ukuqinisekisa ukuthi wonke amazwe ayaqhubeka nokuvala izikhala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo,nokusebenzisa izifundo abazifundile ukwakha izinhlelo zempilo ezizilungiselele nokubekezela kwekusasa". Izimpawu zokuqala eziqaphelekayo ekuvuseleleni Kulolu cwaningo olusha,amazwe ambalwa abike ngokungenhloso isikalo sasemuva sokuthola kuzinkundla zonke umsebenzi wokuletha nokusebenza kwezempilo yomphakathi okubalulekile kusukela 2020-2021 ukubikwa kukhombisa ukubaluleka kwegxathu kubuyelwe emazingeni angaphambi kobhubhane ukulethwa kokusebenza nokusebenza okukhulu kwezinhlelo.
translations.reference: Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis: The ongoing disruptions are due to both the immediate and cascading effects of the crisis, including reduced access to healthcare services, the strain on healthcare workers and resources, and the closure of some healthcare facilities or limitations on the availability of essential medicines and supplies. "While it is encouraging to see that many health services are being restored in areas affected by the crisis," said Dr. Rudi Eggers, Director of Integrated Health Services at the WHO, "we need to ensure that all countries continue to address the gaps in health service delivery and use the lessons learned to build more resilient and prepared health systems for the future." Early signs of recovery In a new study, a few countries have reported a significant rebound in the delivery and utilization of essential public health services since 2020-2021. The data highlights the importance of sustained efforts to restore services to pre-crisis levels and to improve the overall performance of health systems.

translations.source: Ekupheleni kuka-2022, amazwe amaningi abike izimpawu ezingacacile zokuvuselelwa kokusebenza okufaka ukusebenza kwezocansi, ukuzala, abakhullwe, abazelwe, izingane nezempilo yentsha, ukudla okunempilo ukugomma,izifo ezithelelanayo (okufaka umalaleveva, iHIV, iTB nezinye izifo ezithelelana ngokocansi) izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe,izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,ukuphathwa kwengqondo, okwengqondo nesifo sokusebenzisa izidakamiza, ukunakekelwa kwabantu abadala nokunakekelwa okulekelelayo nokwesiko. Inombolo yamazwe abika ukuphazamiseka ohlelweni lwawo lesaplayi sheni ezweni okungafika kuhhafu (29 ka-59 wamazwe aphendulayo) okunganekota (18 wa-66 wamazwe) aphedulayo kulonyaka odlule. Ngaphandle kwezinkomba zokuvuselela,ukuphazamiseka komsebenzi kuyaqhubeka emazweni onke namazinga emali engenayo kuzona zonke izinhlelo soomsebenzi wokulatha nokulandelela umsebenzi ezindaweni. Amazwe aphinde abhekane nokunyuka komsebenzi okumele wenziwe - ikakhulukazi emisebenzini yokubheka, ukuxilonga nokwelapha izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo - okungaholela kumiphumela engemihle uma abantu bebambezeleka ukuthola ukunakekelwa ngesikhathi. Ukuvuselelwa kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile kubucayi ngenxa yokuphazamiseka - okufaka imisebenzi yokugqugquzela ezempilo, ukuvinjwa kwezifo, ukwelapha, ukuvuselelwa kwesimilo nephaliyeshini - kungaba nokukhulu umthelela ohlukene wezempil nezinga lomuntu ukunobhubhane qobo ikakhulukazi kubantu babuthaka.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations.
translations.hypothesis: At the end of 2022, many countries reported signs of a resurgence in health service delivery, including improvements in sexual and reproductive health, maternal and child health, nutrition, immunization, and the prevention and treatment of infectious diseases (including HIV, tuberculosis, and other sexually transmitted infections). There were also improvements in the management of non-communicable diseases, mental health, and substance use disorders, as well as in the care of older adults and long-term care. However, a significant number of countries reported disruptions to their health systems, with nearly half (29 out of 59 responding countries) reporting moderate disruptions and over a quarter (18 out of 66 responding countries) reporting major disruptions in the previous year. In addition to these disruptions, many countries are also facing increased demand for health services, particularly for the diagnosis and treatment of infectious diseases. This increased demand, coupled with the ongoing disruptions, could lead to delays in care and poorer health outcomes, particularly for vulnerable populations. The restoration of essential health services is critical to mitigating the impact of these disruptions and ensuring that people have access to the care they need.

translations.source: Ukuhlanganisa imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile elinye igxathu elibalulekile eliya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo noguquko,amazwe amaningi asebe nenqubekela phambili ekuhlanganiseni imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 endleleni yokuletha imisebenzi yezempilo. Cishe u-80%-90% wamazwe ahlanganyele ngokuphelele ekugonyweni kweCOVID-19 ukuxilonga nemisebenzi yokuphathwa kwecala kanye nemisebenzi yesimo sangemuva kweCOVID-19 endleleni yomsebenzi wokuletha. Kuyafana amazwe amaningi (u-80% wamazwe awu-83 aphendulile) abike okungenani okuncane ekukhuphukeni kokuthola amathuluzi abalulekile eCovid-19 (isibonelo ukuxilongwa kweCOVID-19, ukwelaphangxube, imigomo namathuluzi okuzivikela siqu - PPE), ngezinkinga zokusebenza kwezempilo nokwentuleka koxhasomali okumele izivimbo ezijwayeleke kakhulu. Ukuqhubeka kokusekwa kuyadingeka ukuvuselela,ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela Amazwe amaningi aseqalile asebenzisa asebekufundile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19 okufaka ngezikhungo zenamba yokusbenza kokusha ukuphazamisa amasu endleleni yokulethwa kwezempilo.
translations.reference: Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.
translations.hypothesis: Integrating COVID-19 activities into essential health services represents another crucial step towards system strengthening and transformation. Many countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 activities into existing health service delivery pathways. Approximately 80-90% of countries that have fully engaged in COVID-19 response are integrating case management and post-COVID-19 management activities into the health service delivery system. Furthermore, a significant number of countries (80% of the 83 that responded) have reported at least some improvements in the availability of essential COVID-19 tools (e.g., COVID-19 testing, treatment, guidelines, and personal protective equipment), addressing health workforce challenges and supply chain disruptions that were common during the pandemic. Continued support is needed to sustain, scale up, and prepare for future challenges. Many countries have begun to leverage the lessons learned during the COVID-19 pandemic to strengthen health systems and improve the resilience of health service delivery.

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka ukubekwa kwezindlela zokwelapha ngefoni,ukugqugquzela ukunakekelwa usekhaya nokungenelela kokuzinakekela, izindlela zokuqinisa ukubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo, amandla nezindlela zokweseka, ukwenza kabusha ekuthengeni nokuletha imithi namasaplayi ezinye izindlela zokuxhumana nomphakathi ukubambisana nabanikezeli bomkhakha ozimele nowomphakathi. Amakota amathathu ezwe abaike ukufakwa koxhasomali olwengeziwe oluya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo lwesikhathi eside, ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela. Amazwe agcizelele isidingo sokwesekwa kweWHO ekubhekaneni nezinkinga ezisele ngokwengqikithi yeCOVID-19 nangale,ikakhulukazi okujwayele ukuhlobana nokuqinisa ukusebenza kwezempilo, ukwakha amandla okubheka imisebenzi yezempilo, ukwakha ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala okuncike kuzindlela zokunakekela, ukubusa, inqubomgomo nokuhlela nokuhlela ezezimali noxhasomali. Inothi kubahleli: Emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lwephasile yomhlaba weWHO, amazwe angu-222, izindawo bamenywa ukuba baphendule kucwaningo oluncike kuwebhu eqinisikesiwe phakathi kukaLwezi 2022 noMasingana 2023. Ucwaningo lwesine lulandele kucwaningo lwephasile edlule yango-2020 no2021 lweWHO: uMzuliswano 1 (Nhlaba-Mandulo 2020) uMzuliswano 2 (Masingana-Ndasa 2021) noMzuliswano 3 (Lwezi-Zibandlela 2021) okwakhombisa ukukhulu bokuthi ubhubhane lalukhahlameza ukuqhubeka kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile nokuthi amazwe enza kanjani.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis: This includes the implementation of telemedicine, promotion of home-based care and self-care strategies, strengthening the resilience of the health workforce, empowering communities, and innovative ways to procure and deliver medicines and supplies, as well as improved communication channels to collaborate with the private and public sectors. Three key areas for investment are identified: strengthening primary health care systems, promoting long-term sustainability and preparedness. Countries emphasized the need for WHO support in addressing the ongoing challenges related to COVID-19, and beyond, particularly in areas such as health workforce resilience, strengthening health systems, building primary health care capacity based on care pathways, governance, policy and planning, and financial and human resource mobilization. For researchers: In the fourth cycle of the WHO global pulse survey, 222 countries and territories were invited to participate in a web-based survey between July 2022 and January 2023. This fourth survey follows the previous pulse surveys conducted by WHO in 2020 and 2021: Survey 1 (March 2020), Survey 2 (May 2021), and Survey 3 (September 2021), which highlighted the impact of the pandemic on the continuity of essential health services and how countries were responding.

translations.source: Ngesikhathi ucwaningo lwephasile lunemikhawulo njengokubika ukuchema nokumela,amandla alomzamo ukuthi mukhulu futhi uletha ulwazi ngokuphindekile. Itemu "izwe" kumele liqondwe ukuthi lifaka wonke amazwe, imingcele nezindawo. Abahlaziya okudumile kwaqedwa kuncike ezimpendulweni eziphuma emazweni angu-84, abahlukumezi noma izindawo ezaphendula okungenani kusahluko socwaningo olulodwa kuyona yonke imizuliswano emine yocwaningo.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis: While research using surveys has limitations, such as potential biases in reporting and response rates, the strength of this study lies in its large sample size and the wealth of information it provides. The term "country" should be understood to encompass all nations, territories, and regions. The analysis included responses from 84 countries, with at least one region from each country contributing data to all four sections of the survey.

translations.source: Isitatimende sokubungaza ukuphela kweminyaka engu-50 yeHRP: iSweden ne-United Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthen Ireland Ukubamba isimo seNqubomgomo neKomidi lokuXhumana (PCC) Amalungu abungaza ukuphela kuka-50 HRP nokuzuza okuningi, iSweden neUnited Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthern Ireland ukufunda ukuqapheleka okulandelayo kokwamukela nokuhalalisa emhlanganweni wesi-36 weHRP PCC. Isizukulwane sethu sesibone inqubekela phambili enkulu kusayensi nezobuchwepheshe ukuthi konke okuqhubekayo kwesizukulwane ndawonye, nokubona okwemfundo okumbalwa,ezobuchwepheshe, ezokwelapha noguquko lwenhlalakahle luqhutshwa ukuguquka kokuvikela kulandelwa ukwenzangcono kwezempilo yokuzala, ukwenza kabusha kwezempilo kanye namalungelo nokulingana ngokobulili. Kepha ukucabanga izingqinamba emkhakheni weSRHR uqhubeke futhi kuphikiswane,okunye ukuqhubeka kusembuzweni futhi namhlanje. Sincoma abasebenzi beHRP ababambisani nabeseki kwabakutholile okuningi kuze kube yinamhlanje nokubona izinto ezinkulu ezenzekile ngesikhathi esinguhhafu wekhulunyaka ekukhiqizeni ubufakazi obeseka ukungena okuphelele nokulinganayo kwezempilo namalungelo ezocansi nokuzala.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.
translations.hypothesis: Statement celebrating the 50th anniversary of the HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. The Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) of the HRP announces the 50th anniversary and significant achievements. Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland are pleased to announce the upcoming 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has witnessed remarkable progress in science and technology, with advancements across various fields, including education, technology, medicine, and social development, all driven by a spirit of innovation and a commitment to improving human well-being. However, challenges remain in the field of sexual and reproductive health and rights (SRHR), and some issues continue to be debated even today. We commend the staff of the HRP who have worked tirelessly and achieved so much over the past half-century, producing evidence that supports the full and equal realization of sexual and reproductive health and rights.

translations.source: Singathanda ukuveza imikhakha emikhulu emithathu yokuzuziwe Okokuqala ucwaningo olusezingeni eliphezulu lweHRP lube nomthelela obonakalayo kunqubomgomo yezempilo yomphakathi,amazinga, nokwenza emhlabeni wonke okubhekene nezidingo zokuguquka kusibalo sabantu esahlukene.
