Okwesibili HRP iqapheleke ngeHRP Alliance, isiqinise izikhungo zocwaningpo endaweni nasemhlabeni,kwavumela abacwaningi abaningi ukuthi bathole amakhono okucwabinga ukuqinisekisa ubunhalo obuphezulu bocwaningo lweSRHR ukuthi liqhubeke futhi lunyuke esikhathini esizayo. Okwesithathu,umhlaba uhlelwa yizinkinga, ukuxabana nokunyusa ukuqaba ekulinganeni kobulili, iHRP isize ukubamba isisekelo samalungelo abasefazane nezingane nabantu ababuthaka. I-HRP ihlala iyithuluzi elibalulekile kuhlelo lweUnited Nations yocwaningo lokhukhiqiza kubantu nokuqhubeka nokuba umholi ovelele kwezempilo yezocansi nokuzala emhlabeni.
translations.reference: We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health.
translations.hypothesis: We are pleased to highlight three key areas where the Human Reproduction Program (HRP) has made significant contributions. First, high-quality HRP research has had a tangible impact on public health policies, guidelines, and global efforts to address the diverse needs of populations. Second, the HRP has fostered collaboration through the HRP Alliance, connecting research centers worldwide and enabling numerous researchers to develop expertise, ensuring the continued quality and advancement of sexual and reproductive health research. Third, in a world grappling with challenges, inequalities, and discrimination related to gender, the HRP has helped to advance the rights of women, children, and vulnerable populations. The HRP remains a vital tool within the United Nations system, driving research for human development and continuing to be a leading force in global sexual and reproductive health.

translations.source: HRP nababambisani bayo sebesisondeze kusithembiso sango-1994 KuNkomfa Yamazwe angaphandle Esibalweni Sabantu neNtuthuko, futhi isimemezelo sibeka amalungelo abantu ikakhulukazi abesifazane namantombazane phakathi nokugcineka kwentuthuko. Sifisa ukuzwakalisa ukubonga nokujabula kwethu kunobhala weHRP, ababambisani, kanye nabaxhasi kanye nohlelokuxhumana lomhlaba ezizindeni zokubambisana nongoti abasiza ukwenza isiqiniseko ukuthi imizamo inikela ekwenzeni umehluko kubantu besifazane, amadoda, izingane, intsha, nemiphakathini eyahlukene iHRP izimisele ngokusiza. Ngokubambela amalunga ePCC sivumeleni siqinisekise ukubambisana kwethu nani ukweseka okuzuziwe kweHRP ekuphumeleleni okuncike ku-ajenda 2030.
translations.reference: HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.
translations.hypothesis: HRP and its partners are building on the commitments made in 1994 at the International Conference on Population and Development, and this declaration places human rights, particularly the rights of women and girls, at the center of sustainable development. We would like to express our gratitude and appreciation to the HRP secretariat, its partners, and the global community for their collaboration and support in ensuring that the efforts contribute to making a difference in the lives of women, men, children, youth, and diverse communities. By joining the PCC, we reaffirm our commitment to working together to support HRP's efforts to achieve the 2030 Agenda.

translations.source: I-WHO yethula umzamo omusha wokwenza ngcono ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane Ukusiza amazwe ukuthi azilungisele okungcono kwekusasa lobhubhane,WHO yethule umzamo namahlanje ozonikeza imiyalelo yokuhlela ngokuhlanganisa ngokuphendula kunoma iyiphi iphathojini yokuphefumula njengomkhuhlane noma ikhorona. I-Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, noma iPRET, ihlanganisa amathuluzi akamuva nezindlela zokufunda okwabelwana ngazo kanye nesenzo esiyiqoqo esisungulwe phakathi nobhadane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zakamuva zezempilo zomphakathi. Ngomzamo iWHO izosebenzisa indlela yokudlulisela ukuyala amazwe ekuhleleni ubhubhane,ngoba kukhona amandla amaningi namandla amaningi afanayo kumaqoqo ephathojini. I-PRET iphendula isimemezelo somuyalelo wezobuchwepheshe nokwesekwa kokugqugquzelwa nokuqiniswa kokuhlanganisa ukuzilungiselela nokuphendula,njengova kuveziwe kuzinqumo zeWorld Health Assembly.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization (WHO) has launched a new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness. To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, the WHO today announced a program that will provide guidance on planning, including how to respond to any respiratory pathogen, such as influenza or coronavirus. The Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, combines the latest tools and learning resources in a comprehensive package developed in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through this initiative, the WHO will use a phased approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, recognizing that there are many commonalities across different pandemic scenarios. The PRET initiative responds to the call for technical and operational guidance on strengthening preparedness and response, as outlined in resolutions of the World Health Assembly.

translations.source: Ububhane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zezempilo zikhombisile ukuthi amazwe adinga ukuthi alungele ukubhekana nezinsongo zezifo ezithathelwanayo, ngezinhlelo zokuzilungiselela ezilungiselelwe ngesandla kanye nokuxhumana okungcono nokusebenzisana neminye imikhakha efana nezolimo. "Ukuzilungiselela, ukuvikela nemisebenzi yokuphendula akumele kube yisifundazwe somkhakha wezempilo kuphela," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Njengeziphuthumayo zempilo zinomthelela kumikhakha eminingi, ngakho nokuzilungiselela kwethu nemizamo yokuphendula mayilingane nemikhakah, izinhlobo namagciwane. Kubucayi futhi ukuthi ukukhuluma komphakathi nokulingana kuphakathi kwemizamo yethu, ikakhulukazi kulabo bantu abagcindezelekile futhi abasengcupheni enkulu." I-PRET iwuguquko endleleni yeWHO ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ngokusebenzisa indlela yelensi yokudlulisela, ukunokugxila ezifweni ezithile.
translations.reference: The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases.
translations.hypothesis: The COVID-19 pandemic and other emerging health threats have highlighted the need for countries to be prepared to address infectious disease risks, with tailored preparedness plans and improved communication and collaboration with other sectors, such as agriculture. "Preparedness, prevention, and response efforts should not be the sole responsibility of the health sector," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "Because health emergencies have an impact on many sectors, our preparedness and response efforts must be coordinated across sectors, addressing the specific risks, vulnerabilities, and pathogens. It is also crucial that community engagement and equity are at the heart of our efforts, particularly for those who are most vulnerable and at greatest risk." The PRET represents a shift in the WHO's approach to pandemic preparedness, using a lens of transmission to focus on specific diseases.

translations.source: I-WHO izoqhubeka nokuthuthukisa nokusabalalisa imiyalelo ezifweni ezithile njengoba kudingeka. Ukwethulwa komzamo kuzokwenzeka ngosuku lokugcina lwemihlangano abangaphezu kuka-120 abamele Member State, ababambisani, noNobhala weWHO, ukuchaza indlela eya phambili yePRET okufaka ukuthuthuka kwebalazwe lokwenza lomhlaba kokuzilungiselela lobhubhane lwegciwane lokuphefumula. Imizamo yePRET isifundo sokuqala sigxile kumagciwane pokuphefumula, okufaka umkhuhlane, igciwane lekhorona namagciwane ezokuphefumula. Ngokunikezwa ubhubhane oluqhubekayo nokwesabisa okungenzeka emkhuhlaneni, lemojuli izokwenza amazwe abheke ngokucophelela, ukuhlola, ukubuyekeza imizamo yokuhlela ubhubhane lwezokuphefumula ukuqinisekisa banamandla okusebenzayo, namandla akhona. Uhlelo lusendleleni lokuhlonza iqoqo elilandelayo lamagciwane, njengama-arbovirus, ukuthi kubhekwane nawo ngaphansi kwalomzamo.
translations.reference: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative.
translations.hypothesis: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidelines for specific settings as needed. The launch of this initiative will take place during a meeting of over 120 representatives from Member States, partners, and the WHO Secretary-General, to outline the next steps for the Pandemic Prevention and Response (PPR) framework, including strengthening global preparedness for respiratory pandemics. The initial phase of the PPR initiative will focus on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and other respiratory viruses. Given the ongoing pandemic and the potential for future outbreaks of respiratory diseases, this module will encourage countries to carefully review, assess, and update their respiratory disease preparedness plans to ensure they are effective and sustainable. The program is also exploring the possibility of including a wider range of pathogens, such as arboviruses, for consideration under this initiative.

translations.source: Lokhu kuzolandela okuhamba phambili okuhlonziwe ngeziphakamiso eziyishumi zokuqinisa ukwakhiwa komhlaba kokuzilungiselela kokuphuthuma kwezempilo, ukuphendula nokubekezela (HEPR). Umzamo wePRET ukuyisa esikhathini esisha sokuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokumela uguquko lwemisebenzi ngqo yeWHO ukweseka wonke amaMember State ekuqiniseni ezempilo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo, ukuvikela namandla okuphendula nokukwazi. Umzamo ungaphinde usize ukusebenzisa izinjongo nokuhlinzekwa kwePandemic Accord okwamanje okusaxoxwa ngayo yiWHO Member State. Kulandela lokho okufakiwe nezingxoxo emihlanganweni yababambe iqhaza, iWHO imema ababambisani kulesimemezelo somhlaba sokwenza ukuwandisa ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane legciwane lokuphefumula. Kufuneka ukwenza
translations.reference: This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action
translations.hypothesis: This initiative builds upon previous efforts and presents ten key recommendations to strengthen national health systems' preparedness for health emergencies, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET initiative aims to usher in a new era of pandemic preparedness and reflects the WHO's commitment to supporting all Member States in strengthening their health systems' capacity for emergency preparedness, protection, and response. The initiative may also help to implement the objectives and provisions of the ongoing Pandemic Accord negotiations within the WHO Member States. Following the release of these recommendations and discussions at relevant meetings, the WHO is inviting partners in this global initiative to scale up efforts to prepare for future respiratory pandemics. This requires...

translations.source: AmaMember State eWHO, nababambisani basebenza ndawonye ukuhlonza nokuhambisa phambili ukwenza izenzo zokuqinisa izwe, isifunda, nokuzilungiselela komhlaba kwezobhubhane nokuvela kwezifo ezithelelanayo ezisabisayo. Siyayibona indima yomphakathi nayo yonke imikhakha sidinga ukulinganisa nokugcina umfutho ekupheleni kwesakile yokusaba nokunganaki lokho kujwayelekile ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane nokuphendula. Sikhumbula izifundo zobhubhane olwedlule ne-ephidemikhi, ikakhulukazi lezo ezidalwa amagciwane okuphefumula. Lawo magciwane azoqhubeka nokuletha ukusabisa komhlaba okukhulu ngamandla okudala izinkinga ezinkulu nokufa, izinhlelo zempilo ezingaphezu kwamandla, ukwehlisa ezomnotho zomhlaba, ukwengeza ukungalingani, okukhona ekungeneni kumathuluzi omabili okuvikela ubhubhane nokunakekelwa ezempilo kubona bonke abantu. Lesi yisimemezelo sokwenza ukuqhubeza ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokuvela kokwesaba emhlabeni.
translations.reference: WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
translations.hypothesis: WHO Member States and partners work together to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and other emerging health threats. We recognize the crucial role that all sectors must play in ensuring equitable access to and sustained investment in pandemic preparedness and response. Drawing lessons from past pandemics and epidemics, particularly those caused by respiratory pathogens, we must address the significant global risks posed by these agents. These agents have the potential to cause widespread disruption, death, overwhelm healthcare systems, trigger economic downturns, exacerbate inequalities, and limit access to essential tools for pandemic prevention and health care for all. This is a call to action to accelerate global pandemic preparedness and response.

translations.source: Ukusebenza kokuzilungiselela kuncike kubukhulu bokuhlela nokwenza kokuxhumana. Ukuzimisela kweqoqo kuzenze ezilandelayo kuzobonisa ukuqhubeka okutholakele ngoZibandlela 2025 futhi kuzochazwa (Ukuzilungiselela nokubekezela kokusabisayo okuvelayo (PRET) ukubheka umhlahlandlela: Ukulungiselelwa kokubuyekeziwe ukuhlela okuqinisekisa izenzo zokuhamba phambili nokucabanga izifundo zezigemegeme ezedlule. Ukubona ingcuphe efakwe amagciwane ezokuphefumula, ukuhlela kobhubhane lamagciwane ezokuphefumula ancike kuzingqikithi ezihlonzwe kusifundo sePRET #1: Ukuhlelela ubhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula kuhamba phambili
translations.reference: Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority
translations.hypothesis: The effectiveness of preparedness depends on the extent of planning and coordination. The following initiatives will demonstrate progress towards the 2025 goals and will focus on: (Preparedness and Response to Emerging Threats (PRET) framework: Reviewing and updating the framework to ensure proactive measures and lessons learned from past outbreaks. Addressing the threat of respiratory pathogens: Planning for respiratory disease outbreaks will be guided by the principles outlined in the PRET #1: Proactive planning for respiratory disease outbreaks.

translations.source: Ukunyusa ukuxhumana kubabambisani ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ukuhlela ngezinhlelo zokuxhumana nokusebenzisana. Lokhu kufaka ukwakha izinhlelo ezilinganayo ukwenza imisebenzi ngokuhlanganyela; Nokwabelana ngolwazi ekwenzeni okuhle, izingqinamba kanye namathuba. Ukunikela ngotshalomali olugcinekile, izimali nokubheka kokulungela ubhubhane ngokugxila ekubhekeni izikhala ezihlonze ngesikhathi sobhubhane olwedlule. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 luphendule ukukhombisa ukuthi yikuphi okungazuzwa ngokuzibophezela kwezepolitiki, ukukhuluma komphakathi kanye noxhasomali.
translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding.
translations.hypothesis: Enhanced communication plays a crucial role in disaster preparedness by facilitating the planning and coordination of response efforts. This includes establishing clear communication channels to enable collaborative action, and sharing information to improve understanding, address challenges, and capitalize on opportunities. Sustained investment in communication infrastructure, resources, and disaster preparedness training, with a focus on lessons learned from past disasters, is essential. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the benefits of strong political commitment, public engagement, and support for effective communication.

translations.source: Phakathi nalomsebenzi ukuqinisekisa ukulingana ukulungela obhubhane olulandelayo ndawonye. Ukwenza komphakathi wonke kuyadingeka ukwenza inqubekela phambili evezwe kule simemezelo sokwenza. Ukwenza kumele kuqinise imiphakathi ebekezelayo; egcinayo nokugcineka nokwakha izindlela zezinhlelo; nokubambisa kwamandla amakhulu kokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula. Lesi simemezelo sokwenza sakhishwa eKuzilungiseleni nokubekezela kokuvela kokusabisayo (PRET): Umhlangano womhlaba wekusasa lobhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula obanjwe ngo-24-26 Mbasa 2023 eGeneva, eSwitzerland.
translations.reference: At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis: This initiative aims to ensure equitable preparedness for the next pandemic, together. The active participation of all stakeholders is essential to achieve the progress outlined in this call to action. This action must empower vulnerable communities, promote sustainability and resilience, and build capacity for rapid response and adaptation. This call to action was launched at the Preventing Epidemics: Preparing for the Future of Respiratory Pathogens conference, held from May 24-26, 2023, in Geneva, Switzerland.

translations.source: I-WHO inxenxa ukunyuswa kokwenza ngamathuluzi aphakanyisiwe ukulwa nomalaleveva Ukumaka uSuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni, Inhlangano Yezempilo Yomhlaba (WHO) izokhipha isimememzelo sokunyusa ukwenza kokungenelela okukhona nokusha ukusindisa izimpilo kumalaleveva. Okungafika kuzigidi ezingu-1.5 izingane ezisengcupheni enkulu yokugula nokufa okusuka kumalaleveva eGhana, eKenya naseMalawi manje sebethole umgomo wokuqala kamalaleveva, RTS, S/AS01 (RTS,S), siyabonga kuhlelo lokuhlola oluqhubekayo oluxhunyaniswa yiWHO. Ukuhlolwa komgomo wamalaleveva, owethulwe ngo-2019,kwenyusa ukulingana kokuthola ukuvikelwa kumalaleveva kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu futhi kusindisa izimpilo. Ukwenziwe kakhulu, iWHO ilinganisela ukuthi umgomo kamalaleveva ungasingisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane ngonyaka.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO is urging increased use of recommended tools to combat malaria. To mark World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call to action to boost the use of existing and new interventions to save lives from malaria. In Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, where up to 1.5 million children are at high risk of contracting malaria and dying from it, the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), has now been rolled out thanks to a pilot program supported by the WHO. The malaria vaccine, introduced in 2019, has increased access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and is saving lives. Furthermore, the WHO estimates that the malaria vaccine could save tens of thousands of children's lives each year.

translations.source: "Sinamathuluzi ukwehlisa umalaleveva,iphekheji yokungenenela ebandakanya ukulawula ivektha,imithi yokuvikela, ukuhlola nokwelapha," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Lokhu kulandelwa ngumgomo ophephile nosebenzayo kamalaleveva, ongasindisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane unyaka ngamunye. Ngotshalomali olugcinekile nokunyuka kwemizamo ukufika kulabo abasengcupheni kakhulu, ukuqedwa kwamalaleveva emazweni amaningi sekuyafika. "Usuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni 2023 lumakwe ngaphansi kwengqikithi, "Isikhathi sokuwqeda umalaleveva, tshala, yenza kabusha yenza". Kule ngqikithi, iWHO inxenxa okunye ukwenza okusebenzayo kwamathuluzi akhona namasu okuvikela, ukuxilonga nokwelapha umalaleveva, ikakhulukazi kulesosibalo esingcindezelekile.
translations.reference: “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.
translations.hypothesis: "We have the tools to eliminate malaria, a comprehensive package that includes vector control, preventative medicines, diagnosis, and treatment," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "This is followed by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which can save millions of children's lives each year. With sustained funding and increased efforts to reach the most vulnerable, malaria elimination in many countries is now within reach." The 2023 World Malaria Day was marked under the theme, "A mosquito-free future: Invest in malaria elimination." Under this theme, the WHO is urging continued and effective use of existing tools and strategies for malaria prevention, diagnosis, and treatment, particularly in high-burden areas.

translations.source: Ngokombiko wokugcina wamalaleveva emhlabeni,oshicilelwe ngoZibandlela 2022, bekunesilinganiso sika-247 wezigidi zamacala amasha kamalaleveva ngo-2021. I-WHO African Region iyaqhubeka ukuthwala umthwalo onzima wesifo- okuballelwa esilinganiswenisa ku-95% wamacala kamalaleveva (u-234 wezigidi) no-96% wokufa (593 000) ngo-2021. Kungafika ku-80% ukufa okudalwa umalaleveva eSifundeni se-Afrika obukukubantwana abangaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Amasu amasha namathuluzi Amazweasenze inqubekela phambili ekwandiseni ukungena kumisebenzi yamalaleveva kusibalo sabantu esisengcupheni kakhulu.
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations.
translations.hypothesis: According to the latest global malaria report, published by the World Health Organization in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to bear the brunt of the disease, accounting for 95% of malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of deaths (593,000) in 2021. Up to 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region are among children under the age of five. New tools and strategies are helping to increase access to malaria interventions for vulnerable populations.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kokuqhubeka abantu abaningi abasengcupheni yamalaleveva basantula ukungena emisebenzini engavimba, noma itthole bese yelapha isifo. Izingqinamba ekwandeni kokungena kumisebenzi kamalaleveva sezihlanganisisiwe,ikakhulukazi esu-Saharan Afrika, ngobhubhane lweCOVID-19, ukujika izinkinga zobuntu, uxhasomali olunezivimbo, izinhlelo zokubheka ezintekenteke, ukwehla ekwenzeni kwamathuluzi okulwa ngqo nomalaleveva. Ukubhekana nalokhu okusabisayo nokweseka amazwe ekwakheni izinhlelo zokubekezela zamalaleveva, iWHO isanda kushicilela amasu amasha nomhlahlandlela: isu elisha lelinesidakamizwa esingenamalaleveva ukuvika eAfrika; umzamo omusha wokumisa ukusabalala kweAnophelesi stephensi ezindaweni zasemadolobheni;
translations.reference: Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;
translations.hypothesis: Despite ongoing efforts, many people remain vulnerable to malaria and struggle to access timely diagnosis and treatment. Several factors contribute to the challenges in controlling malaria, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, including the COVID-19 pandemic, social and economic inequalities, limited access to healthcare, unreliable diagnostic tools, and declining funding for direct malaria control interventions. To address these challenges and support countries in building resilient malaria control programs, the WHO has recently published new guidelines and strategies: a new strategy for malaria elimination in Africa; a new initiative to control the spread of *Anopheles stephensi* in urban areas.

translations.source: umhlahlandlela omusha, uthuthukiswe ndawonye yiWHO neUN-Habitat, ukuyalela babaholi bedolobha ekulawulweni kwemalalaeveva edolobheni. ithuluzi elisha ukusiza amazwe ahlola umalaleveva wabio wezinhlelo zokubheka nokuhlonza izindawo zotshalomali I-WHO iphinde yanyusa ukwethembeka, ukubalula nokuthola iziphakamiso zemalaleveva. Ukuhlanganiswa kwemiyalelo yeWHO kwamalaleveva sekukhona manje ngezinkundla ezimbili zedijithali: iMAGICapp kanye ne-"MalariaToolkit" isisetshenziswa. I-WHO igqugquzela amazwe ukuthi ahlanganise kuziphakamiso ekuhlelweni kwezifo zendawo kumthelela omkhulu.
translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.
translations.hypothesis: A new guideline, developed jointly by the WHO and UN-Habitat, urges city leaders to address urban malaria. This new tool helps countries assess the bio-environmental risks associated with investment projects. The WHO has also improved the reliability, quantification, and identification of malaria risks. The WHO's guidelines on malaria are now available through two digital platforms: the MAGICapp and the "MalariaToolkit." The WHO encourages countries to integrate these risk assessments into local planning for a greater impact.

translations.source: Izindlela zokungenelela okusha Ukuqhubeka kotshalomali ekuthuthukisweni nokubekwa kwemigomo emisha kamalaleveva namathuluzi esizukulwane esilandelayo kuzobalulekaukuthola okuqondwe ngomalaleveva emhlabeni 2030. Umgomo wesibili kamalaleveva, umgomo iR21/Matrix-M (R21), uma ugunyaziwe ungasiza ukuvala isikhala esikalekayo phakathi kwesaplayi mokufuneka nokuqhubeka ukwehlisa ukugula kwezingane nokufa okudalwa umalaleveva. Kuhamba phambili kuWHO ukuqhubeka ngokubheka kangoti kweR21 malaleveva ngesikhathi sekwengezwe okuphepha okubalulekile,nemininingo kusuka ekuqhubekeni kukaR21 isigaba 3 sesikhona nokinikezelwa kuWHO. Isigaba sokukhiqiza iRTS,S umgomo wamalaleveva emazweni engeziwe eAfrika kulindeleke ukuthi aqale ngokuqala kuka-2024.
translations.reference: Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.
translations.hypothesis: Strategies for Innovation: Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and tools will be crucial for achieving malaria elimination goals by 2030. The second-generation malaria vaccine, known as R21/Matrix-M (R21), if approved, has the potential to significantly reduce the gap between the demand for malaria vaccines and the current supply, ultimately contributing to a decrease in childhood malaria and deaths. The World Health Organization (WHO) is actively pursuing the continued evaluation of the R21 vaccine, with ongoing safety assessments and discussions regarding the progression of R21 from its current Phase 3 trials and its potential approval by the WHO. The rollout of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional African countries is expected to begin in early 2024.

translations.source: Endaweni yokulawula ivekitha,kukhona imikhiqizoemisha engu-28 kokuzayo kweR&D. Amathuluzi asabhekisiswa afka, isibonelo, izinhlobo ezintsha zamanethi okulapha izinambuzane, izicuphomziqonde ukudonsa omiyane, isipeshali ripheleli, okwasendlini, (i-eaves tubes) nobunjiniyela bofuzo lwemiyane. Abacwaningi bahambisa phambili ukuthuthuka kokulashwa okungeyona i-ACTS emikhakheni wemithi omele umalaleveva ngenxa yokuphuthuma nokusabalala kokungavuminokuncane kwe-artemisinin. Ukdevelophwa kwemithi yesizukulwane esilandelayo, njenge ACTS, okusebenzisa ukuhlanganiswa kwe-artemisinin nezidakamizwa zimbili, kusendleleni kuzokwehlisa ingcuphe yokungavumi kwesidakamizwa. Amanothi aya kumhleli: ngolwazi olwengeziwe ekukhankaseni ngoSuku lukamalaleveva eMhlabeni lweWHO, vakashela u-http://www.who.int/campains/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis: In the vector control department, there are 28 new research and development projects underway. The tools being investigated include, for example, new types of mosquito nets, traps for catching mosquitoes, a complete insecticide, indoor residual spraying, and engineering solutions for mosquito control. Researchers are advancing the development of non-ACT treatments for malaria, addressing the growing problem of artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation drugs, such as ACTs, that combine artemisinin with two other drugs, is aimed at reducing the risk of drug resistance. For more information on promoting World Malaria Day, please visit http://www.who.int/campains/world-malaria-day/2023.

translations.source: Uhlelo loKudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe i-mRNA iya esigabeni esilandelayo ekuthuthukeni kwayo Ukwethulwa kwehabhu yezobuchwepheshe imRNA indawo e-Afrigen, eNingizimu Afrika. Ababambisani bahlangane eKapa ukuxoxa ngesigaba esilandelayo semRNA umgomo ngokuthuthukisa nokwabelana ngolwazi. Ngaphezu kwababambiqhaza abangu-200 bamazwe angaphandle abasebenza ngoHlelo loKudlulisela lezoBuchwepheshe beMRNA abahlangane eKapa kulelisonto ukuze bahlangane bebonana. Ukujoyinwa iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) uMqondisi Jikelele uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus noDkt Joe Phaahla uNgqongqoshe weZempilo noNgqongqoshe Wezimboni nokuhweba uMnumzane Ebrahim waseNingizimu Afrika ndawonye nezikhulu ezisezingeni eliphezulu eziphuma emazweni axhasile, lomhlangano owehlukile ubheke ukuqhubeka kusukela ukwethula kweWHO neMedicines Patent Pool (MPP) ngoNhlangulana 2021.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021.
translations.hypothesis: The mRNA Technology Transfer Hub is moving to the next phase of its development. The hub, which is located in Afrigen, South Africa, held a meeting in Cape Town to discuss the next steps in the mRNA program, including further development and knowledge sharing. In addition to the 200 international participants involved in the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub, representatives from the World Health Organization (WHO), including Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, and South African Minister of Health Dr. Joe Phaahla, as well as the Minister of Industry and Trade, Mr. Ebrahim, joined the meeting this week. This unique gathering, building on the WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) announcement in July 2021, brought together high-level officials from supporting countries.

translations.source: "Ngiyajabula ukuba lapha eKapa kanye nababambisani bethu ukuzokweseka imodeli egcinekayo yokudluliselwa kobuchwepheshe mRNA ukunikeza amazwe anemali engenayo encane kuya kwephakathi nendawo ukuthola ngokulingana umgomo kanye neminye imikhiqizo yezempilo esindisa izimpilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngijabule kakhulu ngokuzuziwe kubona bonke ababambe iqhaza kulephrojekthi,ngaphansi kweminyaka emibili sesikhombisile ukuthi uma sisebenza ngokubambisana, siphumelela sonke." Ababambiqhaza bomhlangano kufaka ababambisani bomkhiqizo wasemazweni angu-15 oHlelweni, ongoti abaphambili, imboni abameli bomphakathi kanye nabaxhasimali. Ngesikhathi somhlangano wezinsuku esinhlanu, ababambiqhaza bazokwabelana ngokuqhubeka baxoxe ngokubucayi kwezinto zokugcineka koHlelo njengezinkinga zegunyabunikazi nezindlela zokulawula kanyenesayensi yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA nokusebenza okubalulekile okuhlobene neLMIC kwezinye izndawo zezifo njengeHIV nesifo sofuba. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 sekukale ngaphansi ukuthi ukungalingani okuphelele kukhona ekutholeni imikhiqizo yezempilo, ikakhulukazi imigomo.
translations.reference: “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.
translations.hypothesis: "I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for the transfer of mRNA technology, enabling low- and middle-income countries to access vaccines and other life-saving health products equitably," said WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "I am particularly impressed by the dedication of all those involved in this project; in just under two years, we have demonstrated that when we work together, we can achieve great things." The meeting participants include representatives from the 15 countries participating in the program, leading scientists, industry representatives, and donors. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical aspects of the program, including intellectual property issues, regulatory pathways for mRNA technology, and the practical implementation of the program in addressing key disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored the stark inequalities that exist in access to health products, particularly vaccines.

translations.source: Kusukela ngoNdasa 2023, ngaphezu kweminyaka emithathu ngemva kokuthi Iwho imemezele iCOVID-19 njengePublic Health of International Concern (PHEIC), u-69.7% wesibalo sabantu emhlabeni sebethole okungenani idosi eyodwa yomgomo weCOVID-19. Okuqaphelekayo lengxenye izohlala ingaphansi kuka-30% emazweni anemali engenayo encane (amaLIC). Loli Hlelo luhlose ukuxhasa ekutholeno okulinganayo emigomweni ye-mRNA ngokunyusa ukuhambisa kwamandla okukhiqiza okugcinekayo kuwona wonke amaLMIC, ukwandisa okwesifunda nokubambisana kwaphakathi nezifunda nokudevelopha nokusiza ukusebenza kwasendaweni ngokwenziwa kokuqeqeshwa nokwesekwa ngongoti. Yikuphi okwehlukile kumodeli yoKudluliselwa kweZobuchwepheshe kwe-mRNA kuhlelo olunezinto eziningi oluvumela ukwabelana ngobuchwepheshe,ukuthola okuningi ukuze ukukhiqizwa ngokwesifunda nendawo kulabo abadingayo kungafinyelela ngokuphindiwe. UDkt Phaahla, uNgqongqoshe weZempilo eNingizimu Afrika, uthe: "Esikubona namhlanje lapha,umzumzu womlando, uHlelo oluhlose ukusiza iLMIC ngohlelokuxhumana lokubambisana emhlabeni.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network.
translations.hypothesis: Since 2023, more than three years after the WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the world's population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. However, this figure remains below 30% in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). This initiative aims to promote equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the sustainable manufacturing capacity of these countries, fostering regional and international collaboration, and supporting local production through training and technical assistance. This is a unique approach to mRNA technology transfer within a comprehensive program that allows for the sharing of expertise and enables increased regional and local production for those who need it most. Dr. Phaahla, the Minister of Health of South Africa, stated: "What we are seeing here today is a historic moment, a program designed to help LMICs through a global partnership."

translations.source: Ngiyajabula ukubona inqubekela phambili eyenziwe esikhathini esifishane kangaka, siyakwamukela ukwesekwa okusuka emazweni amaningi ahlukene-amazwe afana neNingizimu Afrika anokukhiqizwa okuqinile anamandla, futhi sizimisele ukusebenza ndawonye, ukufunda nokwabelana kuthina sonke." Ungqongqoshe Blade Nzimande weMfundo ePhakeme neSayensi, uthe: "Umbono wethu weHabhu yoKudlulisela ubuchwepheshe be-mRNA kungaphezu kweCOVID-19 nje. Amandla okuthi siyakha afuna usizo kuthina ukubhekana nobhubhane lwesikhathi esizayo imigomo yalo engasebenzisa inkundla efanayo yobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Kungumbono osubeke amehlo awo ekubhekaneni nezifo ezivamile ezindaweni zethu njengoMalaleveva, isifo sofuba, iHIV ne-AIDS." Lomcimbi uphinde umake uhambo olubalulekile loHlelo nokwethulwa kwendawo yeHabhu yobuchwepheshe ne-mRNA e-Afrigen eKapa kukhona uDkt Tedros noDkt Matshidiso Moeti weWHO Regional Director for Afrika, Charles Gore uMqondisi oMkhulu weMPP kanye noNgqongqoshe abahlonishwayo ababamba iqhaza ekusikweni kweribhini emcimbini e-Afrigen.
translations.reference: I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis: "We are delighted to see the progress made in such a short time, and we welcome the support from various countries. Countries like South Africa, with its strong manufacturing capabilities, are essential, and we are committed to working together, learning from each other, and sharing our knowledge." Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education, Science and Innovation stated, "Our vision for the mRNA technology transfer hub extends beyond COVID-19. The capabilities we are building will also be crucial in helping us address future pandemics and other health challenges, potentially utilizing the same technological platform. We are focusing on addressing common diseases in our region, such as malaria, tuberculosis, HIV, and AIDS." The event also marked a significant milestone: the launch of the mRNA technology transfer hub at Afrigen in Cape Town. Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, Regional Director of the WHO for Africa, Charles Gore, Director-General of Medicines Patent Pool, and other distinguished guests participated in the ribbon-cutting ceremony at Afrigen.

translations.source: USolwazi Petro Terblance, uMqondisi oMkhulu we-Afrigen, uthe: "Iqembu lonke le-Afrigen liyajabula ukufika kulokhu okubalulekile nokuqedwa kwenkundla yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Lenkundla inendawo ekupheleni kokuthuthukiswa komgomo mRNA nokukhiqizwa kwendawo lapho ihabhu ye-mRNA yomgomo weCOVID-19 ikhandidethi AfriVac 2121 njengamanje isezingeni lesikalo esiphezulu. Ngaphezu kwezinyanga ezingu-18 Afrigen isihambe uguquko olukhulu ngokwesekwa kohlelokuxhumana lababambisani nabeluleki abenza loluHlelo. Sesikhulise amandla ethu nokukwazi ukuhlangabezana nezinga elihle eliphezulu lokuthuthukiswa komgomo we-mRNA, ukusiza injongo yokwakha ukukwazi okugcinekile kumaLMIC ukukhiqiza imigomo ye-mRNA.". I-Afrigen isisungulwe ngempumelelo uhlelo lokukhiqizwa kwemigomo yeCOVID-19 ngesikalo selebhu njengamanje kuyanyuka uhlelo ngezinga elifanele lokukhiqiza ngezigaba imigomo ukuthi isetshenziswe ngesigaba sesibili noma sokuqala sokuhlola kwezokwelapha kumazinga eGMP.
translations.reference: Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards.
translations.hypothesis: Professor Petro Terblanche, CEO of Afrigen, stated: "The entire Afrigen team is thrilled to reach this significant milestone in the establishment of the mRNA technology platform. This platform represents a crucial step in the development of mRNA vaccines and manufacturing capacity, with the mRNA candidate for the COVID-19 vaccine, AfriVac 2121, currently at an advanced stage of development. Over the past 18 months, Afrigen has made significant progress with the support of its partners and experts involved in this program. We have strengthened our capabilities and are now ready to meet the high standards required for the development of mRNA vaccines, contributing to the goal of building sustainable capacity in low- and middle-income countries to produce mRNA vaccines." Afrigen has successfully established a laboratory-scale production system for COVID-19 vaccines, and is now scaling up the process to produce vaccines in stages that meet the requirements for clinical trials in GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) environments.

translations.source: Kuhlelo oluhambisanayo, i-Afrigen izoqhubeka nokwenza ukuqeqesha nokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe kubabambisani bohlelokuxhumana. Abaxhasi badlala indima ebalulekile ekwesekeni uHlelo ngesamba soxhaso kuze kube yimanje ngezigidi ezingu-117 neFrance ekubeni ngeyokuqala ukuxhasa umsebenzi wokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Ababekhona emhlanganweni bekungabamele abaphuma e-European Commission, iBelgium, iGermany kanye neNorway, iCanada, i-Africa Union, iNingizimu Afrika kanye ne-ELMA Foundation. UCaroline Delany, uMqondisi Jikelele weGlobal Affairs Canada waseSouthern and Eastern Afrika, uthe: "iCanada iqinisekisa ukuqgubeka nokweseka kwe-mRNA Technology Transfer Programme futhi siyajabula ukumemezela ukuqhubeka kokunikelwa kwezigidi ezingu-15 ngamadola aseCanada emhlanganweni namhlanje. Lokhu kuletha iminikelo eyisamba yeCanada kuHlelo lwezigidi ezingu-45 kumadola aseCanada.
translations.reference: In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.
translations.hypothesis: In a collaborative effort, Afrigen will continue to provide training and technical expertise to its partners. Donors play a crucial role in supporting the program, having already contributed a total of $117 million, with France being the first to support the mRNA technology transfer initiative. Those present at the meeting represented organizations from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, and Norway, as well as Canada, the African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, Director General for Global Affairs at Southern and Eastern Africa, Canada, stated: "Canada is committed to the advancement and support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme, and we are pleased to announce a further contribution of $15 million Canadian dollars at today's meeting. This brings Canada's total contribution to the program to $45 million Canadian dollars."

translations.source: Lokhu kwesekwa koxhasomali ekusebenzeni kweHabhu eNingizimu Afrika kanye nababambisani bohlelokuxhumana emhlabeni wonke. Siyakhulwa kakhulu ekubalulekeni kokwakha ukukwazi ezingeni lendawo nelesifunda". UMnu Martin Seychell, uSekela Mqondisi omkhulu we-European Commission esho. "I-EC isivele ixhase ngezigidi ezingu-€40 ukusungula ihabhu yokudlulisela yezobuchwepheshe kweMRNA futhi isanda kusayina esinye isibonelelo nge-EIB kwezigidi ezingu-€15.5 ukusiza ukwandisa amandla okukhiqizwa kwemigomo. Lokhu yingxenye yotshalomali lonke ngaphansi kwesu le EU Global Gateway, lapho ngaphezu kwesigidigidi esisodwa sama-euro selihlanganisiwe ngaphansi komzamo weTeam Europe ekukhiqizweni kwemikhiqizo yezempilo. Manje sekubalulekile ukuphinde sibhekane nokulawula okungenzeka nokukhona okufunakalyo, ukuqinisekisa ukuthiakukhona ukukhiqiza kuphela kwemigomo esezingeni eliphezulu kuzona zonke izifunda emhlabeni kodwa nokuphindeka kokutholakala okulinganayo kwaleyo migomo."
translations.reference: These funds support the running of the Hub in South Africa as well as network partners around the world. We firmly believe in the importance of building capacity at local and regional level”. Mr Martin Seychell, Deputy Executive Director European Commission, said. “The EC has already contributed €40 million to the establishment of the mRNA technology transfer Hub and has recently signed another grant with the EIB for €15,5 million to facilitate the expansion of vaccines manufacturing capacity. This is part of the overall investment under the EU Global Gateway strategy, where more than €1 Billion have been mobilised under the Team Europe Initiative on Manufacturing health products. It is now particularly important to also address possible regulatory and demand bottlenecks, to ensure not only production of vaccines of the highest standard in all regions of the world, but also rapid and equitable access to those vaccines.”
translations.hypothesis: This support for the Hub in South Africa and its global partners is crucial. We place great importance on building capacity at the regional and local levels," said Mr. Martin Seychell, Deputy Director-General of the European Commission. "The EC has already provided €40 million to establish an mRNA technology transfer hub, and has recently signed another grant agreement with the EIB for €15.5 million to help increase manufacturing capacity. This is part of a broader investment under the EU Global Gateway, where over €1 billion has been mobilized under the Team Europe approach for the production of health products. It is now essential to also address potential supply chain disruptions and ensure that high-quality vaccines are produced in all regions of the world, and that these vaccines are equally accessible."

translations.source: Ukuqokwa kobuholi oubalulekile okwenziwelwe ukuhambisa indlela yesu nemizamo yeWHO Inhlangano yeZempilo yoMhlaba (WHO) sebeqoke izikhulu eziphezulu iminyaka emihlanu ukuba ngabaholi eqembini labaholi ekomkhulu eGeneva. Ukuqokwa okusha kulandela ukuphinde kuqokwe uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus kuhlandla lesibili leminyaka emihlanu njengoMqondisi Jikelele. Ubuholi bonkebeqembu buhlanganiswe ukuhambisana nokuhamba phambili kweNhlangano kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo nokusebenzisana noMqondisi Jikelele ukuhambisa okuya kukhona okuhamba phambili ne-ajenda yentshisekelo yoguquko lweWHO. Ukuqhuba inqubekela phambili yokwenza kweWHO 13th General Programme of WORK (GPW13) ukuthola okuqondiwe "okungezigidigidi ezintathu" nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSuatainable Development Goals, umsebenzi weWHO kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo egxile kokuhamba phambili okuhlanu:
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities:
translations.hypothesis: The appointment of key leadership is crucial for guiding the organization and its efforts. The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed senior officials to leadership positions at its headquarters in Geneva for the past five years. This latest round of appointments follows the re-election of Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus for a second five-year term as Director-General. The entire leadership team will work together to drive the organization forward over the next five years and to collaborate with the Director-General to implement the WHO's strategic priorities and agenda for change. To advance the WHO's 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), which focuses on achieving the "triple billion" targets and contributing to the Sustainable Development Goals, the WHO's work over the next five years will focus on five key priorities:

translations.source: ukwenza ukulandelana ngendlela okuya ekugqugquzeleni ezempilo nokuvikela isifo ngokubhekana nomsuka ozidalayo; ushintsho olwenzekayo kuzinhlelo zezempilo okuya ekunakekelweni kwezempilo zokuqala; ukuqinisa izinhlelo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula; amahhanisi amandla esayensi,ukwenza kabusha,idatha nezobuchwepheshe ukwenzangcono ezempilo; kanye nokuqinisa iWHO njengabanegunya lokuhola kwezempilo zomhlaba nokuthola umthelela emazweni.
translations.reference: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries.
translations.hypothesis: This involves: improving health outcomes and disease prevention by addressing the root causes; transforming health systems to prioritize primary healthcare; strengthening emergency preparedness and response systems; leveraging the power of science, innovation, data, and technology to improve health; and strengthening the WHO's role as a leader in global health and its impact on countries.

translations.source: Qembu labaholi ekomkhulu, linenamba elinganayo kubantu besifazane nabesilisa, bazosebenza ngokuhlanganyela nesifunda seWHO namahhovisi emazweni. Amalungu amasha eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu:uDkt Jeremy Farrar ozoba nguSosayensi omkhulu weWHO kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Ukuqokwa kukaDkt Farrar kwamenyezelwa ngoZibandlela 2022. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Farrar uzobheka umkhakha wezesayensi, ukuhlanganisa ndawonye ngoti abaphambili nohlelokuxhumana kwezesayensi nokwenza kabusha emhlabeni wonke ukuyalela, ukudevelopha nokuhambisa ezempilo ezisezingeni eliphezulu inqubomgomo nemisebenzi kubantu abayidinga kakhulu. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO uDkt Farrar ubenguMqondisi weWellcome Trust.
translations.reference: The headquarters leadership team, comprising equal numbers of women and men, will work jointly with WHO regional and country offices. New members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Jeremy Farrar will become WHO’s Chief Scientist as of 8 May 2023. The appointment of Dr Farrar was previously announced in December 2022. In this role Dr Farrar will oversee the Science Division, bringing together the best experts and networks in science and innovation from around the world to guide, develop and deliver high quality health policies and services to the people who need them most. Prior to joining WHO Dr Farrar was Director of the Wellcome Trust.
translations.hypothesis: The newly formed leadership team, with an equal number of men and women, will work closely with the WHO regional and country offices. The new members of the leadership team include: Dr. Jeremy Farrar, who will become the Chief Scientist of the WHO starting on August 8, 2023. Dr. Farrar's appointment was announced in September 2022. In this role, Dr. Farrar will oversee the science sector, bringing together leading experts and fostering scientific collaboration worldwide to guide, develop, and deliver high-quality health policies and programs to those who need them most. Prior to joining the WHO, Dr. Farrar was the Director of the Wellcome Trust.

translations.source: Ngaphambi kokujoyina Wellcome ngo-2013 uDkt Farrar uchithe iminyaka engu-17 njengoMqondisi weClinical Reaserch Unit esiBhadlela seTropical Diseases eVietnam lapho ukucwaninga kwakhe kwakugxile kwezempilo emhlabeni ngokugxila ekuveleni kwezifo ezithelelanayo. UDkt Farrar ungumcwaningi wezokwelapha neziqu zobudokotela kanye nePHD. UDkt Ailan Li uzoba nguMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weUniversal Health Coverage,Healthier Populations kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Li izobheka imizamo yeNhlangano ukugqugquzela ukubangcono kwezempilo nokuphila ngokungenelela okuhlobene nezendawo ezenhlalakahle, ezempilo zokubhekene nezomnotho okufaka ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukulawula ugwayi, ukuphepha kwamakhemikhali, ukuphepha komgwaqo, izinhlelo zokudla nokunomsoco, ukusebenza siqu, ukungcoliswa komoya nokwemisebe yelana ngendlela ye-One Health. UDkt Li ubelokhu esebenza njengomele iWHO eKingdom of Cambodia kusukela ngomhlaka-15 kuNtulikazi 2019.
translations.reference: Before joining Wellcome in 2013 Dr Farrar spent 17 years as Director of the Clinical Research Unit at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases in Vietnam where his research interests were in global health with a focus on emerging infectious diseases. Dr Farrar is a clinician researcher with a medical degree and a PhD. Dr Ailan Li will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations as of 8 May 2023. In this role Dr Li will oversee the Organization’s efforts to promote better health and well-being through interventions relating to the environmental, social, and economic determinants of health, including climate change, tobacco control, chemical safety, road safety, food systems and nutrition, physical activity, air pollution and radiation, through a One Health approach. Dr Li has been serving as the WHO Representative to the Kingdom of Cambodia since 15 July 2019.
translations.hypothesis: Prior to joining Wellcome in 2013, Dr. Farrar spent 17 years as the Director of the Clinical Research Unit in the Tropical Diseases Institute in Vietnam, where his research focused on global health with a particular emphasis on the emergence of infectious diseases. Dr. Farrar holds a medical degree, a doctorate, and a PhD. Dr. Ailan Li will be appointed as Deputy Director-General for Universal Health Coverage and Healthier Populations, effective March 8, 2023. In this role, Dr. Li will oversee the organization's efforts to promote health and well-being through interventions related to social determinants of health, health systems, and economic factors, including climate change, non-communicable diseases, chemical safety, road safety, food and nutrition, physical activity, air quality, and zoonotic diseases, using a "One Health" approach. Dr. Li has been working as a consultant for the World Health Organization (WHO) in the Kingdom of Cambodia since July 15, 2019.

translations.source: Ngaphambi kwalesisikhundla, uDkt Li wasebenza njenge Regional Emergency Director weWHO Health Emergencies Programme eHhovisi leSifunda leWHO eWestern Pacific. UDkt Li uneziqu zobudokotela neziqu kwezesayensi yenhlalakahle yezempilo. UDkt Yukiko Nakatani uzoba nguMszi woMqondisi Jikelele wokungena kwezemithi nokukhiqizwa nemikhiqizo yezempilo. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Nakatani uzobheka ukuthuthuka nokwenza kwemigomo yeWHO nezinqubomgomo ukuqinisekisa ukutola ukulingana kumithi efanele, imigomo kanye nokuxilongwa kusona sonke isibalo sabantu yonke indawo okufaka ukuvikela nokuphendula ku-ephidemikhi. UDKt Nakatani ususebenze njengoMqondisi woMdlavuza nedivishini yokulawula izifo eJapanese uNgqongqoshe weZmpilo,ezokusebenza nenhlalakahle njengoTechnica Advisory kuMinister of State wezokusebenza zokugoma iCOVID-19 eJapan.
translations.reference: Prior to this role, Dr Li served as Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme in the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr Li holds a medical degree and degree in health social sciences. Dr Yukiko Nakatani will become Assistant Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health Products as of 2 May 2023. In this role Dr Nakatani will oversee the development and implementation of WHO’s norms and policies to ensure equitable access to quality medicines, vaccines and diagnostics for all populations everywhere, including for preventing and responding to epidemics. Dr Nakatani has been serving as the Director of the Cancer and Disease Control Division of the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare and as the Technical Advisor to the Minister of State for the COVID-19 Vaccination Task Force in Japan.
translations.hypothesis: Prior to this appointment, Dr. Li served as the Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme at the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr. Li holds medical degrees and a degree in public health sciences. Dr. Yukiko Nakatani will serve as the Director-General's Special Envoy for Access to Medicines and Health Products. In this role, Dr. Nakatani will oversee the development and implementation of WHO strategies and policies to ensure equitable access to essential medicines, vaccines, and health products for all people, everywhere, including preparedness and response to epidemics. Dr. Nakatani previously served as the Director of Cancer and Infectious Disease Control at the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare, and also served as a Technical Advisor to the Minister of State for COVID-19 Response in Japan.

translations.source: UDkt Nakatani uneziqu zobudokotela nePhD kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Razia Pendse uzoba nguChef deCabinet kusukela ngomhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Pendse uzohola iHhovisi loMqondisi Jikelele, esiza ukuhambisa iNhlangano kokuhamba phambili nemizamo, futhi uzoqinisekisa ukuhambisana neqembu lobuholi beWHO kuwona womathathu amazinga eWHO. UDkt Pendse ubesebenza njengoMqondisi weHealthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases kuHhovisi leSifunda leWHO eSouth-East Asia futhi wayemele iWHO eSri Lanka. UDkt Pendse uneziqu zobudokotela neziqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi.
translations.reference: Dr Nakatani holds a medical degree and a PhD in public health. Dr Razia Pendse will become the Chef de Cabinet as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Pendse will head the Director-General’s Office, helping to drive the Organization’s priorities and initiatives, and will ensure alignment within the WHO leadership team and across the three levels of WHO. Dr Pendse has been serving as the Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases in the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and was previously the WHO Representative to Sri Lanka. Dr Pendse holds a medical degree and master's degree in public health.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Nakatani holds medical degrees and a PhD in public health. Dr. Razia Pendse will assume the position of Chef de Cabinet starting November 4, 2023. In this role, Dr. Pendse will lead the Director-General's Office, supporting the organization in advancing its key initiatives, and will ensure alignment with the WHO's executive leadership at all three levels. Dr. Pendse previously served as Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases at the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and represented the WHO in Sri Lanka. Dr. Pendse holds medical degrees and a master's degree in public health.

translations.source: UDkt Jerome Salomon uzoba Ngumsizi waMqondisi Jikelele wezokukhavwa kweZempilo eMhlabeni, izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nokungaxhunyanwa ngazo kusukela ngomhlaka-17 Mbasa 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Salamon uzobheka iphothifoliyo enkulu yezinhlelo zezobuchwepheshe ezibheka iHIV, igciwane lehephathithisi, ukuthelelana kwezifo zocansi, isifo sofuba, umalalaeveva, izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe, ezempilo zengqondo, isifo sokusebenzisa izidakamizwa izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo njengezifo zenhliziyo, ushukena, izifo zondendende zokuphefumua kanye nomdlavuza. UDkt Salomon ubesebenza njengoMqondisi Jikelele weZempilo kubungqongqoshe beZempilo nokuvikela eFrance. Wayeyilungu leBhodi labaphathi beWHO ngaphambi kokuqokwa kwakhe futhi nesipiliyoni sakhe esikhulu ekuphathweni kwezinhlelo zempilo,izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nezempilo yomphakathi kumazwe angaphandle. UDkt Salomon uneziqu zobudokotela,izqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi kanye nePHD ku-ephidemiloji.
translations.reference: Dr Jérôme Salomon will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Communicable and Noncommunicable Diseases as of 17 April 2023. In this role Dr Salomon will oversee a broad portfolio of technical programmes covering HIV, viral hepatitis, sexually-transmitted infections, tuberculosis, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, mental health, substance use disorders, and noncommunicable diseases such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases and cancer. Dr Salomon has been serving as the Director-General for Health at the Ministry of Health and Prevention of France. He was a Member of the WHO Executive Board prior to his appointment and has extensive experience in health systems management, communicable diseases and international public health. Dr Salomon holds a medical degree, a master’s degree in public health and a PhD in epidemiology.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Jerome Salomon will serve as the Director-General's Special Advisor on Global Health, communicable and non-communicable diseases, starting on May 17, 2023. In this role, Dr. Salomon will oversee a broad portfolio of technical programs addressing HIV, tuberculosis, sexually transmitted infections, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, mental health, and substance use disorders, as well as non-communicable diseases such as cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, respiratory diseases, and cancer. Dr. Salomon previously served as the Director-General of Health at the French Ministry of Health and Prevention. He was a member of the WHO's Executive Board prior to his appointment and has extensive experience in managing health programs, communicable diseases, and public health initiatives internationally. Dr. Salomon holds medical degrees, a Master's degree in public health, and a PhD in epidemiology.

translations.source: Lamaphothifolo amalungu akhona eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu: uDkt Samira Asma uzoqhubeka njengoMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weMininingo, ukuhlaziya nokulethwa kwemithelela. UDkt Asma ubheke iphothifoliyo eqinisekisa ukuthi idatha yezempilo ikhona futhi iyatholakala, futhi isetshenziswa ukwenzangcono imiphumela yezempilo emhlabeni wonke, okufaka ukulandelela nokuqhuba ukuqhubeka okubheke kokuqondiwe okungamabhiliyoni amathathu futhi okuhlobene nesempilo yeSustainable Development Goals. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO ngo-2018, uDkt Asma ubambe izikhundla zobuholi e-U.S. Centers for Disease Contrel and Prevention (CDC) iminyaka engaphezu kwamashumi amabili. Uneziqu zobudokotela kwezokuhlinza amazinyo kanye neziqu kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Bruce Alyward useqokelwe ukuba nguMsizi aMqondisi Jikelele weze-Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division kusuka ngomhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023.
translations.reference: The portfolios of existing members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Samira Asma will continue as the Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. Dr Asma oversees a portfolio which ensures that health data are reliable and accessible, and are used to improve health outcomes worldwide, including tracking and accelerating progress towards the triple billion targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining WHO in 2018, Dr Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She holds a doctorate degree in dental surgery and a master's degree in public health. Dr Bruce Aylward has been appointed Assistant Director-General of the Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division as of 4 May 2023.
translations.hypothesis: The following appointments have been made within the leadership team at the organization: Dr. Samira Asma will continue as Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division. Dr. Asma oversees a portfolio that ensures health data is available and utilized to improve health outcomes globally, including tracking and driving progress towards the three billion target and health-related Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining the organization in 2018, Dr. Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She holds degrees in dentistry and public health. Dr. Bruce Alyward has been appointed as Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division, effective April 4, 2023.

translations.source: UDkt Aylward ubehola iTransformation Agenda yeWHO kanye nomsebenzi wenhlangano kuma-ejensi amaningi okuthola amathuluzi eCOVID-19 okuqhubeka kuhabhu (ACT-A). Kundima yakhe, uDkt Aylward uzohambisa ajenda yeNhlangano yokuguqula ezokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala njengemaphakathi lezokukhavwa kwezempilo emhlabeni kanye nokubheka umsebenzi weWHO wokusebenza kwezinhlelo zempilo, ukugoma, ukuzala, abakhulelwe kanye nezempilo yezingane. UDkt Alyward uneziqu zobudokotela kanye nemastazi yezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Hanan Balykhy uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMszi waMqondisi Jikelele weAntimicrobial Resistance. UDkt Balkhy uzonikezela ngobuchwepheshe nobuholi bezepolitiki ukubheka ezempilo nomthwalo wezomnotho ekungavumini kwesidakamizwa,okufaka ngokubambisana kwemikhakha eminingi neNhlangano yeZolimo noKudla Inhlangano yoMhlaba yezeMpilo yeZilwane kanye neUnited Nations Environment Programme. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO, uDkt Balkhy ubenguMqondisi omkhulu weWokuvikela ukuthelelana nokulawula eSaudi Arabia Ministry of Nationa Guard.
translations.reference: Dr Aylward has been leading WHO’s Transformation Agenda and the Organization’s work on the multi-agency Access to COVID-19 Tools Accelerator (ACT-A) Hub. In his new role, Dr Aylward will drive the Organization’s agenda to transform primary health care as central to universal health coverage, as well as overseeing WHO’s work on health systems, immunization and reproductive, maternal and child health.
